dataPoints.interpretation: Share of material topic GRI disclosures meeting all required content elements for 'in accordance' status; typical first-year reporters achieve 55â€“65%.

dataPoints.name: Materiality Coverage (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of material topics identified through the double materiality assessment that are addressed by a GRI topic-specific standard.

dataPoints.name: GRI-ISSB Mapped Disclosures
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of GRI 2 and topic-specific disclosures that also satisfy an ISSB S1/S2 or ESRS requirement, enabling dual-use reporting.

dataPoints.name: Quantitative Metric Coverage (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of GRI disclosures with quantitative metrics (vs qualitative-only); higher quantitative coverage supports comparability.

userInteraction: Upload the sustainability report or disclosure inventory and map each disclosure to the applicable GRI Standard and disclosure number.
Run the completeness checker to identify disclosures missing mandatory content elements.
Review the materiality gap analysis to confirm all material topics have a corresponding GRI standard disclosure.
Use the cross-framework mapper to identify GRI disclosures that simultaneously satisfy ISSB, ESRS, or TCFD requirements.
references: GRI-ISSB Collaboration MOU and Mapping Table (2022)

acronyms.def: International independent organisation providing the most widely used sustainability reporting standards globally.
GRI requirement to explain how an organisation manages each material topic, addressing policies, actions, and effectiveness.
Composite metric measuring the proportion of mandatory GRI disclosure content elements that have been reported.

dataLineage.flow: Map each disclosure to GRI Standard and disclosure number, check content elements
dataLineage.output: GRI completeness scorecard

dataLineage.flow: Cross-reference material topics against available GRI topic standards
dataLineage.output: Materiality coverage gap analysis

dataLineage.flow: Map GRI disclosures to equivalent cross-framework requirements
dataLineage.output: Dual-use disclosure efficiency report

/gri-alignment.valueSummary: Reduces GRI compliance effort by automating disclosure completeness checking, guides reporters from 'with reference' to 'in accordance' status, and maximises dual-reporting efficiency by identifying GRI disclosures that simultaneously satisfy ISSB, ESRS, and TCFD requirements.

/health-adaptation-finance.title: Health Adaptation Finance
/health-adaptation-finance.description: Analyses climate adaptation investments in health infrastructure and systems, quantifying co-benefits, adaptation returns on investment, and alignment with WHO climate-health nexus frameworks. Covers heat stress resilience, vector disease surveillance, climate-resilient hospitals, and health system capacity building in climate-vulnerable regions.

calculationEngine.formula: AROI = (Lives_saved × VSL + DALY_averted × VSL_DALY - CapEx_adaptation) / CapEx_adaptation
standards: Lancet Countdown on Health and Climate Change
calculationEngine.brief: Computes the economic return on adaptation investment in health infrastructure by monetising lives saved and disability-adjusted life years averted using WHO-recommended value of statistical life (VSL) estimates adjusted for purchasing power parity. A positive AROI indicates adaptation investment exceeds its cost; WHO evidence suggests health adaptation yields 3â€“7× returns in vulnerable regions.

dataPoints.name: Climate-Related DALY Burden (per 100k)
dataPoints.interpretation: Disability-adjusted life years lost per 100,000 population attributable to climate-sensitive diseases (malaria, dengue, diarrhoea, heat stress); highest in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

dataPoints.name: Health Adaptation Finance Gap ($ bn/yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual funding shortfall for climate-resilient health systems in LMICs relative to assessed needs; current adaptation finance to health is under $5bn/yr.

dataPoints.name: Climate-Resilient Hospital Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Composite index assessing hospital structural resilience, backup power, water security, and surge capacity for climate-related health emergencies.

dataPoints.name: Heat Mortality Adaptation Benefit
dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated reduction in heat-attributable mortality from urban heat island mitigation and cooling centre deployment in high-heat-risk cities.

userInteraction: Select the geographic focus (country or region) and health system investment category (hospital infrastructure, surveillance, cooling, vector control).
Input adaptation investment parameters and compute the AROI using WHO VSL and regional DALY estimates.
Review the climate health vulnerability map to prioritise intervention areas by disease burden and adaptation deficit.
Generate the adaptation co-benefits report quantifying lives saved, DALY reduction, and economic productivity gains.
references: WHO â€” Climate Change and Health (2021)
Lancet Countdown on Health and Climate Change (Annual Report 2023)
Global Commission on Adaptation â€” Health Adaptation (2019)
Gasparrini et al. (2023) â€” Heat Mortality and Adaptation

acronyms.def: Summary measure of population health representing years of healthy life lost due to illness, disability, or premature death.
Economic estimate of society's willingness to pay for marginal reductions in mortality risk, used to monetise lives saved.
World Bank classification for countries with GNI per capita below defined thresholds, most climate-vulnerable health systems.

dataLineage.flow: Regionalise by climate hazard type (heat, floods, vector disease)
dataLineage.output: Climate health vulnerability scores by geography

dataLineage.flow: Apply AROI formula with WHO VSL and regional DALY costs
dataLineage.output: Adaptation ROI by investment type

dataLineage.flow: Map hazard intensification trajectories to health burden projections
dataLineage.output: Future health burden under climate scenarios

/health-adaptation-finance.valueSummary: Supports climate finance institutions and development banks in prioritising health system adaptation investments, demonstrating economic co-benefits to governments, and meeting emerging climate-health disclosure requirements under WHO-UNFCCC health adaptation frameworks.

/heat-mortality-risk.title: Heat Mortality Risk
/heat-mortality-risk.description: Models excess heat mortality using exposure-response functions calibrated to city-level temperature and demographic data, providing quantitative heat risk assessments for life insurers, reinsurers, and public health investors. Incorporates urban heat island effect, adaptation capacity, and future climate scenario projections under SSP scenarios.

calculationEngine.formula: EHMR = Σ_t max(0, T_t - MMT) × β_heat × Population × BaselineMortality
standards: Gasparrini et al. (2017) â€” Mortality Risk Attributable to High and Low Temperatures
IPCC AR6 Chapter 7 â€” Health Impacts
calculationEngine.brief: Applies the Gasparrini et al. distributed lag non-linear model framework to estimate daily excess mortality as a function of temperature exposure above the minimum mortality temperature (MMT). The cumulative lagged effect over 0â€“21 days is summed to annual excess mortality, scaled by city population and baseline all-cause mortality rate.

dataPoints.name: Excess Heat Mortality (per 100k/yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual excess deaths per 100,000 population attributable to heat above MMT; Mediterranean cities average 25â€“35; Nordic cities average 5â€“10.

dataPoints.name: Minimum Mortality Temperature (°C)
dataPoints.interpretation: Temperature at which all-cause mortality is minimised; varies by local acclimatisation; London MMT ~18°C, Athens MMT ~25°C.

dataPoints.name: Urban Heat Island Offset (°C)
dataPoints.interpretation: Temperature premium of urban core vs rural surroundings; increases excess heat mortality exposure by 15â€“35% for urban populations.

dataPoints.name: SSP3-7.0 Heat Mortality Uplift (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Projected increase in excess heat mortality by 2080 under SSP3-7.0 high-emission scenario relative to current baseline, controlling for adaptation.

userInteraction: Select the city or region and load historical temperature and mortality data to calibrate the exposure-response function.
Apply urban heat island adjustment factors and demographic vulnerability weights (elderly population share).
Project excess heat mortality under SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, and SSP3-7.0 climate scenarios to 2050 and 2080.
Use the insurer longevity impact tab to quantify the excess mortality loading on life insurance and annuity books.
references: Gasparrini et al. (2017) â€” Global, Regional, and National Burden of Mortality Attributable to Non-Optimal Temperature
Lancet Countdown Indicator 1.1.2 â€” Heat-Related Mortality (2023)
Vicedo-Cabrera et al. (2021) â€” The Burden of Heat-Related Mortality Attributable to Recent Human-Induced Climate Change
IPCC AR6 WG2 Chapter 7 â€” Health, Wellbeing and the Changing Structure of Communities

acronyms.def: City-specific temperature at which all-cause daily mortality reaches its minimum, used as the reference threshold for heat and cold mortality attribution.
Statistical framework modelling non-linear and lagged effects of temperature on mortality used in Gasparrini et al.
Phenomenon where urban areas experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas due to heat-absorbing built surfaces.

dataLineage.source: ERA5 daily temperature data by city
dataLineage.flow: Compute daily excess above MMT, apply 21-day distributed lag
dataLineage.output: Daily excess heat exposure

dataLineage.flow: Calibrate exposure-response function, extract β_heat coefficient
dataLineage.output: City-specific heat mortality response curves

dataLineage.flow: Apply projected temperature change to exposure-response model
dataLineage.output: Future excess heat mortality by scenario and city

/heat-mortality-risk.valueSummary: Enables life insurers and reinsurers to quantify the impact of rising heat mortality on longevity assumptions, supports public health investors in prioritising cooling infrastructure, and provides city-level heat risk intelligence for physical climate risk disclosure under TCFD and TNFD frameworks.

/holdings-deep-dive.title: Holdings Deep Dive
/holdings-deep-dive.description: Provides granular position-level ESG analytics with full look-through across fund of fund structures, enabling issuer-level attribution of ESG score, carbon footprint, controversy exposure, and PAI indicator contributions. Supports portfolio managers in identifying specific holdings driving portfolio-level ESG underperformance.

calculationEngine.formula: ESG_contribution_i = w_i × (ESG_i - ESG_portfolio) / ESG_portfolio × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Decomposes the portfolio ESG score into position-level contributions by computing the weighted deviation of each holding's ESG score from the portfolio average. Positive contributors improve the portfolio score; negative contributors represent holdings whose ESG quality drags on the overall portfolio ESG rating.

dataPoints.name: ESG Score Range (portfolio)
dataPoints.interpretation: Distribution of ESG scores across individual holdings; wide dispersion (>30 points range) indicates high stock-specific ESG risk concentration.

dataPoints.name: Top 5 Carbon Contributors (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of portfolio financed emissions attributable to the 5 largest carbon contributors; Pareto concentration typical in diversified equity portfolios.

dataPoints.name: Controversy Flag Rate (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of holdings with active high-severity ESG controversies; above 15% indicates elevated reputational risk concentration.

dataPoints.name: PAI Indicator Coverage (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of mandatory SFDR PAI indicators computable at holding level without estimation; higher coverage reduces PAI disclosure uncertainty.

userInteraction: Load the full portfolio holdings file including fund look-through positions and select the ESG data provider.
Review the ESG contribution waterfall to identify the top positive and negative ESG score contributors by position.
Drill into individual holdings using the deep-dive card to review pillar-level scores, controversy flags, and PAI data.
Export the position-level ESG attribution report for investment committee or client reporting.
references: MSCI ESG Fund Rating and ESG Score Methodology (2023)

acronyms.def: Fund accounting system holding the official position of record for fund holdings, used as the source for ESG look-through.
Investment management system holding real-time portfolio positions including unsettled trades.
SFDR mandatory indicators measuring negative sustainability effects of investment decisions.

dataLineage.flow: Apply fund look-through, map to ISIN ESG database
dataLineage.output: Position-level ESG scores and PAI data

dataLineage.flow: Compute position ESG contributions vs portfolio average
dataLineage.output: ESG contribution waterfall by holding

dataLineage.flow: Weight financed emissions by portfolio weight and EVIC
dataLineage.output: Position-level carbon footprint contributions

/holdings-deep-dive.valueSummary: Enables portfolio managers and ESG analysts to perform root-cause analysis of portfolio ESG scores at the individual holding level, prioritise engagement or divestment decisions based on ESG contribution and controversy risk, and produce granular SFDR PAI disclosures from position-level data.

/human-rights-risk.title: Human Rights Risk
/human-rights-risk.description: Assesses supply chain human rights risk using UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGP) and EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D) frameworks, identifying salient human rights issues and high-risk supplier relationships. Provides sector-specific risk scoring for child labour, forced labour, gender-based violence, and freedom of association.

calculationEngine.formula: SHRRS = Σ_k P(harm_k) × Severity_k × Breadth_k × Remediability_k
standards: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (2011)
calculationEngine.brief: Prioritises human rights issues by combining the probability of harm occurrence, its severity (scale 1â€“5 aligned to UNGP Principle 14), breadth of impact (number of people at risk), and irremediability. This UNGP-aligned salience methodology identifies which human rights issues require the most urgent attention under the due diligence obligation.

dataPoints.name: Salient Risk Score (top issue)
dataPoints.interpretation: Score for the highest-priority salient human rights issue; scores above 70 require documented action plan under CS3D Article 8.

dataPoints.name: High-Risk Supplier Ratio (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of Tier 1 suppliers in high-risk geographies or sectors for forced labour, child labour, or unsafe working conditions.

dataPoints.name: Remediation Case Resolution Rate (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of reported human rights grievances resolved or in active remediation; UNGP Principle 29 requires effective non-judicial remedy mechanisms.

dataPoints.name: Supply Chain Audit Coverage (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of Tier 1 spend covered by third-party social audits; insufficient coverage risks CS3D non-compliance.

userInteraction: Map the supply chain by commodity, sourcing geography, and production process to identify high-risk supplier relationships.
Run the salience assessment across the identified human rights issues, scoring probability, severity, breadth, and remediability.
Prioritise the top 5 salient human rights issues and define due diligence actions for each.
Generate the CS3D-aligned human rights due diligence report with supplier engagement and remediation tracking.
references: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (2011)
EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D) (2024)

acronyms.full: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
acronyms.def: UN framework establishing corporate responsibility to respect human rights through due diligence and remedy.
EU directive requiring large companies to identify and address human rights and environmental impacts across their value chains.
Ongoing process to identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for how a company addresses its adverse human rights impacts.

dataLineage.flow: Map to geographic and sectoral human rights risk indices (KnowTheChain/Verisk)
dataLineage.output: Supplier human rights risk scores

dataLineage.flow: Extract non-conformances by human rights issue category
dataLineage.output: Audit finding heatmap by supplier

dataLineage.flow: Classify by human rights issue, track resolution status
dataLineage.output: Remediation tracking dashboard

/human-rights-risk.valueSummary: Enables companies and investors to meet CS3D human rights due diligence obligations, identify and prioritise salient human rights risks in supply chains, and demonstrate respect for human rights under the UNGP framework. Supports ESRS S2 (Workers in the Value Chain) and GRI 409/410/411 disclosures.

/impact-hub.title: Impact Investing Hub
/impact-hub.description: Provides an integrated impact measurement framework covering theory of change documentation, impact KPI tracking, additionality assessment, and IMP five dimensions of impact analysis. Supports impact-first and blended finance investors in managing, measuring, and reporting impact across diverse asset classes and SDG themes.

calculationEngine.formula: IWR = Financial_return + Σ_k (ImpactKPI_k × Monetisation_k × Attribution_k)
calculationEngine.brief: Augments financial return with monetised impact value by weighting each impact KPI by its social or environmental monetisation coefficient (e.g. tCO2e averted at social cost of carbon, lives improved at VSL-equivalent) and discounting by attribution to investor contribution versus baseline. Provides a unified return metric combining financial and impact performance.

dataPoints.name: Impact Multiple of Money (IMM)
dataPoints.interpretation: Ratio of total social/environmental value created per €1 invested; private equity impact funds target IMM of 3× or above for market-rate strategies.

dataPoints.name: SDG Alignment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Average number of SDGs materially addressed per portfolio company; concentrated impact strategies average 2â€“3; broad ESG funds average 5+.

dataPoints.name: Additionality Rating
dataPoints.interpretation: Composite rating assessing enterprise additionality (whether the investee would have achieved impact without impact capital) and investor additionality (whether the capital enabled additional impact).

dataPoints.name: Impact Integrity Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Assessment of impact management system quality covering strategy, execution, learning, and accountability dimensions.

userInteraction: Define the theory of change for each investment, mapping activities to outputs, outcomes, and impact for the target beneficiary population.
Select IRIS+ impact metrics aligned to the investment's primary SDG theme and configure the data collection cadence.
Run the additionality assessment using the IMP counterfactual framework to determine whether impact is additional to baseline.
Generate the IMP five dimensions report (what, who, how much, contribution, risk) for investor reporting.
references: Impact Management Project â€” Five Dimensions of Impact (2019)
Harvard Business School â€” Impact Weighted Accounts (2021)
BlueMark â€” Making the Mark Impact Benchmark (2022)

acronyms.def: Global initiative establishing shared fundamentals for impact measurement and management.
GIIN's thematic taxonomy of impact performance metrics used across the impact investing industry.
Impact investing metric dividing total social/environmental value created by total capital invested.

dataLineage.source: Investment portfolio data with SDG classification
dataLineage.flow: Map to IRIS+ metrics, define theory of change per investment
dataLineage.output: Impact framework database

dataLineage.flow: Validate against third-party sources, apply monetisation coefficients
dataLineage.output: Monetised impact value by investment

dataLineage.flow: Assess additionality using IMP enterprise and investor contribution framework
dataLineage.output: Additionality scores by investment

/impact-hub.valueSummary: Provides impact investors and fund managers with a rigorous framework for measuring, managing, and reporting impact across diverse asset classes, enabling credible impact claims, SDG alignment reporting, and blended finance structuring decisions grounded in evidence of additionality.

/impact-verification.title: Impact Verification
/impact-verification.description: Manages the end-to-end impact verification workflow including evidence collection, third-party verifier assignment, verification methodology selection, and audit trail management. Ensures impact claims meet recognised standards (GVerify, GIIRS, BlueMark, B Lab) and provides structured outputs for investor impact reporting and regulatory disclosure.

calculationEngine.formula: VCS = w_1 × EvidenceQuality + w_2 × MethodologyRobustness + w_3 × IndependenceIndex + w_4 × DataAuditability
calculationEngine.brief: Constructs a composite verification confidence score by assessing evidence quality (primary vs proxy data), methodology robustness (RCT > quasi-experimental > survey), verifier independence (arms-length vs affiliated), and data auditability (system-generated vs manually reported). Higher scores indicate more reliable impact claims.

dataPoints.name: Verification Confidence Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores above 0.80 indicate high-confidence impact claims; below 0.60 requires re-verification or methodology upgrade before investor reporting.

dataPoints.name: Evidence Quality Ratio (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of impact KPIs supported by primary data (direct measurement, system records) vs proxy or modelled data.

dataPoints.name: Third-Party Verification Rate (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of impact claims independently verified by a third-party verifier; UNPRI recommends 100% for impact-first strategies.

dataPoints.name: Time to Verification (days)
dataPoints.interpretation: Average elapsed time from evidence submission to verification completion; longer timelines indicate evidence quality issues.

userInteraction: Create a verification request for each impact claim, specifying the IRIS+ metric, evidence package, and target verification standard.
Assign an independent verifier from the approved panel and configure the methodology selected for this claim type.
Upload evidence documents and track their review status through the verification workflow stages.
Receive the verification opinion and confidence score, and integrate findings into the impact report.
references: BlueMark â€” Making the Mark: Assessing the Practice of Impact Investing (2022)
B Lab â€” B Impact Assessment Methodology

acronyms.def: Composite metric assessing the reliability of impact claims based on evidence quality, methodology, and verifier independence.
Gold standard impact evaluation methodology using random assignment to treatment and control groups.
Rating system assessing the social and environmental performance of impact investment funds and companies.

dataLineage.flow: Assess evidence quality tier, flag proxy data for upgrade
dataLineage.output: Evidence quality assessment by KPI

dataLineage.flow: Match verifier expertise to metric type and sector
dataLineage.output: Verifier assignment and independence check

dataLineage.flow: Aggregate VCS scores, track resolution of findings
dataLineage.output: Verification portfolio dashboard

/impact-verification.valueSummary: Ensures impact claims are independently verified to recognised standards, protecting investors from impact washing, enabling credible impact performance reporting, and building the evidence base required for regulatory disclosures under SFDR Article 9 sustainable investment requirements.

/impact-weighted-accounts.title: Impact Weighted Accounts
/impact-weighted-accounts.description: Implements the Harvard Impact Weighted Accounts (IWA) methodology to translate environmental and social impacts into financial equivalents, enabling comparison of impact performance across companies. Covers employment quality adjustments, product impact (including health and environmental harm), and environmental profitability (EP) calculations.

calculationEngine.formula: EP = Financial_EBIT - Environmental_Cost
calculationEngine.brief: Computes environmental profitability by deducting monetised environmental costs (GHG emissions at social cost of carbon, water consumption, land use, air pollutants) from reported EBIT. A positive EP indicates the company creates more financial value than it destroys in environmental terms; negative EP implies hidden environmental losses exceeding reported profit.

dataPoints.name: Environmental Profitability Margin (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: EP as a percentage of revenue; heavily polluting industries (cement, steel, coal) typically show deeply negative EP (-20% to -40%); clean tech can show positive EP above 5%.

dataPoints.name: Employment Quality Wage Premium ($bn)
dataPoints.interpretation: Additional compensation paid to employees above living wage benchmarks across all employment tiers, representing positive employment impact.

dataPoints.name: Product Impact Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Net impact of products on health, environment, and access; tobacco scores deeply negative; vaccine manufacturers score highly positive.

dataPoints.name: Social Cost of Carbon ($/tCO2e)
dataPoints.interpretation: Monetisation coefficient for GHG emissions in environmental profitability calculations; Rennert et al. 2022 updated estimate is $185/tCO2e.

userInteraction: Select the company or portfolio and load financial statements, emissions, water, and employment data.
Apply the IWA environmental cost module using social cost of carbon, water scarcity pricing, and land use valuations.
Compute employment quality adjustments by comparing actual wages to living wage benchmarks by operating country.
Generate the integrated IWA report showing financial, environmental, and social profitability in a unified P&L presentation.
references: Harvard Business School â€” Impact Weighted Accounts Framework (2021)
Rennert et al. (2022) â€” Comprehensive Evidence Implies a Higher Social Cost of Carbon
US Interagency Working Group â€” Social Cost of Carbon (2021)
Serafeim et al. â€” Impact Weighted Financial Accounts (2019)

acronyms.def: Harvard Business School framework translating social and environmental impacts into monetary terms integrated with financial accounts.
Monetised damage cost of emitting one tonne of CO2, reflecting economic harm from climate change impacts.
IWA metric subtracting monetised environmental costs from EBIT to reveal true economic profit after environmental externalities.

dataLineage.flow: Extract EBIT, revenue, wages by geography
dataLineage.output: Financial baseline for IWA

dataLineage.flow: Apply SCC, water scarcity value, land use coefficients
dataLineage.output: Monetised environmental cost by impact category

dataLineage.source: Employment data (wages by tier and country)
dataLineage.flow: Compare to ILO living wage benchmarks by country
dataLineage.output: Employment quality impact in dollars

/impact-weighted-accounts.valueSummary: Enables investors and companies to quantify the true profitability of business activities after accounting for hidden environmental costs and social value creation, supporting investment decisions, impact reporting, and corporate environmental liability analysis beyond what standard financial accounts reveal.

/implied-temp-regression.title: Implied Temperature Regression
/implied-temp-regression.description: Estimates the implied temperature rise embedded in financial market prices through regression of bond spreads, equity risk premia, and commodity forward curves on climate scenario parameters, providing a market-derived cross-check on fundamental portfolio temperature alignment scores.

calculationEngine.formula: MIT = T_baseline + β_spread × ΔCDS + β_equity × ΔERP + β_carbon × P_carbon
standards: Dietz et al. (2021) â€” Market Implied Temperature
calculationEngine.brief: Regresses financial market variables (CDS spread changes, equity risk premium changes, carbon price) on IPCC temperature outcomes across calibrated climate scenarios to invert the relationship and estimate the temperature consistent with current market pricing. Results are cross-validated against NGFS scenario calibrations.

dataPoints.name: Market-Implied Temperature (°C)
dataPoints.interpretation: Temperature consistent with current financial market pricing; persistent range of 2.5â€“3.0°C suggests markets price in insufficient policy ambition relative to Paris targets.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Price Signal (°C per €10/tCO2)
dataPoints.interpretation: Marginal temperature reduction implied by each €10/tCO2 increase in European carbon price; quantifies the market signalling value of carbon pricing.

dataPoints.name: Regression R²
dataPoints.interpretation: Explanatory power of market variables in predicting climate scenario outcomes; higher R² indicates stronger market-scenario alignment.

dataPoints.name: Spread-to-Temperature Conversion (bps/°C)
dataPoints.interpretation: CDS spread widening associated with each additional °C of warming above 2°C, reflecting transition risk pricing in credit markets.

userInteraction: Configure the market variable inputs (CDS indices, equity risk premium, carbon price) and select the temperature scenario calibration dataset.
Run the regression model and review the implied temperature estimate with confidence intervals.
Compare the market-implied temperature against the portfolio's fundamental implied temperature rise (ITR) from company analysis.
Analyse the sensitivity of the implied temperature to individual market variables to identify which markets price climate risk most efficiently.
references: Dietz et al. (2021) â€” Market-Implied Temperature Warming Risk of Listed Equities
ECB â€” Climate-Related Financial Risks: Measurement Methodologies (2020)

acronyms.def: Temperature rise estimated by inverting the relationship between financial market variables and climate scenario outcomes.
Company or portfolio temperature score derived from alignment of GHG reduction targets with sector decarbonisation pathways.
Excess return required by equity investors over the risk-free rate, which may reflect climate transition risk pricing.

dataLineage.flow: Compute spread changes, regress on NGFS scenario temperature outcomes
dataLineage.output: CDS-temperature sensitivity coefficients

dataLineage.flow: Regress ERP changes on warming scenario calibration
dataLineage.output: Equity-implied temperature contribution

dataLineage.flow: Map carbon price to temperature scenario calibration
dataLineage.output: Carbon price implied temperature signal

/implied-temp-regression.valueSummary: Provides a market-derived cross-check on fundamental portfolio temperature alignment, enabling investors to assess whether financial markets are efficiently pricing climate risk and compare market-implied temperatures against science-based Paris Agreement targets.

/infra-esg-dd.title: Infrastructure ESG Due Diligence
/infra-esg-dd.description: Provides pre-investment ESG due diligence framework for infrastructure assets aligned with GIIA Global ESG Reporting and Performance Framework, covering environmental baseline assessment, social impact, stakeholder engagement, and governance of the investee entity. Supports GP/LP reporting and infrastructure-specific ESG risk mitigation planning.

calculationEngine.formula: IERR = w_E × Environmental_score + w_S × Social_score + w_G × Governance_score + w_Transition × Transition_risk
standards: GIIA Global ESG Reporting and Performance Framework
calculationEngine.brief: Constructs an infrastructure-specific ESG risk rating weighted toward environmental and transition risk factors, which dominate value destruction risk in long-duration infrastructure assets. The transition risk sub-score applies stranded asset probability and carbon intensity relative to IEA NZE pathway for energy infrastructure.

dataPoints.name: Infrastructure ESG Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Composite ESG rating for infrastructure assets; energy transition assets (wind, solar, batteries) typically score 70â€“85; fossil fuel infrastructure 30â€“50.

dataPoints.name: Environmental Baseline Completeness (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of required environmental baseline studies completed (biodiversity, noise, air, water, soil); IFC PS6 requires 100% before financial close.

dataPoints.name: Community Benefit Agreement Coverage (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of affected communities with documented community benefit agreements covering employment, access, and compensation.

dataPoints.name: Transition Risk Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Climate transition risk for the asset relative to sector decarbonisation pathway; stranded asset risk rises steeply for fossil fuel infrastructure in 1.5°C scenarios.

userInteraction: Complete the infrastructure ESG screening questionnaire covering asset type, geography, technology, and operating phase.
Run the environmental baseline gap analysis to identify studies required before financial close under IFC Performance Standards.
Assess the social impact and stakeholder engagement plan against GIIA community engagement requirements.
Generate the investment committee ESG risk memorandum with IERR score and recommended ESG action plan.
references: GIIA â€” Global ESG Reporting and Performance Framework (2021)
IFC Performance Standards on Environmental and Social Sustainability (2012)
EDHECinfra â€” Infrastructure ESG Scores Methodology (2023)
GI Hub â€” ESG in Infrastructure Investment (2022)

acronyms.def: Trade association for infrastructure investors, developer of the sector's leading ESG reporting framework.
Eight environmental and social performance standards required for IFC financing and widely adopted in project finance.
Academic research institute providing infrastructure asset ESG ratings and performance benchmarks.

dataLineage.source: Infrastructure asset technical data (capacity, technology, geography)
dataLineage.flow: Score against GIIA ESG criteria by sub-pillar
dataLineage.output: Infrastructure ESG sub-pillar scores

dataLineage.flow: Cross-reference against IFC PS checklist
dataLineage.output: Environmental baseline completeness gap analysis

dataLineage.flow: Assess against GIIA social engagement standards
dataLineage.output: Social license risk rating

/infra-esg-dd.valueSummary: Enables infrastructure investors to identify material ESG risks before financial close, structure ESG covenants in financing agreements, meet GIIA ESG framework reporting obligations, and demonstrate alignment with IFC Performance Standards required for development finance institution co-investment.

/infrastructure-esg.title: Infrastructure ESG
/infrastructure-esg.description: Monitors ESG performance across operating infrastructure portfolios spanning transport, energy, utilities, and digital sectors using GIIA Global ESG Reporting and Performance Framework indicators. Provides asset-level KPI tracking, carbon intensity benchmarking, safety performance monitoring, and regulatory compliance status.

calculationEngine.formula: CI_infra = Total_CO2e / Throughput_metric
calculationEngine.brief: Normalises Scope 1 and 2 emissions by the sector-specific throughput metric (kWh generated for power, vehicle-km for transport, ML treated for water) to produce a comparable carbon intensity indicator. Intensity benchmarking against IEA NZE sector pathways identifies assets requiring accelerated decarbonisation investment.

dataPoints.name: Energy Infrastructure Carbon Intensity (gCO2/kWh)
dataPoints.interpretation: Grid electricity emission factor normalised by generation; renewables score <5 gCO2/kWh; gas peakers score 400â€“500; GIIA IEA NZE target is 30 gCO2/kWh by 2050.

dataPoints.name: Water Loss Rate (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Non-revenue water as a percentage of total water produced; above 20% indicates significant infrastructure efficiency and environmental performance gap.

dataPoints.name: LTIFR (per million hours)
dataPoints.interpretation: Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate; infrastructure sector benchmark is below 1.0 for best-in-class operators; above 3.0 triggers social risk flag.

dataPoints.name: Renewable Energy Share (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of portfolio electricity consumption from renewable sources; GIIA encourages 100% renewable electricity for own operations by 2030.

userInteraction: Load the operating asset register with throughput, energy, water, and safety data for the reporting period.
Review the GIIA ESG KPI dashboard comparing each asset against sector benchmarks and internal targets.
Identify assets with carbon intensity above the IEA NZE pathway and flag for decarbonisation capex review.
Generate the GIIA-format annual ESG performance report for GP/LP reporting.
references: GIIA â€” Global ESG Reporting and Performance Framework (2021)

acronyms.def: Safety metric measuring work-related injuries resulting in time away from work per million hours worked.
Water produced by a utility that does not generate revenue due to leakage, metering errors, or theft.
Trade association whose ESG reporting framework is the standard for infrastructure fund ESG performance disclosure.

dataLineage.flow: Compute sector-specific carbon intensity, water loss rate, renewable share
dataLineage.output: GIIA ESG KPIs by asset

dataLineage.flow: Calculate LTIFR and TRIFR, benchmark against sector peer data
dataLineage.output: Safety performance dashboard

dataLineage.source: IEA NZE pathway data by infrastructure sector
dataLineage.flow: Compare asset carbon intensity to pathway benchmark
dataLineage.output: Decarbonisation gap by asset

/infrastructure-esg.valueSummary: Enables infrastructure fund managers to monitor ESG performance across operating assets, identify environmental and safety underperformers, meet GIIA ESG reporting obligations, and demonstrate portfolio-level progress toward net-zero infrastructure aligned with IEA NZE sector pathways.

/infrastructure-valuation.title: Infrastructure Valuation
/infrastructure-valuation.description: Provides climate-risk adjusted infrastructure asset valuation integrating physical and transition risk factors into discounted cash flow (DCF) models, net asset value (NAV) computation, and internal rate of return (IRR) stress testing. Covers stranded asset probability weighting and insurance cost escalation under climate scenarios.

calculationEngine.formula: NAV_climate = Σ_t (CF_t × (1 - Stranded_prob_t) - ClimateCost_t) / (1 + r_t)^t
standards: IPCC AR6 Physical Risk Cost Estimates
calculationEngine.brief: Adjusts the traditional DCF by discounting each year's cash flows by the cumulative stranded asset probability under the climate scenario, and deducting projected climate-related costs including insurance premium escalation, flood defence capex, and carbon pricing impacts. The risk-free rate r_t is further adjusted for physical risk premium in high-vulnerability geographies.

dataPoints.name: Climate VaR (% NAV)
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum NAV loss at 95% confidence under the NGFS Disorderly transition scenario; coastal and fossil fuel infrastructure bear the highest climate VaR.

dataPoints.name: Stranded Asset Probability (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Probability that the asset becomes economically unviable before end of technical life under a 1.5°C scenario; coal plants average 60â€“80%; wind farms <1%.

dataPoints.name: Insurance Cost Escalation (% pa)
dataPoints.source: Swiss Re sigma / Lloyd's market data
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual rate of physical risk insurance premium escalation for high-risk infrastructure assets; compound effect on 30-year cash flows is material.

dataPoints.name: IRR Sensitivity to Carbon Price (ΔIRR/€10 tCO2)
dataPoints.interpretation: IRR reduction per €10/tCO2 carbon price increase; gas power plants typically show -0.8 to -1.2% IRR sensitivity.

userInteraction: Input the asset cash flow model and select the applicable climate scenario (NGFS Orderly, Disorderly, or Hot House World).
Configure the stranded asset probability curve for the asset type and geography under the selected scenario.
Run the climate-adjusted NAV and compare against the base case to compute the climate value at risk.
Stress-test the IRR against carbon price pathways and insurance cost escalation assumptions.
references: EDHECinfra â€” Climate Risk in Infrastructure Investment (2022)
NGFS â€” Case Studies of Climate-Related Physical Risks and Impacts (2020)
IEA â€” World Energy Outlook Stranded Asset Analysis (2023)
Swiss Re Institute â€” Economics of Climate Change (2021)

acronyms.def: Fair value of an infrastructure asset computed as the present value of future cash flows net of debt obligations.
Discount rate at which the NPV of an infrastructure investment equals zero; primary return metric for infrastructure equity.
Valuation methodology computing asset value as the present value of projected future cash flows.

dataLineage.flow: Apply stranded asset probability and climate cost overlays
dataLineage.output: Climate-adjusted cash flows by year and scenario

dataLineage.source: NGFS economic impact data by scenario
dataLineage.flow: Map GDP and sector impact to infrastructure revenue assumptions
dataLineage.output: Revenue stress factors by scenario

dataLineage.flow: Model insurance premium escalation curves for asset type and geography
dataLineage.output: Climate insurance cost escalation by asset

/infrastructure-valuation.valueSummary: Enables infrastructure investors and lenders to quantify climate risk in asset valuations, compare climate-adjusted IRRs across asset classes, assess stranded asset probability under 1.5°C scenarios, and disclose climate VaR in accordance with TCFD recommendations for asset owners.

/insurance-climate-hub.title: Insurance Climate Hub
/insurance-climate-hub.description: Provides an integrated climate analytics hub for insurance companies spanning underwriting risk (physical hazard exposure), reserving adjustments (climate trend loading), investment portfolio climate alignment, and regulatory stress testing under EIOPA and PRA climate scenario requirements.

calculationEngine.formula: CRclimate = (Claims_base × (1 + ClimateLoading) + Expenses) / Net_Premium
standards: EIOPA Opinion on Sustainability in Solvency II (2021)
Lloyd's of London Climate Risk Appetite Framework
calculationEngine.brief: Adjusts the traditional combined ratio by applying a climate loading factor to the expected loss ratio, reflecting increased claim frequency and severity under climate change scenarios. The loading factor is derived from peril-specific physical risk projections (flood, windstorm, wildfire, drought) calibrated to IPCC AR6 hazard intensity trajectories.

dataPoints.name: Climate Loading Factor (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage uplift applied to base expected losses for climate-sensitive perils; flood loading 15â€“25%; windstorm 5â€“12%; wildfire 20â€“40% in fire-prone regions.

dataPoints.name: Climate-Adjusted Combined Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Combined ratio after applying climate loading; above 100% indicates technical underwriting loss; EIOPA 2022 stress test median was 108%.

dataPoints.name: Physical Risk Underwriting Exposure (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of gross written premium exposed to climate-sensitive perils in high-risk geographies as defined by IPCC RCP8.5 scenarios.

dataPoints.name: Investment Portfolio Climate VaR (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum investment portfolio loss at 95% confidence under NGFS Disorderly transition scenario; relevant for Solvency II investment risk capital.

userInteraction: Configure the underwriting portfolio by peril, geography, and line of business, and select the climate loading scenario.
Review the climate-adjusted combined ratio by peril category and compare against technical pricing assumptions.
Analyse the investment portfolio climate VaR using the NGFS scenario stress test module.
Generate the EIOPA/PRA climate risk disclosure pack including scenario narrative and quantitative impact assessments.
references: EIOPA â€” Opinion on Sustainability in Solvency II (2021)
PRA Supervisory Statement SS3/19 â€” Enhancing Banks' and Insurers' Approaches to Managing Climate Risk (2019)
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 11 â€” Weather and Climate Extreme Events (2021)
Lloyd's â€” Climate Risk Appetite Framework (2022)

acronyms.full: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority
acronyms.def: EU supervisory authority responsible for insurance regulation and Solvency II framework oversight including climate stress testing.
UK regulator supervising insurance companies, which issued SS3/19 on climate financial risk management.
Insurance underwriting performance metric adjusting the traditional combined ratio for projected climate change impacts on claims.

dataLineage.source: Underwriting portfolio data by peril and geography
dataLineage.flow: Apply climate loading factors from IPCC AR6 hazard trajectories
dataLineage.output: Climate-adjusted expected loss ratio by peril

dataLineage.flow: Run NGFS climate scenario stress test on asset values
dataLineage.output: Investment portfolio climate VaR

dataLineage.flow: Integrate climate change module, compare to base case
dataLineage.output: Climate-loaded cat model loss estimates

/insurance-climate-hub.valueSummary: Enables insurance companies to integrate climate risk across underwriting, reserving, and investment functions, meet EIOPA and PRA regulatory climate risk management expectations, and produce quantitative climate scenario analyses for TCFD disclosures and regulatory stress test submissions.

/act-assessment.title: ACT Assessment
/act-assessment.description: Scores corporate low-carbon transition credibility using the ACT (Assessing low-Carbon Transition) methodology across 10 sector-specific indicators. Combines quantitative performance metrics with qualitative policy signals to produce an overall ACT score and track record rating. Benchmarks companies against sector decarbonisation pathways aligned with a 1.5°C scenario.

calculationEngine.formula: ACT_score = Σ(w_i × Indicator_i) / Σ(w_i); Pathway_gap = Actual_intensity – Benchmark_intensity(t)
calculationEngine.brief: Each sector applies a tailored indicator set scored 0–1 and weighted by materiality. Physical and transition benchmark intensities are derived from IEA sector pathways. A negative pathway gap indicates outperformance; positive gap signals misalignment with 1.5°C trajectory.

dataPoints.name: ACT Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher scores denote stronger transition credibility across all 10 indicators

dataPoints.name: Pathway Gap
dataPoints.interpretation: Deviation from IEA 1.5°C sector benchmark in tCO₂e/$M revenue

userInteraction: Select company and sector to load ACT indicator set
Review each indicator score against sector benchmark
Pathway Alignment tab compares intensity trajectory to 1.5°C curve
Scenario overlay shows gap under Current Policies vs NZ2050
Export ACT scorecard as PDF for CDP/investor disclosure

acronyms.def: CDP/ADEME methodology for scoring corporate transition credibility on a 0–1 scale
SBTi framework aligning sector emission pathways to 1.5°C

dataLineage.flow: Map responses to ACT indicators; score 0–1 per indicator
dataLineage.output: ACT composite score and sector pathway gap

dataLineage.flow: Interpolate benchmark intensity by year
dataLineage.output: Pathway gap chart and outperformance flags

/act-assessment.valueSummary: ACT scores translate qualitative climate commitments into a comparable 0–1 metric, enabling fund managers to distinguish genuine transition leaders from laggards. Pathway gap analysis highlights which companies are decarbonising at a pace consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C, providing direct stewardship and engagement evidence.

/advanced-report-studio.title: Advanced Report Studio
/advanced-report-studio.description: Drag-and-drop sustainability report builder supporting multi-framework simultaneous export across GRI, TCFD, CSRD ESRS, ISSB S1/S2, and SFDR. Provides a pre-built template library, module-level content blocks, and version-controlled disclosure management. Enables compliance officers to assemble investor-grade reports without manual data re-entry.

calculationEngine.formula: Coverage_score = Disclosed_disclosures / Required_disclosures × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Each content block is tagged to one or more framework disclosure requirements. A cross-walk matrix maps platform data fields to framework items, auto-populating narrative sections and tables. Coverage score tracks completeness per framework and flags mandatory items outstanding.

dataPoints.name: Frameworks Supported
dataPoints.interpretation: GRI, TCFD, CSRD ESRS, ISSB S1/S2, SFDR PAI, UK TPT

dataPoints.name: Coverage Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of mandatory disclosure items populated for selected framework

userInteraction: Choose primary reporting framework and target disclosure year
Drag content blocks onto report canvas from the module palette
Auto-populate data fields from connected platform modules
Review coverage score per framework and resolve gaps
Export as XBRL, PDF, or structured JSON for regulator submission
references: SFDR Annex I PAI disclosure templates

acronyms.def: Structured data format required for CSRD and SEC climate disclosure filings
Mandatory reporting standards under CSRD covering all ESG topics

dataLineage.flow: Map data fields to framework disclosure items via cross-walk matrix
dataLineage.output: Populated report sections with coverage gap flags

dataLineage.flow: Parse and attach to relevant disclosure blocks
dataLineage.output: Version-controlled report with audit trail

/advanced-report-studio.valueSummary: The Report Studio eliminates the double-entry burden of multi-framework reporting by maintaining a single source of truth mapped to all major standards. Version control and audit trails satisfy ISAE 3000 assurance requirements and reduce external auditor review time by providing traceable data lineage for every reported figure.

/agri-biodiversity.title: Agricultural Biodiversity
/agri-biodiversity.description: Quantifies ecosystem services, pollinator health, and soil biodiversity metrics for agri-sector portfolios using TNFD LEAP methodology, IPBES ecosystem service valuation, and USDA NRCS soil health indicators. Assesses biodiversity intactness across agri-holdings and links species richness decline to financial exposure. Supports TNFD D4 disclosure and EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 alignment.

calculationEngine.formula: BII = (Σ Species_observed_i / Species_expected_i) / n_sites; Pollinator_value = Crop_yield × Pollination_dependency × P_per_tonne
calculationEngine.brief: Biodiversity Intactness Index (BII) compares observed to expected species richness at each site. Pollinator service value monetises yield losses attributable to bee colony decline. Soil biodiversity scores aggregate microbial biomass, earthworm density, and aggregate stability into a 0–100 index.

dataPoints.name: Biodiversity Intactness Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of natural species richness remaining; <75% is the proposed safe limit

dataPoints.name: Pollinator Value at Risk
dataPoints.interpretation: Economic value of crops at risk if pollinator populations decline further

dataPoints.name: Soil Health Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Aggregate of microbial biomass, earthworm density and aggregate stability

userInteraction: Load agri-portfolio holdings with GPS centroids
LEAP Locate tab maps biodiversity sensitive areas and protected zones
Pollinator Health tab shows colony density trend and yield dependency
Soil Biodiversity tab scores each holding on NRCS composite
Financial Exposure tab monetises ecosystem service decline
TNFD D4 tab generates dependency and impact disclosures

acronyms.def: Ratio of observed to expected species richness used to measure human impact on biodiversity
TNFD four-step process for nature-related risk and opportunity assessment

dataLineage.flow: Intersect with portfolio site boundaries to compute BII per holding
dataLineage.output: Site-level BII scores and aggregate portfolio intactness

dataLineage.flow: Apply pollination dependency coefficients to crop revenue
dataLineage.output: Pollinator value at risk in $ per holding

/agri-biodiversity.valueSummary: Agricultural biodiversity metrics directly link ecosystem health to financial performance: declining BII and pollinator populations translate to quantifiable yield and revenue risk. Soil biodiversity scores serve as leading indicators of long-term land productivity, enabling portfolio managers to engage with agri-borrowers on regenerative practices before impairment materialises.

/agri-finance-hub.title: Agriculture Finance Hub
/agri-finance-hub.description: Sustainable agriculture investment analytics platform covering smallholder blended finance structures, agri-value chain emissions mapping, and climate-adaptive lending criteria. Aggregates IFC/MDB pipeline data and CGIAR productivity benchmarks to score agri-lending portfolios against UN Food Systems ambitions. Supports climate-smart agriculture (CSA) taxonomy alignment.

calculationEngine.formula: BlendedIRR = (Concessional_IRR × w_conc + Commercial_IRR × w_comm); FoodSys_score = Σ(w_j × CSA_pillar_j)
calculationEngine.brief: Concessional tranches from MDB/DFI first-loss capital reduce risk for commercial co-investors, enabling viable IRRs on projects with positive development externalities. CSA scoring applies CGIAR three-pillar framework: productivity, resilience, and mitigation. Food systems alignment checks against 5 UN Food Systems Summit tracks.

dataPoints.name: Blended Finance IRR
dataPoints.interpretation: Blended return achievable when concessional capital absorbs first-loss

dataPoints.name: CSA Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Climate-smart agriculture score across productivity, resilience and mitigation pillars

dataPoints.name: Smallholder Reach
dataPoints.interpretation: Development impact metric tracking smallholder beneficiaries per unit of capital deployed

userInteraction: Screen agri-investments against CSA taxonomy criteria
Blended Finance tab models first-loss tranche sizing and IRR waterfall
Value Chain Emissions maps Scope 3 Category 1 agri-emissions by commodity
Smallholder Impact tab shows farmer reach and productivity uplift metrics
Portfolio Climate Risk overlays CMIP6 yield projections onto loan book
MDB Pipeline tab integrates IFC/ADB/EBRD agri-facility data
references: UN Food Systems Summit 2021 Action Tracks

acronyms.def: CGIAR framework increasing productivity, resilience and mitigation in food systems
Specialised institution using public capital to crowd-in private finance for development impact

dataLineage.flow: Overlay CSA taxonomy criteria and compute blended IRR scenarios
dataLineage.output: Scored agri-investment pipeline with CSA alignment tags

dataLineage.flow: Apply CMIP6 climate scenarios to commodity yield models
dataLineage.output: Per-holding climate-adjusted revenue and food security impact estimates

/agri-finance-hub.valueSummary: The Agriculture Finance Hub quantifies the development-commercial return nexus for agri-lending, helping institutional investors structure viable first-loss positions while maximising food security impact. CSA scoring and value chain emissions mapping ensure that climate-smart credentials are substantiated and comparable across the portfolio.

/ai-engagement.title: AI Engagement Analytics
/ai-engagement.description: AI-powered stakeholder engagement platform that analyses sentiment across investor calls, ESG questionnaire responses, and social media channels using NLP and transformer models. Tracks response rates, sentiment trends, and engagement effectiveness by stakeholder cohort. Provides real-time alerts on emerging ESG controversies and engagement escalation triggers.

calculationEngine.formula: Sentiment_score = softmax(W × h_CLS + b); Engagement_index = Response_rate × avg(Sentiment_score)
calculationEngine.brief: A fine-tuned BERT-family model classifies text into positive/neutral/negative sentiment with confidence scores. Engagement index combines response rate with weighted sentiment to capture quality as well as quantity of engagement. Controversy alerts use named entity recognition to surface company mentions in negative ESG contexts.

dataPoints.name: Engagement Response Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of stakeholders who responded to engagement outreach in the period

dataPoints.name: Avg Sentiment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Portfolio-level average sentiment across all ingested engagement text

dataPoints.name: Controversy Alerts (30d)
dataPoints.interpretation: Number of negative ESG controversy signals detected in the last 30 days

userInteraction: Connect data sources: earnings calls, questionnaire responses, social feeds
Dashboard shows engagement index by stakeholder cohort
Sentiment Trend tab plots weekly sentiment scores per company
Controversy Tracker surfaces alerts with source links and severity
Response Tracking tab shows outstanding engagements and follow-up deadlines
Export engagement log for GRI 2-29 disclosure

acronyms.def: ML branch enabling computers to analyse and interpret human language
NLP technique identifying company, person, and location mentions in text

dataLineage.flow: Tokenise and pass through fine-tuned BERT model for sentiment classification
dataLineage.output: Sentiment scores per stakeholder interaction with controversy flags

dataLineage.flow: NER extracts company mentions; classify context sentiment
dataLineage.output: Real-time controversy alert feed with severity ratings

/ai-engagement.valueSummary: AI-driven engagement analytics shift stakeholder management from reactive to predictive by surfacing sentiment deterioration and controversy signals weeks before they appear in formal complaints or press coverage. Engagement index scores provide auditable evidence for PRI Active Ownership reporting and support escalation decisions in stewardship programs.

/ai-governance.title: AI Governance Hub
/ai-governance.description: Centralised governance dashboard for ESG AI models deployed on the platform, covering model risk assessment, algorithmic bias detection, explainability (SHAP), and auditability against EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF standards. Tracks model drift, retraining schedules, and fairness metrics across protected demographic attributes. Supports ISO 42001 AI management system certification readiness.

calculationEngine.formula: SHAP_i = Σ_{S⊆F\{i}}(|S|!(|F|–|S|−1)!/|F|!) × [f(S∪{i})–f(S)]; FairnessGap = max_group(accuracy) – min_group(accuracy)
calculationEngine.brief: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) decomposes model output into additive contributions per feature, enabling regulators and investors to understand AI decision rationale. Fairness gap measures disparate impact across demographic groups. Model drift is detected via population stability index (PSI) on input feature distributions.

dataPoints.name: Model Explainability Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of model output variance explained by top-10 SHAP features

dataPoints.name: Fairness Gap
dataPoints.interpretation: Accuracy disparity across protected demographic groups; <0.05 is acceptable

dataPoints.name: PSI (Model Drift)
dataPoints.interpretation: PSI >0.2 triggers mandatory model retraining review

userInteraction: Model Registry lists all deployed ESG AI models with risk tier classification
Explainability tab shows SHAP waterfall and beeswarm charts per model
Bias Detection tab computes fairness gap across protected attributes
Drift Monitoring shows PSI trends with retraining alerts
EU AI Act Compliance tab maps each model to high-risk categories
Audit Export generates model card for ISO 42001 evidence pack
references: EU AI Act Annex III High-Risk Systems (2024)

acronyms.def: Game-theory method attributing model output to individual input features
Metric measuring shift in feature distributions between training and production data

dataLineage.flow: Compute SHAP values per prediction; aggregate fairness metrics
dataLineage.output: Explainability reports and bias flags per model

dataLineage.flow: Calculate PSI against training baseline; trigger retraining alerts at PSI>0.2
dataLineage.output: Drift monitoring dashboard with model health status

/ai-governance.valueSummary: Rigorous AI governance is essential as ESG ratings, temperature scores, and controversy detectors increasingly rely on ML models whose decisions affect capital allocation. The hub provides the SHAP-based transparency, fairness metrics, and audit trails required by the EU AI Act and institutional-grade model risk management frameworks.

/air-quality-finance.title: Air Quality Finance
/air-quality-finance.description: Clean air project financing analytics covering health co-benefit monetisation, PM2.5 abatement NPV, and air quality bond pricing. Integrates WHO AQG 2021 targets, WHO cost-of-illness methodology for DALY valuation, and IFC air quality performance standards to assess investment viability of clean air projects alongside carbon reduction co-benefits.

calculationEngine.formula: NPV_coBenefit = Σ_t [DALY_avoided(t) × VSL / (1+r)^t]; PM25_abatement = Emission_reduction × CF_PM25
calculationEngine.brief: DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year) avoidance is monetised using country-specific Value of Statistical Life (VSL) from OECD/WHO. PM2.5 concentration-response functions from WHO 2021 translate emission reductions to health outcomes. Carbon co-benefits use IPCC AR6 social cost of carbon to add to total NPV.

dataPoints.name: VSL (Value of Statistical Life)
dataPoints.interpretation: Economic value of preventing one statistical premature death used for co-benefit NPV

dataPoints.name: PM2.5 Abatement Cost-Effectiveness
dataPoints.interpretation: Cost per unit ambient PM2.5 concentration reduction

dataPoints.name: Health Co-Benefit NPV
dataPoints.interpretation: Net present value of health benefits from PM2.5 and NO2 reduction

userInteraction: Input project location and emission reduction profile
PM2.5 Abatement tab calculates concentration change and DALY avoidance
Health Co-Benefits monetises DALY using country VSL
Carbon Co-Benefits adds GHG abatement value at SCC
Investment Analysis tab computes blended NPV with and without co-benefits
WHO AQG Compliance tab shows gap to interim and final targets
references: IPCC AR6 Social Cost of Carbon

acronyms.def: WHO metric combining years of life lost and years lived with disability into a single burden metric
Aggregate willingness-to-pay to reduce mortality risk by one statistical death per year

dataLineage.flow: Apply concentration-response functions to emission reduction scenarios
dataLineage.output: DALY avoidance estimates per PM2.5 and NO2 reduction increment

dataLineage.flow: Monetise DALYs and discount to NPV; add carbon co-benefit at SCC
dataLineage.output: Total co-benefit NPV and blended project IRR

/air-quality-finance.valueSummary: Incorporating air quality health co-benefits into project finance models can increase NPV by 30–60% for urban clean energy and transport projects, unlocking commercially viable investment cases that would otherwise fail standalone carbon pricing hurdles. Co-benefit transparency also strengthens green bond use-of-proceeds documentation.

/air-quality-health-risk.title: Air Quality Health Risk
/air-quality-health-risk.description: Investor-grade air quality health risk analytics computing PM2.5 and NO2 population exposure, DALY burden calculations, and portfolio company liability exposure from regulatory non-compliance. Integrates satellite-derived air quality data (MODIS, Sentinel-5P), WHO AQG 2021 thresholds, and epidemiological concentration-response functions to quantify stranded cost risk for high-emitting facilities.

calculationEngine.formula: DALY = YLL + YLD; YLL = Deaths × LE_remaining; Exposed_pop = Σ_i(Pop_i × [PM25_i > WHO_threshold])
calculationEngine.brief: Annual average PM2.5 exposure is compared against WHO AQG annual target of 5 μg/m³. Concentration-response functions from GBD 2021 translate excess exposure to attributable deaths and DALYs per 10 μg/m³. Facility-level liability estimated from non-attainment penalties under local regulatory regimes.

dataPoints.name: PM2.5 WHO AQG
dataPoints.interpretation: WHO annual guideline; interim target IT-1 is 35, IT-4 is 10 μg/m³

dataPoints.name: Excess DALY Burden
dataPoints.interpretation: Disease burden attributable to ambient PM2.5 above WHO guideline

dataPoints.name: Regulatory Exceedance
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of portfolio facilities exceeding national ambient air quality standards

userInteraction: Upload or connect facility location data
PM2.5 Exposure tab maps satellite-derived concentrations against portfolio sites
DALY Burden tab calculates population health impact per facility
Regulatory Compliance tab flags exceedances vs NAAQS/EU/WHO thresholds
Liability Estimation tab models penalty exposure from non-attainment
Portfolio Aggregation ranks holdings by air quality risk score

acronyms.def: Fine airborne particles <2.5μm diameter linked to cardiorespiratory disease and premature death
IHME annual study quantifying mortality and morbidity from 371 diseases and injuries globally

dataLineage.flow: Grid-level PM2.5 retrieval; intersect with facility locations
dataLineage.output: Facility-level annual average PM2.5 exposure values

dataLineage.flow: Apply CRF to excess PM2.5; compute attributable DALYs and liability
dataLineage.output: Per-facility DALY burden and regulatory non-compliance penalty exposure

/air-quality-health-risk.valueSummary: Air quality health risk translates into tangible financial liability through tightening regulatory standards and potential litigation costs. As WHO 2021 guidelines are progressively adopted into national law, portfolio companies with facilities in high-pollution areas face growing exceedance penalties, operational restrictions, and reputational risk that investor engagement can help mitigate.

/anomaly-detection.title: Anomaly Detection
/anomaly-detection.description: ML-powered ESG data anomaly detection engine that flags statistical outliers, data entry errors, and implausible trend breaks across platform datasets. Uses isolation forest, z-score, and time-series decomposition models to surface quality alerts, enabling data stewards to investigate and remediate before they propagate into disclosures. Tracks anomaly resolution rates and data quality KPIs.

calculationEngine.formula: z_score = (x – μ) / σ; AnomalyScore = avg(IsoForest_score, z_score_flag); IF_score = 2^(–E[h(x)]/c(n))
calculationEngine.brief: Isolation Forest assigns anomaly scores by measuring average path length to isolate an observation; shorter paths indicate outliers. Z-score flags values beyond 3σ from rolling mean. Time-series STL decomposition identifies structural breaks. Ensemble vote requires both models to agree before raising high-priority alert.

dataPoints.name: Anomaly Detection Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Frequency of anomalous observations per thousand ESG data points ingested

dataPoints.name: False Positive Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of model-flagged anomalies confirmed as legitimate on manual review

dataPoints.name: Resolution SLA
dataPoints.interpretation: Target time for data stewards to investigate and resolve high-priority anomaly alerts

userInteraction: Configure anomaly sensitivity thresholds per data category
Alert Dashboard shows open anomalies ranked by severity and data impact
Drill down into flagged record with z-score and isolation score detail
Assign anomaly to data steward for investigation
Mark resolved with remediation notes for audit trail
Data Quality KPI tab tracks rolling anomaly rate and resolution SLA compliance
references: GHG Protocol Data Quality Management Guidance

acronyms.full: Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess
acronyms.def: Time-series decomposition method separating seasonal, trend, and residual components
Statistical measure of spread used in robust outlier detection as an alternative to standard deviation

dataLineage.flow: Apply z-score and Isolation Forest models; ensemble vote for alert threshold
dataLineage.output: Prioritised anomaly alert queue with scores, source fields, and drill-down links

dataLineage.flow: Train Isolation Forest on clean baseline; update model quarterly
dataLineage.output: Updated anomaly model with recalibrated sensitivity thresholds

/anomaly-detection.valueSummary: Proactive anomaly detection prevents erroneous ESG data from flowing into regulatory disclosures, carbon accounting reports, and investment decisions. By catching implausible emissions spikes or carbon intensity inversions at ingestion, the engine reduces material misstatement risk and supports ISAE 3000 limited assurance engagements.

/anti-corruption.title: Anti-Corruption Analytics
/anti-corruption.description: Bribery and corruption risk scoring for portfolio companies and sovereign counterparties using Transparency International CPI, World Bank CPIA governance indicators, and UNGC Principle 10 alignment. Tracks corruption-related controversies, enforcement actions, and FCPA/UK Bribery Act exposure. Scores companies on anti-bribery management system maturity against ISO 37001.

calculationEngine.formula: CRI = 0.40×(100–CPI_score) + 0.30×Controversy_score + 0.20×Enforcement_score + 0.10×ABMS_maturity
calculationEngine.brief: Corruption Risk Index (CRI) inverts CPI (lower CPI = higher risk). Controversy score aggregates media and regulatory alerts on bribery incidents. Enforcement score captures FCPA/Bribery Act fines normalized by revenue. ABMS maturity scores anti-bribery management systems on 0–100 ISO 37001 readiness scale.

dataPoints.name: Transparency International CPI
dataPoints.interpretation: Perceived corruption score; 0=highly corrupt, 100=very clean. Below 50 = elevated risk

dataPoints.name: FCPA Enforcement Fine
dataPoints.interpretation: Average FCPA settlement for portfolio sector; proxy for enforcement intensity

dataPoints.name: ABMS Maturity Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Anti-bribery management system readiness across 10 ISO 37001 controls

userInteraction: Portfolio Corruption Risk tab ranks holdings by CRI composite score
Country Risk Heatmap shows CPI scores for all operating jurisdictions
Controversy Feed surfaces bribery and enforcement alerts with severity
UNGC Principle 10 tab checks alignment of company anti-corruption policy
ABMS Maturity tab scores ISO 37001 control implementation
Scenario Analysis models portfolio CRI impact of entering new high-risk markets

acronyms.def: TI annual index ranking 180 countries by perceived public-sector corruption on 0–100 scale
US law prohibiting bribery of foreign government officials by US persons and issuers

dataLineage.source: TI CPI and World Bank CPIA data
dataLineage.flow: Normalise and weight into CRI; map to portfolio country exposure
dataLineage.output: Per-company CRI score with country and controversy decomposition

dataLineage.flow: NER extracts company enforcement actions; normalise by revenue
dataLineage.output: Enforcement score component and live controversy alerts

/anti-corruption.valueSummary: Corruption risk is a material ESG concern for institutional investors due to legal liability under FCPA and UK Bribery Act, reputational contagion, and the correlation between high corruption environments and governance failures that precede value destruction. CRI scoring enables systematic portfolio screening and targeted engagement with high-risk holdings.

/api-gateway-monitor.title: API Gateway Monitor
/api-gateway-monitor.description: Real-time monitoring dashboard for platform API health, covering request latency, error rates, rate limiting status, and upstream ESG data provider availability. Tracks SLA compliance per endpoint, surfaces degraded providers, and provides historical performance analytics for capacity planning. Integrates with PagerDuty-compatible alerting for P0 outages.

calculationEngine.methodology: SLA compliance and error rate tracking
calculationEngine.formula: Availability = (Uptime_mins / Total_mins) × 100; Error_rate = Error_requests / Total_requests × 100; p95_latency = 95th percentile(response_times)
calculationEngine.brief: API health scoring combines availability, error rate, and p95 latency against SLA thresholds. Rate limit utilisation tracks requests against provider quotas to prevent throttling. Circuit breaker pattern implementation is monitored to ensure degraded upstream providers do not cascade failures.

dataPoints.name: API Availability SLA
dataPoints.interpretation: Target monthly uptime for all critical ESG data API endpoints

dataPoints.name: p95 Latency
dataPoints.interpretation: Latency threshold below which 95% of API requests should complete

dataPoints.name: Rate Limit Utilisation
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of provider API quota consumed in current window

userInteraction: Dashboard shows real-time traffic for all API endpoints
Latency Distribution tab plots p50/p95/p99 response time histograms
Error Analysis tab breaks down 4xx/5xx errors by endpoint and provider
Rate Limiting tab tracks quota consumption with auto-throttle status
SLA Report tab generates monthly availability and latency compliance report
Alert Configuration maps P0/P1/P2 thresholds to notification channels
references: RFC 7807 Problem Details for HTTP APIs

acronyms.def: Contractual commitment on system availability, latency, and error rate thresholds
Software tooling for capturing, aggregating, and alerting on application runtime performance metrics

dataLineage.flow: Aggregate request counts, latency, and status codes per endpoint per minute
dataLineage.output: Real-time health dashboard and SLA compliance reports

dataLineage.flow: Poll availability and quota status; trigger circuit breaker on consecutive failures
dataLineage.output: Provider availability status and rate-limit headroom indicators

/api-gateway-monitor.valueSummary: API gateway monitoring prevents silent data quality degradation where upstream provider outages or quota exhaustion cause stale or missing ESG data to flow into reports without detection. Proactive SLA tracking and circuit breaker visibility ensure data freshness SLAs are met across all downstream disclosure modules.

/api-orchestration.title: API Orchestration
/api-orchestration.description: ESG data pipeline orchestration layer managing multi-provider API workflows, data transformation, normalisation, and routing to downstream platform modules. Supports DAG-based workflow definition, retry logic, data lineage capture, and provider failover. Enables operations teams to configure data feeds from Bloomberg, MSCI, Sustainalytics, and proprietary sources without code changes.

calculationEngine.formula: Pipeline_latency = Σ(node_processing_time_i); Data_freshness = now() – last_successful_run; Coverage = Populated_fields / Total_fields × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Each pipeline is modelled as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of transformation nodes. Lineage metadata is captured at each node to enable full audit trails. Provider failover uses priority-ordered fallback chains: primary provider failure triggers secondary source substitution with data quality flag.

dataPoints.name: Pipeline Success Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of scheduled pipeline runs completing without error

dataPoints.name: Data Freshness
dataPoints.interpretation: Time elapsed since each data feed was last successfully refreshed

dataPoints.name: Field Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of required data fields populated across all portfolio companies

userInteraction: Pipeline Library lists all configured ESG data DAGs with run history
DAG Visualiser shows node-by-node execution status and latency
Data Lineage tab traces any platform data point back to its source API call
Failover Config tab manages priority-ordered provider chains per data type
Coverage Report shows field-level completeness by company and provider
Alert Rules configure failure notifications per pipeline
references: DAMA DMBOK v2 Data Lineage Chapter

acronyms.def: Workflow structure where nodes represent tasks and directed edges encode execution dependencies without cycles
Standard data integration pattern for ingesting, transforming, and storing data from source systems

dataLineage.flow: Extract ESG fields; transform to platform schema; capture lineage metadata per field
dataLineage.output: Normalised ESG records in platform database with full source lineage

dataLineage.flow: Execute DAGs on cron schedule; apply retry logic on failure; trigger failover chain
dataLineage.output: Pipeline run logs, freshness metrics, and coverage reports

/api-orchestration.valueSummary: Robust API orchestration is the invisible backbone of ESG data quality: without lineage capture and failover logic, data gaps from provider outages silently degrade disclosure accuracy. The orchestration layer ensures that every data point in the platform can be traced to its source, transformation, and ingestion timestamp.

/approval-workflows.title: Approval Workflows
/approval-workflows.description: Multi-level ESG disclosure review and approval routing engine supporting configurable sign-off chains, delegated authority matrices, and SLA-tracked review deadlines. Maintains immutable audit trails of all review actions for ISAE 3000 and SOX compliance. Integrates with Advanced Report Studio to gate report publication until all approvals are obtained.

calculationEngine.methodology: Delegated authority matrix with SLA tracking
calculationEngine.formula: SLA_breach = (Review_completed – Review_assigned) > threshold_hours; Approval_completion = Approved_items / Total_items × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Workflow engine enforces the delegated authority matrix: each disclosure type requires sign-off from defined roles in sequence. Parallel approval paths resolve via configurable majority rule. SLA tracking flags overdue reviews and auto-escalates to the next authority tier after the breach window.

dataPoints.name: Approval Cycle Time
dataPoints.interpretation: Average time from disclosure submission to final approval sign-off

dataPoints.name: SLA Breach Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of review tasks exceeding their SLA deadline

dataPoints.name: Pending Approvals
dataPoints.interpretation: Number of disclosure items awaiting at least one required sign-off

userInteraction: Submit disclosure item for review via Report Studio integration
Workflow engine routes to first-tier reviewer per authority matrix
Reviewer approves, rejects, or requests changes with mandatory comment
Escalation logic triggers after SLA breach to next authority tier
Final approver publishes signed-off disclosure with timestamp
Audit Trail tab provides immutable record of all review actions for assurance
