Portfolio Sensitivity computes portfolio P&L impact per €10 carbon price move

acronyms.def: EU ETS compliance permit entitling holder to emit one tonne of CO₂e; traded on ICE and Nasdaq
California cap-and-trade compliance permit; linked to Quebec market since 2014 in Western Climate Initiative

dataLineage.flow: Extract settlement prices per tenor; fit forward curve; compute implied carry and convenience yield
dataLineage.output: Full carbon forward curves for EUA, CCA, ACCU, NZU with term structure analytics

dataLineage.source: NGFS Phase 5 carbon price scenario data
dataLineage.flow: Extrapolate liquid forward curve to 2050 using NGFS scenario prices for planning horizons
dataLineage.output: Extended carbon forward curve to 2050 under 5 NGFS scenarios for long-dated hedging

/carbon-forward-curve.valueSummary: Carbon forward curves are essential inputs for corporate hedging strategies, long-dated infrastructure project financing, and climate-adjusted asset valuation. Contango structures in EUA markets reflect regulatory trajectory certainty, while implied volatility surfaces inform option-based hedge structuring for airlines, utilities, and industrial emitters managing CORSIA and EU ETS compliance costs.

/carbon-reduction-projects.title: Carbon Reduction Projects
/carbon-reduction-projects.description: Internal abatement project portfolio management covering NPV, payback period, marginal abatement cost, and GHG reduction tracking per project. Applies GHG Protocol Project Protocol for baseline and additionality assessment. Prioritises projects on a MAC curve and tracks cumulative emission reduction progress against SBTi targets.

calculationEngine.methodology: MAC curve + GHG Protocol project NPV
calculationEngine.formula: MAC = NPV_cost / GHG_reduction_tCO2e; Project_NPV = –CapEx + Σ_t[Savings_t – OpEx_t] / (1+r)^t; Additionality = (Baseline_emissions – Project_emissions)
calculationEngine.brief: MAC (Marginal Abatement Cost) ranks projects from cheapest to most expensive GHG reduction, forming the abatement cost curve. NPV includes avoided energy costs, carbon cost savings, and capital expenditure. Projects with negative MAC (cost-saving) should be implemented first. Additionality = emission reduction below baseline.

dataPoints.name: Project MAC
dataPoints.interpretation: Marginal abatement cost; negative = cost-saving (energy efficiency); positive = investment required

dataPoints.name: Portfolio GHG Reduction (2030)
dataPoints.interpretation: Total annual emission reduction from all approved internal abatement projects by 2030

dataPoints.name: SBTi Target Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Percentage of SBTi emission reduction gap covered by internal projects vs residual offset need

userInteraction: Project Library lists all internal abatement projects with status and MAC
MAC Curve tab visualises projects ranked by $/tCO₂e with cumulative reduction
Project NPV tab shows discounted cashflow for individual project with sensitivity
SBTi Progress tab tracks cumulative reductions vs annual SBTi glide path
Pipeline tab manages projects in approval queue with governance sign-off
Report Export generates abatement project portfolio for TCFD/CSRD disclosure

acronyms.def: Cost per tonne of CO₂e avoided for a specific abatement measure; negative MAC = net cost-saving project
Upfront investment cost for abatement project equipment, installation, and commissioning

dataLineage.source: Engineering project proposals with cost and emission reduction estimates
dataLineage.flow: Calculate MAC, NPV, and payback; rank on MAC curve; assess additionality vs baseline
dataLineage.output: Prioritised MAC curve with project NPV profiles and cumulative reduction potential

dataLineage.flow: Compare aggregate project reductions against annual SBTi glide path
dataLineage.output: SBTi gap analysis and residual offset requirement by year

/carbon-reduction-projects.valueSummary: An internal abatement project portfolio with rigorous MAC curve analysis enables companies to maximise GHG reduction per dollar invested while minimising reliance on external offsets. SBTi target coverage tracking ensures the organisation remains on track to achieve science-based commitments through genuine operational decarbonisation rather than offset dependency.

/carbon-wallet.title: Carbon Wallet
/carbon-wallet.description: Enterprise carbon credit portfolio management platform covering retirement workflows, chain-of-custody tracking, registry balance reconciliation, and net-zero claim substantiation. Manages voluntary credits (Verra VCU, Gold Standard GS-VER), compliance units (EUAs, CCAs), and CORSIA-eligible units in a unified credit ledger with ISAE 3000-auditable retirement records.

calculationEngine.formula: Net_balance = Purchased – Retired – Transferred; Retirement_certificate = SHA256(credit_serial || entity || date || tCO2e_retired)
calculationEngine.brief: Each credit purchase, transfer, and retirement is recorded as a double-entry ledger transaction. Retirement is irreversible: retired credits are cancelled in the source registry and a cryptographic certificate is generated. Chain-of-custody metadata links each credit to its original project, vintage, methodology, and verification body.

dataPoints.name: Live Credit Balance
dataPoints.interpretation: Current unretired carbon credit balance across all registry accounts

dataPoints.name: Retired Credits (YTD)
dataPoints.interpretation: Volume of credits retired year-to-date for net-zero claim substantiation

dataPoints.name: Average Credit Quality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Quality score reflecting additionality, permanence, and MRV robustness of portfolio credits

userInteraction: Credit Inventory tab shows full portfolio by registry, project type, and vintage
Purchase Workflow tab records new credit acquisitions with registry serial verification
Retirement Wizard initiates retirement with beneficiary, claim type, and vintage selection
Retirement Certificates tab generates and stores ISAE 3000-auditable retirement records
Registry Reconciliation verifies ledger balance against live Verra/GS registry
Quality Analytics tab scores portfolio on ICVCM Core Carbon Principles
references: Verra VCS Program Registry Rules v4
Gold Standard for the Global Goals Registry
SBTi Net-Zero Standard Carbon Removal and Neutralisation

acronyms.def: Verra VCS-verified carbon credit representing one tonne of CO₂e reduced or removed

acronyms.full: Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market
acronyms.def: Independent governance body issuing Core Carbon Principles to establish quality thresholds for voluntary carbon credits

dataLineage.flow: Ingest credit serial numbers and metadata; verify chain-of-custody; reconcile against ledger
dataLineage.output: Registry-verified credit inventory with balance reconciliation and gap flags

dataLineage.flow: Execute retirement in source registry; generate SHA-256 certificate; update net balance
dataLineage.output: Immutable retirement certificates with full chain-of-custody for net-zero claim audit

/carbon-wallet.valueSummary: A disciplined enterprise carbon wallet is essential for substantiating net-zero claims under SBTi Net-Zero Standard and voluntary frameworks. ICVCM Core Carbon Principles filtering ensures the portfolio avoids low-quality offsets that attract greenwashing scrutiny, while registry reconciliation and ISAE 3000-grade retirement certificates provide the audit trail required for external verification.

/cat-bond-ils.title: CAT Bond & ILS Analytics
/cat-bond-ils.description: Catastrophe bond pricing, ILS (Insurance-Linked Securities) portfolio analytics, and parametric trigger design covering hurricane, earthquake, flood, and wildfire perils. Models expected loss, attachment/exhaustion probabilities, and spread-to-risk ratios. Integrates RMS/AIR loss exceedance probability curves and tracks secondary market price movements.

calculationEngine.formula: Expected_loss = Σ_i P(Loss_i) × Loss_i; Spread = Risk_spread + Margin; Risk_spread ≈ EL × (1+loading_factor)
calculationEngine.brief: CAT bond pricing is driven by the expected loss (EL) derived from loss exceedance probability (LEP) curves output by catastrophe models (RMS, AIR). Attachment probability = P(loss > attachment); exhaustion probability = P(loss > exhaustion). Spread = risk spread (proportional to EL) + credit spread + market premium.

dataPoints.name: Expected Loss (EL)
dataPoints.interpretation: Probability-weighted average annual loss as percentage of notional; drives CAT bond pricing

dataPoints.name: Attachment Probability
dataPoints.interpretation: Probability that losses breach the lower trigger threshold, activating partial payout

dataPoints.name: Spread Multiple (multiple-of-EL)
dataPoints.interpretation: Market-implied risk premium above expected loss; reflects investor risk appetite and supply/demand

userInteraction: Market Overview shows live CAT bond issuance pipeline with peril and spread
Pricing Analytics tab models attachment/exhaustion probabilities from LEP curve
Portfolio Aggregation tracks ILS holdings with expected loss and duration
Parametric Triggers tab designs index-based or modelled loss triggers
Climate Stress tab applies IPCC SSP3-7.0 frequency uplift to loss distributions
Secondary Market tab tracks price history and bid-ask spreads per bond
references: RMS/AIR Catastrophe Model Loss Exceedance Probability Documentation
Artemis ILS Market Data and Benchmarks

acronyms.def: Financial instruments transferring insurance risk to capital markets; includes CAT bonds, sidecars, and collateralised reinsurance
Probability curve from catastrophe models showing likelihood of exceeding different loss amounts in a given year

dataLineage.flow: Compute EL from LEP; calibrate attachment/exhaustion probabilities; price risk spread
dataLineage.output: Expected loss, spread, and probability metrics per CAT bond tranche

dataLineage.source: Artemis ILS market data (secondary prices, new issuance)
dataLineage.flow: Track spread multiple vs EL; monitor secondary price movements
dataLineage.output: ILS portfolio mark-to-market and spread-to-risk ratio analytics

/cat-bond-ils.valueSummary: CAT bonds and ILS provide institutional investors with non-correlated yield in low-rate environments while transferring catastrophic weather risk from insurers to capital markets. Climate change is increasing hurricane intensification and wildfire frequency, requiring investors to apply climate-adjusted loss distributions rather than historical average to correctly price expected loss and attachment probability.

/cbam-compliance.title: CBAM Compliance
/cbam-compliance.description: EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism compliance analytics covering embedded carbon calculation for 6 regulated sectors (steel, cement, aluminium, fertilizers, hydrogen, electricity), CBAM certificate procurement strategy, and declarant liability modelling. Tracks third-country carbon prices paid for corresponding CBAM deduction and supports transition period (2023–2025) and full CBAM (2026+) reporting.

calculationEngine.formula: CBAM_liability = Σ_i(ImportTonnes_i × EmbeddedCarbon_i × max(0, EU_ETS_price – Origin_price_i)); EmbeddedCarbon = Direct_emissions + Indirect_emissions_per_tonne
calculationEngine.brief: CBAM certificates cover the gap between EU ETS price and carbon price already paid in country of origin. Embedded carbon includes direct process emissions plus indirect (electricity) emissions per tonne of product using EU methodology or default values. Transition period requires quarterly declarant reports; full CBAM requires annual certificate surrender.

dataPoints.name: CBAM Annual Liability
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual CBAM certificate cost for EU-importing portfolio companies in 6 regulated sectors

dataPoints.name: Embedded Carbon (steel)
dataPoints.interpretation: Default embedded carbon per tonne of steel imports; actual values from verified operator declaration

dataPoints.name: CBAM Certificate Price
dataPoints.interpretation: Certificate price set by weekly average EU ETS auction price; matches ETS exposure

userInteraction: Importer Portfolio tab lists EU-importing entities with sector and volume data
Embedded Carbon tab calculates direct and indirect emissions per imported tonne
Liability Calculator models annual CBAM cost under ETS price scenarios
Origin Price Deduction tracks third-country carbon prices paid for CBAM offset
Certificate Procurement Strategy optimises buying schedule and cost hedge
Regulatory Calendar shows transition period and full CBAM compliance deadlines
references: CBAM Delegated Regulations on calculation methods (2024)

acronyms.def: EU regulation imposing a carbon cost on imports of carbon-intensive goods from non-EU countries without equivalent carbon pricing
EU cap-and-trade compliance carbon market; CBAM certificates are priced at weekly ETS auction average

dataLineage.source: EU customs import data (CN codes, volumes, country of origin)
dataLineage.flow: Map to CBAM sectors; compute embedded carbon using verified or default factors; apply ETS-origin price gap
dataLineage.output: Per-importer CBAM liability schedule with sector breakdown and certificate requirement

dataLineage.source: EU ETS weekly auction price data
dataLineage.flow: Compute CBAM certificate price; model liability under price scenarios
dataLineage.output: CBAM certificate procurement cost and price scenario sensitivity analysis

/cbam-compliance.valueSummary: CBAM is the first major border carbon adjustment to enter legal force, creating direct financial liability for EU importers of steel, cement, aluminium, fertilizers, hydrogen, and electricity. By 2030, as EU ETS prices are projected to reach €120–180/tCO₂, CBAM liability for high-volume industrial importers could reach nine-figure annual costs, making proactive compliance analytics essential for procurement and treasury functions.

/data-quality-monitor.title: Data Quality Monitor
/data-quality-monitor.description: Continuous monitoring of ESG data quality across completeness, accuracy, consistency, and timeliness dimensions. Automated scoring surfaces data gaps and anomalies before they propagate into disclosures or analytics. Threshold-based alerting notifies data stewards of quality degradation in real time.

calculationEngine.formula: DQS = w₁×Completeness + w₂×Accuracy + w₃×Consistency + w₄×Timeliness
calculationEngine.brief: Each dimension is scored 0–1 using rule-based checks; weights default to 0.30/0.30/0.25/0.15 but are configurable per dataset. The composite score rolls up to entity, portfolio, and platform levels with drill-down to individual fields.

dataPoints.name: Platform DQ Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Overall weighted composite; scores below 0.75 trigger amber alert

dataPoints.name: Completeness Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of mandatory fields populated across all active data feeds

dataPoints.name: Accuracy Violations
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of values failing validation rules in the current reporting period

dataPoints.name: Stale Records (>30d)
dataPoints.interpretation: Records whose last-updated timestamp exceeds the staleness threshold

userInteraction: Set staleness and completeness thresholds per dataset in Settings > DQ Rules
Review the dimension heat-map to identify which providers drive the lowest accuracy scores
Drill into flagged fields using the anomaly table and dispatch remediation tasks to data owners
Export the DQ scorecard as PDF for audit committee or regulator submission
references: PCAF (2022) Data Quality Guidance for Financed Emissions
ISO 8000-8: Data Quality â€” Concepts and Measuring

acronyms.def: Weighted composite of completeness, accuracy, consistency, and timeliness sub-scores
Missingness pattern where absence is unrelated to observed or unobserved values
Multi-dimensional assessment of fitness-for-purpose of a dataset

dataLineage.flow: Field-level rule evaluation against type, range, and cross-field logic
dataLineage.output: Per-field and per-record DQ scores

dataLineage.flow: Completeness audit against mandatory disclosure field list
dataLineage.output: Completeness rate and gap inventory

dataLineage.flow: Comparison of last-updated vs. staleness threshold
dataLineage.output: Stale record count and ageing histogram

/data-quality-monitor.valueSummary: Enables data stewards and risk officers to maintain disclosure-grade ESG data quality across all platform inputs. Identifies root-cause providers and fields driving quality degradation, supporting audit readiness and regulatory defensibility.

/data-reconciliation.title: Data Reconciliation
/data-reconciliation.description: Cross-source ESG data reconciliation that identifies conflicts between providers, applies configurable resolution rules, and produces a single golden record for each entity and metric. Reconciliation lineage is preserved for full audit traceability. Supports automated and manual override workflows.

calculationEngine.formula: CRS = Σ |vᵢ − v̅| / v̅ × wᵢ
calculationEngine.brief: For each disputed field the engine computes provider-weighted deviation from the provisional mean; the provider with the lowest deviation and highest reliability weight wins unless a manual override is applied. Lineage records which provider sourced each final value.

dataPoints.name: Open Conflicts
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of entity-metric pairs with material disagreement between two or more providers

dataPoints.name: Auto-Resolved Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of conflicts resolved automatically by priority and reliability rules

dataPoints.name: Golden Record Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of entities with at least one fully reconciled golden record

dataPoints.name: Avg Provider Deviation
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean absolute percentage difference across conflicting provider pairs

userInteraction: Connect data providers in Data Source Manager and map fields to the canonical schema
Run the reconciliation job or schedule it daily post-ingestion
Review auto-resolution decisions in the conflict queue and apply manual overrides where needed
Publish golden records downstream to analytics modules and lock the version for disclosure
references: PCAF (2022) Data Quality and Source Hierarchy Guidance
EFRAG (2023) ESRS 2 General Disclosures â€” BP-2 Disclosures in relation to specific circumstances
GRI Standards 2021 â€” GRI 2-4 Restatements of information

acronyms.def: Single authoritative version of an entity-metric value produced after conflict resolution
Weighted deviation metric used to rank provider reliability during reconciliation
Discipline of creating and maintaining a single consistent view of key business entities

dataLineage.source: Multiple ESG data providers (Bloomberg, MSCI, Refinitiv, proprietary)
dataLineage.flow: Field-level conflict detection using threshold-based deviation analysis
dataLineage.output: Conflict inventory with provider attribution

dataLineage.source: Provider reliability weights (configured in Data Source Manager)
dataLineage.flow: Weighted priority resolution algorithm
dataLineage.output: Golden record with source lineage per field

dataLineage.flow: Reviewer sign-off and justification capture
dataLineage.output: Immutable lineage log for regulatory audit

/data-reconciliation.valueSummary: Produces a single, auditable golden record per entity and metric, eliminating disclosure errors caused by provider disagreement. Enables compliance teams to demonstrate data lineage and conflict resolution rationale to regulators and auditors.

/data-source-manager.title: Data Source Manager
/data-source-manager.description: Centralised registry for all ESG data providers, covering licensing terms, API credentials, rate limits, and data refresh schedules. Administrators configure provider priority rankings that feed the reconciliation engine. Real-time connectivity status and usage telemetry are displayed per source.

calculationEngine.formula: PRI = (1 − ErrorRate) × AvailabilityPct × FreshnessScore
calculationEngine.brief: The reliability index is computed daily from API error rates, uptime, and data freshness relative to the provider's stated refresh cadence. PRI scores drive default priority weights in the reconciliation engine.

dataPoints.name: Active Providers
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of data sources with valid credentials and at least one successful pull in the last 7 days

dataPoints.name: API Error Rate (7d)
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of API calls returning non-200 responses across all providers

dataPoints.name: Avg Freshness Lag
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean difference between provider stated refresh cadence and actual delivery time

dataPoints.name: License Expiry <30d
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of provider licenses expiring within 30 days requiring renewal action

userInteraction: Add a new provider by supplying API endpoint, credentials, and field mapping template
Set refresh schedule and priority rank; ranks feed the reconciliation conflict-resolution engine
Monitor connectivity status and error-rate trends on the provider health dashboard
Renew licenses and rotate API keys before expiry to avoid data feed interruptions
references: PCAF (2022) Data Source Hierarchy and Quality Tiers
ISO/IEC 25012:2008 Software Engineering â€” Data Quality Model
ESMA (2023) Guidelines on ESG Rating Data Providers

acronyms.def: Composite daily score of API availability, error rate, and data freshness for each provider
Contractual commitment by provider on data availability, latency, and quality
Programmatic interface used to pull data from external ESG providers

dataLineage.source: Provider API endpoints and SFTP feeds
dataLineage.flow: Credential validation, rate-limit tracking, and field mapping to canonical schema
dataLineage.output: Connectivity status and per-provider field coverage report

dataLineage.flow: Expiry date monitoring with configurable alert lead time
dataLineage.output: License renewal queue with contract metadata

dataLineage.flow: Error rate and latency aggregation by provider and endpoint
dataLineage.output: Provider Reliability Index time series

/data-source-manager.valueSummary: Gives data operations teams a single control plane for all ESG data sources, ensuring credentials, licenses, and schedules are actively managed. Reliable provider configuration underpins the accuracy and completeness of every downstream analytics and disclosure module.

/data-validation.title: Data Validation Engine
/data-validation.description: Rule-based validation of ESG data submissions that flags type errors, range breaches, cross-field inconsistencies, and regulatory format violations. Validation rules are versioned and mapped to framework requirements, enabling traceable error remediation. Batch and real-time validation modes are supported.

calculationEngine.formula: VPR = (Records − Errors) / Records × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Every submitted record is evaluated against a library of atomic rules (type, range, regex, cross-field, referential integrity); failures are classified as blocking errors or warnings. The pass rate and error inventory are surfaced per submission batch and data domain.

dataPoints.name: Validation Pass Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of records passing all assigned validation rules without blocking errors

dataPoints.name: Blocking Errors
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of records with at least one error that must be resolved before acceptance

dataPoints.name: Warning Flags
dataPoints.interpretation: Non-blocking anomalies surfaced for data owner review without halting ingestion

dataPoints.name: Rule Library Size
dataPoints.interpretation: Total active validation rules across all frameworks and data domains

userInteraction: Import a data submission file or connect a real-time feed and assign it to a rule set
Review the error report grouped by rule category and data domain
Dispatch failed records to data owners with embedded remediation guidance
Re-run validation after corrections and certify the clean batch for downstream use
references: ESRS 2 (2023) BP-1 General basis for preparation of sustainability statements
GRI Standards 2021 â€” GRI 2-5 External assurance
XBRL International (2023) ESG Taxonomy Validation Rules
SEC (2024) Climate Disclosure Rule — Data Tagging Requirements

acronyms.def: Percentage of submitted records that pass all assigned validation rules
Machine-readable tagging standard used in structured regulatory ESG filings
Multi-dimensional fitness-for-purpose assessment applied to submitted ESG data

dataLineage.source: ESG data submissions (file upload, API, SFTP)
dataLineage.flow: Atomic rule evaluation: type checks, range guards, regex patterns, cross-field logic
dataLineage.output: Error and warning inventory with field-level attribution

dataLineage.flow: Rule-to-framework mapping and version control
dataLineage.output: Compliance gap report per framework

dataLineage.flow: Error dispatch to data owners with rule explanation and fix guidance
dataLineage.output: Remediation audit trail with timestamps

/data-validation.valueSummary: Prevents erroneous ESG data from reaching disclosures and analytics by catching errors at the point of ingestion. Versioned rule libraries ensure validation standards stay aligned with evolving regulatory frameworks, supporting audit-ready data pipelines.

/data-versioning.title: Data Versioning
/data-versioning.description: Point-in-time ESG data snapshots enable full version control for auditable disclosures and retrospective analysis. Each data change is recorded with actor, timestamp, and change reason, supporting regulatory restatement workflows. Snapshot comparison tools highlight what changed between versions.

calculationEngine.formula: VDI = Σ |vᵢₜ − vᵢₜ₋₁| / vᵢₜ₋₁ / N
calculationEngine.brief: The drift index measures the mean absolute percentage change across all tracked metrics between two snapshot versions, flagging material restatements that may require regulatory notification. Each individual change is logged with before/after values and change rationale.

dataPoints.name: Active Snapshots
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of named point-in-time snapshots retained for audit and comparison

dataPoints.name: Avg Fields Changed per Commit
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean number of data fields updated in each versioned commit

dataPoints.name: Material Restatements (YTD)
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of version transitions where a tracked KPI changed by more than 5% versus prior version

dataPoints.name: Oldest Retained Snapshot
dataPoints.interpretation: Age of the oldest snapshot under the configured 3-year audit retention policy

userInteraction: Tag a snapshot before any bulk data update or regulatory submission to create a named restore point
Use the diff viewer to compare any two snapshots field-by-field
Flag material restatements and attach a justification note for the audit record
Export a version history report for disclosure appendix or regulator request
references: GRI Standards 2021 â€” GRI 2-4 Restatements of information
ESRS 2 BP-2 Disclosures in relation to specific circumstances
PCAF (2022) Audit Trail and Data Governance Guidance
ISAE 3000 (Revised) Assurance Engagements Other than Audits

acronyms.def: Mean absolute percentage change across tracked metrics between two snapshot versions
A fixed snapshot of data values captured at a specific timestamp for audit or analysis
Data warehousing pattern for tracking historical changes to dimension attributes over time

dataLineage.flow: Snapshot capture triggered by manual tag, scheduled job, or pre-submission hook
dataLineage.output: Immutable versioned snapshot stored with metadata

dataLineage.flow: Diff computation between consecutive or selected snapshots
dataLineage.output: Field-level change log with before/after values and actor attribution

dataLineage.flow: Threshold comparison against materiality limits
dataLineage.output: Restatement register with regulatory notification flags

/data-versioning.valueSummary: Provides the complete audit trail required by CSRD, GRI, and PCAF for data restatement transparency. Enables compliance teams to demonstrate exactly what data underpinned any historical disclosure and why it may have changed.

/db-explorer.title: Database Explorer
/db-explorer.description: Visual SQL query builder for ad-hoc exploration of the ESG platform database, with schema browsing, query history, and result export. Non-technical analysts can construct queries using a drag-and-drop interface while advanced users have a full SQL editor. Results are displayable as tables or charts.

calculationEngine.formula: QPS = 1 / (ExecutionTime × RowsScanned / IndexHitRate)
calculationEngine.brief: The query performance score penalises slow, full-table-scan queries and rewards indexed lookups. The explorer surfaces execution plans and suggests index creation where scans dominate, helping analysts write efficient queries without DBA intervention.

dataPoints.name: Tables Available
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of tables visible to the current user role in the platform database

dataPoints.name: Saved Queries
dataPoints.interpretation: Total queries saved to the shared library across all users

dataPoints.name: Avg Query Execution Time
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean wall-clock execution time across all queries run in the last 7 days

dataPoints.name: Row Export Limit
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum rows exportable per query to CSV or Excel without DBA approval

userInteraction: Browse the schema tree to locate the table or view relevant to your analysis
Build a query using the visual builder or write SQL directly in the editor tab
Run the query, review results in the data grid, and switch to the chart view for quick visualisation
Save the query to the shared library and schedule it as a recurring report if needed
references: Supabase (2024) Row Level Security and Role Configuration

acronyms.def: Standard language for querying and manipulating relational databases
PostgreSQL feature that restricts which rows a user can see or modify based on policy rules
SQL subset (CREATE, ALTER, DROP) used to define and modify database schema

dataLineage.flow: Schema introspection via information_schema and pg_catalog
dataLineage.output: Table, column, and index metadata for query builder

dataLineage.flow: SQL parsing, execution plan generation, and query execution
dataLineage.output: Result set with metadata and execution statistics

dataLineage.flow: Save, tag, and schedule query jobs
dataLineage.output: Reusable query catalogue with version history

/db-explorer.valueSummary: Democratises data access for analysts and compliance teams by removing dependency on engineering for ad-hoc ESG data extraction. Governed access controls ensure exploration happens within data security boundaries while maintaining full audit logs.

/db-migration-console.title: DB Migration Console
/db-migration-console.description: Alembic database migration management console providing schema version control, migration history, dry-run previews, and rollback capability. Displays the current migration head, pending migrations, and dependency graph. Designed for platform administrators managing schema changes across environments.

calculationEngine.formula: MDS = |AppliedHead − CodebaseHead| × AvgMigrationRisk
calculationEngine.brief: The drift score quantifies how far behind an environment is from the codebase migration head, weighted by the estimated schema risk of each unapplied migration (additive vs. destructive DDL). A score above zero indicates a deployment action is required.

dataPoints.name: Current Head (Codebase)
dataPoints.interpretation: Latest migration revision present in the codebase migration chain

dataPoints.name: Applied Head (Database)
dataPoints.interpretation: Highest migration revision applied to the target database environment

dataPoints.name: Pending Migrations
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of migrations in the codebase chain not yet applied to the target environment

dataPoints.name: Last Migration Applied
dataPoints.interpretation: Timestamp of the most recently applied migration in the target environment

userInteraction: Select target environment (dev / staging / prod) from the environment picker
Review the pending migration list and click "Dry Run" to preview DDL without applying
Apply pending migrations sequentially and monitor the execution log for errors
Use the rollback function to revert to a named revision in a non-production environment
references: Alembic (2024) Official Documentation â€” Autogenerate and Migration Commands
PostgreSQL 15 â€” ALTER TABLE and DDL Locking Behaviour

acronyms.def: SQL commands that define or modify database schema structure
The most recent revision in the Alembic migration chain for a given branch
Quantification of schema lag between codebase head and applied database revision

dataLineage.flow: Script parsing to extract DDL operations and dependency graph
dataLineage.output: Migration inventory with risk classification (additive / destructive)

dataLineage.flow: Comparison of applied revision against codebase head
dataLineage.output: Pending migration list and drift score

dataLineage.flow: Sequential DDL application with transaction wrapping and rollback on error
dataLineage.output: Execution log with per-statement timing and error capture

/db-migration-console.valueSummary: Gives platform administrators authoritative visibility into schema state across all environments, preventing deployment mismatches that cause runtime errors. Dry-run and rollback capabilities reduce the risk of destructive schema changes in production.

/decarbonisation-hub.title: Decarbonisation Hub
/decarbonisation-hub.description: Enterprise-wide decarbonisation programme management platform consolidating emission reduction targets, project portfolios, budget allocation, and progress tracking in a single view. Supports Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) near-term and net-zero target setting. Executive dashboards surface headline progress against committed pathways.

calculationEngine.formula: DR = (Baseline Emissions − Current Emissions) / Baseline Emissions × 100
calculationEngine.brief: The decarbonisation rate measures absolute Scope 1+2 (and optionally Scope 3) emission reductions against a fixed baseline year. Trajectory modelling compares actual rate against the linear SBTi-required 4.2% per year (1.5°C) or sector-specific pathway to confirm alignment.

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Decarbonisation Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Year-on-year absolute Scope 1+2 emission reduction across all corporate entities

dataPoints.name: SBTi-Aligned Projects
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of active decarbonisation projects validated as consistent with SBTi criteria

dataPoints.name: CapEx Committed
dataPoints.interpretation: Total capital expenditure committed to decarbonisation projects in current programme

dataPoints.name: Net-Zero Target Year
dataPoints.interpretation: Committed net-zero year per the corporate climate strategy

userInteraction: Set the baseline year and absolute emission baseline in programme settings
Register decarbonisation projects with technology type, expected abatement, cost, and timeline
Monitor actual emission reductions against the SBTi-required linear pathway on the trajectory chart
Identify off-track business units and allocate additional projects to close the abatement gap
references: GHG Protocol (2015) Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard
TCFD (2021) Guidance on Metrics, Targets, and Transition Plans

acronyms.def: Multi-stakeholder body that validates corporate emission reduction targets against 1.5°C science
State where anthropogenic GHG emissions are balanced by removals from the atmosphere
Projected emission trajectory assuming no additional decarbonisation action

dataLineage.flow: Baseline normalisation and year-on-year reduction calculation
dataLineage.output: Decarbonisation rate and trajectory vs. SBTi pathway

dataLineage.flow: Abatement curve construction across all active projects
dataLineage.output: Aggregate abatement potential vs. required reduction gap

dataLineage.flow: Budget-to-abatement efficiency ratio calculation
dataLineage.output: Cost per tonne CO₂e abated by project and technology type

/decarbonisation-hub.valueSummary: Provides board and sustainability leadership with a single source of truth on decarbonisation progress, pipeline, and budget. Identifies abatement gaps early so management can commission additional projects before target deadlines are missed.

/decarbonisation-roadmap.title: Decarbonisation Roadmap
/decarbonisation-roadmap.description: Science-based decarbonisation pathway builder that maps emission reductions to specific technologies, costs, and delivery timelines through to net zero. Marginal abatement cost curves rank interventions by cost-effectiveness. Scenario comparison allows evaluation of accelerated vs. delayed action paths.

calculationEngine.formula: MAC = ΔCost / ΔEmissions Reduced (€/tCO₂e)
calculationEngine.brief: Each decarbonisation lever is evaluated by dividing the net incremental cost (CapEx + OpEx − savings) by the lifetime emission reduction in tCO₂e. Levers are ranked from lowest to highest MAC to construct an abatement priority stack that maximises emission reduction per unit of capital.

dataPoints.name: Required Annual Reduction
dataPoints.interpretation: Minimum year-on-year absolute Scope 1+2 reduction rate to remain on a 1.5°C-aligned trajectory

dataPoints.name: Lowest-MAC Lever
dataPoints.interpretation: Cheapest abatement lever identified; negative MAC indicates net cost saving

dataPoints.name: Technical Abatement Potential
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum emission reduction achievable using all identified technologies at current maturity

dataPoints.name: Residual Emissions (2040)
dataPoints.interpretation: Residual hard-to-abate emissions requiring carbon removal or offset by target year

userInteraction: Define the baseline emission profile by Scope and sector, and set the net-zero target year
Add technology levers with CapEx, OpEx, savings, and abatement estimates; the MAC curve auto-populates
Sort levers by MAC and drag them into the prioritised implementation timeline
Run scenario comparison to quantify cost and emission differences between accelerated and delayed action paths
references: IEA (2023) Net Zero by 2050 â€” A Roadmap for the Global Energy Sector
SBTi (2023) Corporate Net-Zero Standard v1.2 â€” Annex on Residual Emissions
McKinsey (2023) Global Energy Perspective â€” Marginal Abatement Cost Curves
IPCC AR6 WG3 (2022) Mitigation of Climate Change â€” Chapter 12

acronyms.def: Incremental cost to reduce one additional tonne of CO₂ equivalent using a specific technology or measure
Ranked visualisation of emission reduction levers plotted by MAC and abatement volume
Technology that captures CO₂ at source and stores it permanently in geological formations

/digital-product-passport.title: Digital Product Passport
/digital-product-passport.description: EU Digital Product Passport (DPP) compliance module for product lifecycle sustainability data including materials, repairability, carbon footprint, and end-of-life instructions. Generates machine-readable DPP records conforming to the ESPR framework. Supports QR-code linked consumer disclosure and supply chain data collection.

calculationEngine.formula: PSS = Σ wᵢ × Sᵢ across {Carbon, Materials, Repairability, Recyclability, Chemical Safety}
calculationEngine.brief: Each of five sustainability dimensions is scored 0–100 using lifecycle assessment data and regulatory threshold benchmarks; dimension weights follow ESPR delegated act priorities by product category. The composite score drives the EU energy label class equivalent for digital passports.

dataPoints.name: DPP Records Issued

dataPoints.name: Avg Product Carbon Footprint
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean product carbon footprint across all registered SKUs in the current portfolio

dataPoints.name: Repairability Index (Avg)
dataPoints.interpretation: Average repairability score across registered products; EU threshold is ≥6.0

dataPoints.name: DPP Completeness
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of DPP records with all ESPR-mandatory data fields populated

Complete mandatory ESPR data fields: materials, hazardous substances, repairability, carbon footprint
Generate the DPP record and obtain the unique product identifier (UPI) and QR code

ISO 14067:2018 â€” Carbon footprint of products
ISO 14040/14044:2006 â€” Life cycle assessment principles and requirements

/disclosure-adequacy-analyzer.title: Disclosure Adequacy Analyzer
/disclosure-adequacy-analyzer.description: Automated gap analysis of sustainability disclosures against framework requirements including CSRD/ESRS, GRI, TCFD, SFDR, and ISSB/IFRS S1-S2. Each disclosure element is scored against required, recommended, and voluntary criteria. Gap reports prioritise missing disclosures by regulatory materiality and deadline.

calculationEngine.formula: DCS = Disclosed Mandatory Items / Total Mandatory Items × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Mandatory disclosure items are extracted from framework requirements databases and matched against the entity's disclosure text using NLP classification. The coverage score distinguishes mandatory from recommended items and highlights which specific data points are absent or insufficiently detailed.

dataPoints.name: ESRS Mandatory Coverage
dataPoints.source: ESRS requirement database vs. disclosure text
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of ESRS-mandatory disclosure requirements addressed in the current sustainability statement

dataPoints.interpretation: Count of applicable GRI topic standards with at least partial disclosure

dataPoints.name: Open Mandatory Gaps
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of mandatory disclosure requirements with no matching content identified

Upload or link the current sustainability report and disclosure documents

Assign gap remediation tasks to disclosure owners and track closure before filing

GRI (2021) GRI 1 Foundation and GRI 2 General Disclosures
IFRS S1 (2023) General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information

acronyms.def: Percentage of mandatory framework disclosure requirements addressed in the entity's sustainability statement
Mandatory EU sustainability disclosure standards under CSRD, set by EFRAG
Global sustainability disclosure standards issued by the ISSB under the IFRS Foundation

/disclosure-hub.title: Disclosure Hub
/disclosure-hub.description: Centralised sustainability disclosure management platform aggregating all framework obligations, filing calendars, document versions, and approval workflows in one place. Supports CSRD, GRI, TCFD, SFDR, ISSB, CDP, and national regulatory filings. Status dashboards provide real-time readiness across all jurisdictions.

calculationEngine.formula: DRI = Σ (Completed Items / Required Items × Weight) per Framework
calculationEngine.brief: The readiness index aggregates completion across all active framework modules, weighting each by regulatory penalty severity and filing deadline proximity. A DRI below 0.85 triggers escalation alerts to the Chief Sustainability Officer dashboard.

dataPoints.name: Active Frameworks
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of sustainability frameworks with active filing obligations for the current entity

dataPoints.name: Overall DRI
dataPoints.interpretation: Weighted disclosure readiness index across all active frameworks; target ≥0.90 at T−30 days

/diversity-equity-inclusion.title: DEI Analytics
/diversity-equity-inclusion.description: Workforce diversity metrics, pay gap analysis, and inclusion survey benchmarking across gender, ethnicity, disability, and seniority dimensions. Tracks progress against CSRD ESRS S1 workforce disclosure requirements and voluntary DEI targets. Peer benchmarking contextualises performance against sector comparators.

calculationEngine.formula: APG = (Median Male Pay − Median Female Pay) / Median Male Pay × 100
calculationEngine.brief: The adjusted pay gap controls for role, seniority, and tenure to isolate the residual unexplained difference attributable to gender. The unadjusted gap measures raw median pay difference; both are required under ESRS S1 and the EU Pay Transparency Directive.

dataPoints.name: Gender Pay Gap (Unadjusted)
dataPoints.interpretation: Raw median pay difference between male and female employees across all roles and grades

dataPoints.name: Women in Senior Leadership
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of senior leadership positions (VP and above) held by women

dataPoints.name: Ethnicity Pay Gap
dataPoints.interpretation: Median pay gap between majority and minority ethnic groups (where disclosure is voluntary)

dataPoints.name: Inclusion Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Composite inclusion survey score across belonging, fairness, voice, and growth dimensions

Configure peer benchmark comparators by sector and geography
Review the DEI scorecard and identify the largest gaps relative to targets and peers
Generate the ESRS S1 workforce diversity disclosure data pack for the sustainability statement
references: ESRS S1 (2023) Own Workforce â€” S1-6 Characteristics, S1-16 Remuneration
UK Gender Pay Gap Reporting Regulations 2017
SASB Human Capital â€” Employee Inclusion metrics

/dme-alerts.title: DME Alerts
/dme-alerts.description: Real-time alert feed from the Dynamic Materiality Engine surfacing threshold breaches across entity-level materiality scores, topic momentum shifts, and emerging risk signals. Alerts are classified by severity, topic, and affected entity, with configurable notification routing to risk owners. Historical alert archive supports trend analysis.

calculationEngine.formula: APS = Materiality Score × Momentumβ × Coverage Multiplier
calculationEngine.brief: The priority score weights materiality severity by the rate of score change (momentum) and the breadth of coverage in external data signals. Alerts with APS above the 90th percentile trigger push notifications; those above the 95th percentile escalate to the risk committee feed.

dataPoints.name: Open Alerts (Unacknowledged)
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of active alerts not yet acknowledged by an assigned risk owner

dataPoints.name: Critical Alerts (APS > P95)
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of alerts exceeding the 95th percentile priority score threshold

dataPoints.interpretation: Mean time from alert generation to owner acknowledgement and closure

dataPoints.interpretation: Total alerts generated in the trailing 30 days across all monitored entities

userInteraction: Configure materiality score thresholds and momentum triggers per topic in DME Settings
Review open critical alerts first, sorted by APS descending
Acknowledge alerts and assign to the responsible risk owner with a response deadline
Archive closed alerts and review recurring patterns in the 30-day trend analysis
references: TCFD (2021) Guidance on Scenario Analysis for Non-Financial Companies
ESRS 1 (2023) Chapter 3 â€” Materiality Assessment Process

/dme-competitive.title: DME Competitive Intelligence
/dme-competitive.description: Peer materiality benchmarking powered by the Dynamic Materiality Engine, comparing an entity's topic-level materiality scores against sector peers and industry leaders. Identifies where competitors face higher or lower material risk, informing strategic positioning and disclosure strategy. Peer cohort is configurable by sector, geography, and market cap.

calculationEngine.formula: RMI = Entity Scoreᵢ / Sector Median Scoreᵢ × 100
calculationEngine.brief: The RMI compares each entity's topic materiality score against the sector median, producing a relative index where 100 equals median. Topics with RMI above 130 flag as areas of elevated relative exposure; those below 70 indicate relative resilience versus peers.

dataPoints.name: Peer Cohort Size
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of companies in the configured peer comparison cohort

dataPoints.name: Topics Above Peer Median
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of material topics where the entity scores above the sector median RMI

dataPoints.name: Highest RMI Topic
dataPoints.interpretation: Topic with the greatest relative materiality exposure versus the peer cohort

dataPoints.name: Lowest RMI Topic
dataPoints.interpretation: Topic with the greatest relative resilience versus the peer cohort

userInteraction: Configure the peer cohort using sector, geography, and market cap filters
Review the RMI heat map to identify topics where the entity faces elevated relative exposure
Drill into each topic to compare the entity's signal drivers against the peer distribution
Export the competitive materiality report for use in strategy and investor engagement presentations
references: SASB (2023) Materiality Map â€” Industry-Specific Material Topics
GRI (2022) Sector Standards â€” Material Topic Benchmarks

/dme-contagion.title: DME Contagion
/dme-contagion.description: Systemic materiality contagion mapping that traces how material ESG risks propagate across supply chains, sector networks, and financial portfolios. Network analysis identifies contagion amplifiers and systemic nodes. Stress scenarios model contagion spread under accelerated materialisation of a triggering event.

calculationEngine.formula: CPSᵢ = Σⱼ wᵢⱼ × Materialityⱼ × Exposureᵢⱼ
standards: ECB Climate Stress Test Contagion Methodology
calculationEngine.brief: The contagion propagation score for entity i sums the product of supply-chain exposure weight, materiality score, and directional dependency for all counterparty nodes j. High CPS entities are systemic amplifiers whose materialisation would cascade risk broadly through the network.

dataPoints.name: Network Nodes Mapped
dataPoints.interpretation: Total entities modelled in the ESG contagion network including suppliers, customers, and financial counterparties

dataPoints.name: Systemic Amplifier Nodes
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of nodes in the top 1% of contagion propagation scores identified as systemic risks

dataPoints.name: Max Contagion Depth
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum supply chain distance over which contagion effects are modelled

dataPoints.name: Avg CPS (Portfolio)
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean contagion propagation score across all portfolio companies

userInteraction: Load the supply chain graph by connecting the network data source or importing a counterparty matrix
Select a triggering ESG event (e.g. regulatory shock, physical hazard) and a source node
Review the contagion heat map showing propagation intensity and depth through the network
Identify systemic amplifier nodes and flag them for enhanced monitoring or engagement
references: NGFS (2022) Scenarios for Central Banks and Supervisors â€” Network Contagion Analysis
ECB (2021) Economy-wide Climate Stress Test â€” Methodology
IPCC AR6 WG2 (2022) â€” Chapter 16 Key Risks and Compound Events
FSB (2020) The Implications of Climate Change for Financial Stability

calculationEngine.formula: PMS = Σᵢ (Topic Scoreᵢ × Topic Weightᵢ) / Σᵢ Weightᵢ
calculationEngine.brief: The platform materiality score is a weighted composite of all scored topics, with weights set by the administrator to reflect entity-specific strategic priorities. It provides a single headline signal for board-level materiality communication.

calculationEngine.formula: EMSᵢ = 0.5 × FinancialMaterialityᵢ + 0.5 × ImpactMaterialityᵢ
calculationEngine.brief: Financial materiality is scored from risk exposure and financial magnitude signals; impact materiality is scored from severity, breadth, and irremediability of impacts on people and environment. Equal weighting follows ESRS 1 double materiality symmetry; weights are configurable per entity type.

/dme-financial-risk.title: DME Financial Risk
/dme-financial-risk.description: Quantification of financial risks arising from material ESG topics identified by the Dynamic Materiality Engine. Maps material topics to balance sheet, income statement, and valuation impacts using scenario-based magnitude estimation. Outputs feed TCFD financial risk disclosures and internal stress testing.

calculationEngine.formula: EFRS = Σᵢ Materialityᵢ × Magnitudeᵢ × Likelihoodᵢ
calculationEngine.brief: Each material topic is assigned a financial magnitude (% of EBITDA or asset value at risk) and likelihood (probability within the planning horizon) derived from scenario data and sector benchmarks. The EFRS aggregates across topics, adjusting for correlation, to produce a total financial exposure estimate.

dataPoints.interpretation: Aggregate financial magnitude of all material ESG risks across all topics at 70th percentile severity

dataPoints.name: Highest Risk Topic

dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated EBITDA reduction under an orderly 1.5°C transition scenario over a 5-year horizon

dataPoints.name: Risk-Weighted Materiality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Materiality score weighted by financial exposure magnitude across all active topics

/dme-financial-risk.valueSummary: Translates abstract ESG materiality scores into quantified balance sheet and earnings impacts that financial risk managers and CFOs can act on. Provides the financial risk quantification backbone for TCFD and ESRS SBM-3 disclosures.

/dme-hub.title: DME Hub
/dme-hub.description: Central hub for the Dynamic Materiality Engine providing navigation to all DME sub-modules, system configuration, data source management, and platform-level materiality settings. Displays a consolidated summary of DME outputs across alerts, entity scores, portfolio views, and competitive intelligence. Administrative controls for topic library and weight configuration.

calculationEngine.formula: DHS = Data Freshness × Coverage Rate × (1 − Error Rate)
calculationEngine.brief: The DME health score reflects the operational status of the materiality engine: data freshness (hours since last signal ingestion), coverage rate (share of topics with active signals), and signal processing error rate. A DHS below 0.80 triggers a system alert to the platform administrator.

dataPoints.name: DME Health Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Composite operational health of the Dynamic Materiality Engine; target ≥0.90

dataPoints.name: Topics in Library
dataPoints.interpretation: Total ESG topics defined in the DME topic library across E, S, and G dimensions

dataPoints.name: Entities Monitored
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of companies and assets for which DME produces active materiality scores

dataPoints.name: Signal Ingestion Lag
dataPoints.interpretation: Average delay between external signal publication and ingestion into the DME scoring engine

acronyms.def: Operational composite reflecting data freshness, topic coverage, and signal processing error rate

/dme-index.title: DME Index
/dme-index.description: Sector-level ESG materiality indices derived from Dynamic Materiality Engine scores aggregated across all entities in each GICS sector. Indices track materiality momentum over time, enabling macro-level sustainability trend analysis and sector rotation signals for ESG investors. Custom index construction is supported.

calculationEngine.formula: SMIₛ = Σᵢ∈ₛ (EMSᵢ × MarketCapᵢ) / Σᵢ∈ₛ MarketCapᵢ
calculationEngine.brief: The sector materiality index is a market-cap-weighted average of entity materiality scores within each GICS sector, providing a capitalisation-weighted signal of aggregate sector ESG risk. Index momentum (12-month change) is used as a sector rotation factor in quantitative ESG strategies.

dataPoints.name: Highest-Risk Sector Index
dataPoints.interpretation: GICS sector with the highest current sector materiality index value

dataPoints.name: Fastest-Rising Sector
dataPoints.interpretation: Sector showing the largest absolute 12-month increase in sector materiality index

dataPoints.name: Index Universe Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Total entities and GICS sectors included in the sector index calculation

dataPoints.name: Cross-Sector Dispersion (σ)
dataPoints.interpretation: Standard deviation of sector materiality index values, measuring cross-sector risk differentiation

Review the sector index heat map and identify sectors with rising materiality momentum

/dme-pd-engine.title: DME PD Engine
/dme-pd-engine.description: Probability of default adjustment model that integrates Dynamic Materiality Engine scores into credit risk assessment, producing ESG-adjusted PD estimates for loan and bond portfolios. Quantifies how material ESG topics translate into credit deterioration risk. Supports IFRS 9 staging and ECB supervisory disclosure requirements.

calculationEngine.formula: PDₐₛᵍ = PDᵇᵃˢᵉ × exp(α + β × MaterialityScore)
standards: ECB Guide on Climate Risks in Credit
calculationEngine.brief: The ESG-adjusted PD applies a log-linear multiplier to the base credit model PD, where β is calibrated to historical ESG-credit correlation data. A MaterialityScore of 80/100 with a calibrated β of 0.008 increases PD by approximately 90%. α is a sector-specific intercept controlling for base-rate differences.

dataPoints.name: Avg PD Uplift (Portfolio)
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean ESG-driven probability of default increase across the credit portfolio

dataPoints.name: Entities with PD Uplift > 100 bps
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of borrowers where ESG materiality drives a PD uplift exceeding 100 basis points

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Weighted PD (ESG-Adjusted)
dataPoints.interpretation: Exposure-weighted average ESG-adjusted PD across the full credit portfolio

dataPoints.name: β Calibration R²
dataPoints.interpretation: Goodness of fit of the ESG-PD relationship estimated from historical default and ESG score data

Run the PD adjustment and review the obligor-level PD uplift table

EBA (2022) Guidelines on Management of ESG Risks in Credit Institutions

Accounting standard governing financial instrument classification and expected credit loss provisioning

/dme-portfolio.title: DME Portfolio
/dme-portfolio.description: Portfolio-level aggregation of Dynamic Materiality Engine scores with exposure-weighted materiality calculation, topic attribution, and risk decomposition. Enables portfolio managers to understand how entity-level ESG materiality translates into portfolio-level risk concentration. Supports TCFD portfolio-level disclosure and SFDR PAI reporting.

calculationEngine.formula: PfMS = Σᵢ (EMSᵢ × wᵢ)
calculationEngine.brief: The portfolio materiality score is the exposure-weighted average entity materiality score. Weight wᵢ equals entity i's market value divided by total portfolio value. Topic-level attribution decomposes the portfolio score into contributions from each ESG topic, enabling thematic risk management.

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Materiality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Exposure-weighted composite materiality score across all portfolio positions on a 0–100 scale

dataPoints.name: Top Contributing Topic
dataPoints.interpretation: ESG topic contributing the largest share of the portfolio materiality score

dataPoints.name: High-Materiality Positions
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of portfolio positions with entity materiality score above the high-materiality threshold of 70

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Score Trend (12M)
dataPoints.interpretation: Change in portfolio materiality score over the trailing 12 months, indicating rising aggregate ESG risk

SFDR (2019) Article 7 â€” Principal Adverse Impact Aggregation

/dme-risk-engine.title: DME Risk Engine
/dme-risk-engine.description: Forward-looking financial risk modelling engine that translates Dynamic Materiality Engine topic scores into quantified risk factors including earnings volatility, asset impairment, and stranded asset exposure. Integrates with the stress testing framework to produce ESG-adjusted VaR and CVaR estimates.

calculationEngine.formula: ERF = Σᵢ (MaterialityScoreᵢ × Sensitivityᵢ × TimeHorizonᵢ)
calculationEngine.brief: Each material topic contributes a risk factor calculated as the product of materiality score, financial sensitivity (% EBITDA per materiality point), and horizon scaling (risks discounted further out). The composite ERF feeds the Monte Carlo stress engine to generate ESG-adjusted VaR at 95% and 99% confidence.

dataPoints.name: ESG VaR (95%, 1yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: ESG-attributable Value at Risk at 95% confidence over a 1-year horizon

dataPoints.name: ESG CVaR (99%, 1yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: Expected loss in the worst 1% of ESG risk outcomes over a 1-year horizon

dataPoints.name: Top Risk Driver Topic
dataPoints.interpretation: Topic with the highest ESG risk factor contribution to portfolio VaR

dataPoints.name: Stress Scenario Delta (Hot House)
dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated portfolio NAV reduction under a disorderly 3°C NGFS Hot House World scenario

/dme-scenarios.title: DME Scenarios
/dme-scenarios.description: Scenario-based materiality evolution projections showing how ESG topic scores are expected to change under different macro, policy, and physical climate futures. Integrates NGFS, IEA, and IPCC scenario pathways with DME topic drivers to produce forward materiality trajectories. Scenario comparison highlights which topics diverge most between pathways.

calculationEngine.formula: SMSₛᵢ = EMSᵢᵀ − EMSᵢ₀ = f(Policyₛ, Physicalₛ, Technologyₛ)
calculationEngine.brief: The scenario materiality shift captures the projected change in entity topic score from the current baseline to a future horizon T under scenario s, driven by policy stringency, physical hazard intensity, and technology cost trajectory parameters. Scenarios span Orderly (Net Zero 2050), Disorderly, and Hot House World NGFS categories.

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Avg Materiality (2030, HHW)
dataPoints.interpretation: Projected portfolio materiality score in 2030 under Hot House World (3°C+) scenario

/document-similarity.title: Document Similarity
/document-similarity.description: NLP-powered similarity analysis of sustainability disclosure documents enabling peer comparison, year-on-year change detection, and boilerplate identification. Uses transformer-based sentence embeddings and cosine similarity to quantify textual overlap. Flags sections with low year-on-year change that may indicate insufficient updating of disclosures.

standards: SEC Staff Comment Letters on Boilerplate
calculationEngine.brief: Document sections are encoded as dense embedding vectors using a fine-tuned ESG language model. Cosine similarity between two section vectors ranges from 0 (orthogonal) to 1 (identical). Sections with CSS above 0.92 versus the prior year are flagged as potentially boilerplate; those below 0.30 versus peers indicate idiosyncratic disclosure language.

dataPoints.name: Avg Year-on-Year Section Similarity
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean cosine similarity between current and prior year sections across all disclosure documents

dataPoints.name: Boilerplate-Flagged Sections
dataPoints.interpretation: Count of sections flagged as potentially boilerplate due to near-identical year-on-year language

dataPoints.name: Peer Disclosure Similarity (Median)
dataPoints.interpretation: Median cosine similarity between the entity's disclosure and sector peers, indicating differentiation

dataPoints.name: Documents Indexed
dataPoints.interpretation: Total sustainability disclosure documents indexed in the similarity search database

userInteraction: Upload the current year disclosure and the prior year document for year-on-year change analysis
Select a peer cohort to run cross-company similarity benchmarking

Use the semantic search to find the most similar peer sections for language benchmarking

ESMA (2022) Supervisory Briefing on Sustainability Disclosures â€” Quality Standards
GRI (2021) Assurance of GRI Reports â€” Guidance for Practitioners

acronyms.def: Measure of angular distance between two document embedding vectors; 1 = identical, 0 = unrelated
AI/ML techniques for extracting meaning and structure from unstructured text
Transformer-based AI model used to generate dense semantic embeddings of disclosure text

dataLineage.output: Similarity score matrix with section-level granularity

/double-materiality.title: Double Materiality Assessment
/double-materiality.description: CSRD-mandated double materiality assessment workflow covering both financial materiality (how sustainability matters affect the entity financially) and impact materiality (how the entity affects people and the environment). Produces an IRO register with scored impacts, risks, and opportunities for ESRS disclosure. Supports EFRAG-prescribed stakeholder engagement documentation.

calculationEngine.formula: DMSᵢ = max(FinMatᵢ, ImpMatᵢ)
calculationEngine.brief: A topic is material under CSRD if it is material on either or both dimensions; the conservative max operator reflects ESRS 1 symmetry. Financial materiality is scored from magnitude and likelihood of financial effects; impact materiality from severity (scale, scope, irremediability) and likelihood of impact. Both dimensions are scored 0–100 with a threshold of 40 for materiality determination.

dataPoints.name: Material Topics Identified
dataPoints.interpretation: Total topics assessed as material on at least one dimension of the double materiality matrix

dataPoints.name: Financially Material Only
dataPoints.interpretation: Topics material solely on the financial materiality dimension

dataPoints.name: Impact Material Only
dataPoints.interpretation: Topics material solely on the impact materiality dimension

dataPoints.name: Doubly Material Topics
dataPoints.interpretation: Topics meeting the materiality threshold on both financial and impact dimensions simultaneously

userInteraction: Define the entity scope, value chain boundaries, and stakeholder universe in DMA Settings
Score each topic on financial materiality (magnitude, likelihood) and impact materiality (severity, likelihood)
Review the double materiality matrix and confirm topic classifications with the sustainability committee
Export the IRO register and ESRS 2 IRO-1 documentation pack for assurance and disclosure
references: ESRS 1 (2023) Chapters 3–5 â€” Double Materiality, IRO Identification, Materiality Assessment
EFRAG (2023) Implementation Guidance on Materiality Assessment (IGMA)
GRI 3 (2021) Material Topics â€” Step-by-Step Guidance
European Commission (2023) FAQ on CSRD Implementation

acronyms.def: Maximum of the financial and impact materiality scores; determines whether a topic is material under CSRD
ESRS taxonomy of sustainability matters: actual/potential impacts, financial risks, and financial opportunities
Mandatory EU sustainability disclosure standards defining double materiality requirements under CSRD

dataLineage.flow: Scoring of financial magnitude, likelihood, impact severity, and impact likelihood per topic
dataLineage.output: IRO register with dimension scores and materiality determination

dataLineage.flow: Stakeholder materiality input aggregation and weighting
dataLineage.output: Stakeholder materiality perspective integrated into topic scoring

/double-materiality.valueSummary: Provides a structured, auditable workflow for the CSRD double materiality assessment, reducing assessment time and ensuring regulatory compliance with ESRS 1 requirements. /double-materiality-workshop.title: Double Materiality Workshop
/double-materiality-workshop.description: Collaborative stakeholder workshop facilitation tool for double materiality assessment, enabling multi-participant scoring, deliberation, and consensus building. Real-time aggregation of stakeholder ratings produces facilitated materiality heat maps. Workshop outputs are automatically formatted for ESRS 2 IRO-1 stakeholder engagement documentation.

calculationEngine.formula: CMSᵢ = μ(Scoresᵢ) ± 1.96 × σ(Scoresᵢ) / √N
calculationEngine.brief: The consensus score is the mean of all participant ratings on each topic-dimension combination, with a 95% confidence interval. Topics where the CI is wide (high participant disagreement) are flagged for deliberation. The workshop facilitator can trigger live polling rounds to resolve disagreements.

dataPoints.interpretation: Share of required topic-dimension scores submitted by registered participants

Review the live heat map and flag high-disagreement topics for facilitated deliberation
Close the workshop and export the consensus materiality matrix and ESRS 2 stakeholder engagement log

/ecosystem-services.title: Ecosystem Services Valuation
/ecosystem-services.description: Natural capital accounting and ecosystem service valuation using TEEB, InVEST, and SEEA-EA methodologies. Quantifies the economic value of provisioning, regulating, cultural, and supporting services for corporate and portfolio nature-related risk assessment. Supports TNFD LEAP framework disclosure and CBD Kunming-Montreal targets.

calculationEngine.formula: ESV = Σᵢ (Areaᵢ × BiomeCoefficientᵢ × ConditionMultiplierᵢ)
standards: TEEB (2010) The Economics of Ecosystems and Biodiversity
SEEA EA (2021) System of Environmental-Economic Accounting
calculationEngine.brief: Ecosystem service value is calculated by multiplying land area by biome-specific service coefficients ($/ha/yr from TEEB/de Groot 2012 database) adjusted by an ecosystem condition multiplier (0–1) derived from remote sensing or site assessment data. The total economic value encompasses use and non-use values across all service categories.

dataPoints.name: Total Ecosystem Service Value
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual monetary value of ecosystem services across all assets in the corporate footprint

dataPoints.name: Highest-Value Service
dataPoints.interpretation: Ecosystem service category generating the largest attributed economic value

dataPoints.name: Dependencies on High-Risk Biomes
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of total ESV drawn from biomes with ecosystem condition score below 0.50

dataPoints.name: Biodiversity Intactness Index (Avg)
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean Biodiversity Intactness Index across all assessed asset locations; global safe boundary is 0.90

userInteraction: Map all asset locations to biome types using the spatial overlay tool
Assign ecosystem condition scores per site using remote sensing data or site assessments
Calculate ecosystem service values using TEEB coefficients and review by service category
Export the natural capital account and TNFD LEAP dependency/impact assessment for disclosure
references: TEEB (2010) The Economics of Ecosystems and Biodiversity â€” Synthesis Report
SEEA EA (2021) System of Environmental-Economic Accounting â€” Ecosystem Accounts
TNFD (2023) Recommendations for Nature-related Financial Disclosures v1.1
de Groot et al. (2012) Global Estimates of the Value of Ecosystems and their Services â€” Global Environmental Change

/em-climate-risk.title: EM Climate Risk
/em-climate-risk.description: Emerging market climate risk analytics combining physical hazard exposure, transition risk vulnerability, sovereign fiscal capacity, and capital flight risk into a composite EM Climate Risk Index. Covers 80+ emerging and frontier markets. Supports sovereign ESG analysis, EM fixed income risk assessment, and development finance institution due diligence.

calculationEngine.formula: EMCRI = 0.35 × PhysicalRisk + 0.30 × TransitionVulnerability + 0.20 × FiscalCapacity⁻¹ + 0.15 × CapitalFlightRisk
standards: World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal
calculationEngine.brief: Physical risk uses ND-GAIN vulnerability and readiness scores; transition vulnerability reflects fossil fuel export dependency and carbon intensity of GDP; fiscal capacity inverts the IMF fiscal space index; capital flight risk uses FX reserve adequacy and current account deficit metrics. The composite index is normalised 0–100 with 100 being maximum risk.

dataPoints.name: Highest-Risk Country
dataPoints.interpretation: Emerging market with the highest composite climate risk index score in the current universe

dataPoints.name: Avg EMCRI (EM Universe)
dataPoints.interpretation: Population-weighted average EMCRI across all 84 tracked emerging and frontier markets

dataPoints.name: Portfolio EMCRI (Exposure-Weighted)
dataPoints.interpretation: Sovereign debt portfolio's exposure-weighted composite EM climate risk index

dataPoints.name: Fossil Fuel Export Dependency > 40% GDP

/commodity-hub.title: Commodity Intelligence Hub
/commodity-hub.description: Aggregates price analytics, supply chain climate exposure, and TCFD commodity risk signals across energy, metals, and agricultural raw materials. Integrates forward curve data with physical supply disruption probabilities and carbon intensity benchmarks for portfolio-level commodity risk management.

calculationEngine.formula: CommodityRisk = w₁×PriceVol + w₂×SupplyDisruption + w₃×CarbonIntensity
calculationEngine.brief: Price volatility is measured as 90-day realised vol scaled by commodity-specific VaR. Supply disruption probability is derived from geopolitical risk scores, climate hazard exposure of production regions, and logistics concentration indices. Carbon intensity (tCO₂e/tonne) follows GHG Protocol Scope 3 Category 1 for upstream raw material extraction.

dataPoints.name: Price Volatility (90d)
dataPoints.interpretation: Annualised 90-day realised volatility; energy commodities typically 30-55%, base metals 18-30%

dataPoints.name: Supply Disruption Probability
dataPoints.interpretation: Probability of >10% supply shock within 24 months based on geopolitical and climate hazard models

dataPoints.name: Carbon Intensity
dataPoints.interpretation: Lifecycle GHG intensity per tonne of commodity produced and shipped to port

dataPoints.name: Commodity Climate VaR
dataPoints.interpretation: Value-at-risk attributable to climate physical and transition drivers at 95th percentile

dataPoints.name: Forward Curve Contango
dataPoints.interpretation: Spot-to-12m futures spread indicating market expectation of supply/demand trajectory

/commodity-intelligence.title: Commodity Intelligence
/commodity-intelligence.description: Delivers commodity market intelligence with a climate transition risk overlay, mapping price sensitivity to carbon pricing scenarios, clean energy substitution timelines, and demand destruction risk for fossil and transitional materials. Supports buy-side and treasury teams in hedging climate-linked commodity exposures.

calculationEngine.formula: D_adj(t) = D_base(t) × (1 - SubstitutionRate(t)) × CarbonPriceElasticity
calculationEngine.brief: Base demand follows IEA World Energy Outlook trajectories per scenario. Substitution rate captures technology displacement (e.g., EV uptake reducing oil demand). Carbon price elasticity is sector-calibrated: oil −0.05 to −0.15/$/tCO₂ per IPCC AR6 WGIII. Transition risk premium = spread between current forward price and scenario-consistent fundamental value.

dataPoints.name: Oil Demand Destruction (NZ2050)
dataPoints.interpretation: Reduction in oil demand under Net Zero 2050 relative to stated policies baseline

dataPoints.name: Carbon Price Elasticity (Oil)
dataPoints.interpretation: Change in oil demand per $1 increase in carbon price

dataPoints.interpretation: Cumulative demand increase for lithium, cobalt, and nickel driven by energy transition

dataPoints.name: Transition Risk Premium
dataPoints.interpretation: Price discount applied to fossil commodities reflecting long-run transition demand destruction

dataPoints.name: Supply Concentration (HHI)
dataPoints.interpretation: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index measuring market concentration for critical commodity supply

/commodity-inventory.title: Commodity Inventory
/commodity-inventory.description: Tracks physical commodity holdings with carbon intensity benchmarking against sectoral and regional peers, computes inventory-level GHG exposure, and monitors alignment with science-based commodity procurement targets. Designed for corporate treasury and sustainability teams managing physical raw material stocks.

calculationEngine.formula: CI_relative = (CI_holding - CI_benchmark) / CI_benchmark × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Carbon intensity per tonne is calculated using supplier-specific emission factors where available, falling back to regional and process-level LCA averages. Benchmark intensity follows SBTi sector pathways for agricultural commodities (FLAG guidance) and IEA sectoral benchmarks for energy and metals. Relative intensity score drives procurement decarbonisation prioritisation.

/community-impact.title: Community Impact Analytics
/community-impact.description: Measures social value creation from corporate community investment programmes using the S1000+ framework, Social Return on Investment methodology, and UN SDG contribution mapping. Quantifies community investment ROI in monetary social value terms and benchmarks performance against industry peers.

calculationEngine.formula: SROI = Total Social Value (£) / Total Investment (£)
calculationEngine.brief: Social value is monetised by mapping outcomes to financial proxies (HACT Social Value Bank, New Economy Manchester). Deadweight (what would have happened anyway), attribution (share of credit), displacement (negative side-effects), and drop-off (outcome decay) adjustments reduce gross social value to net present social value. SROI ratios above 3:1 are considered high-performing for infrastructure programmes.

dataPoints.name: SROI Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Social value generated per unit of monetary investment; >3× is considered high-performing

dataPoints.name: Total Social Value
dataPoints.interpretation: Monetised present value of all community outcomes attributed to the investment programme

dataPoints.interpretation: Number of Sustainable Development Goals materially contributed to by the programme

dataPoints.name: Community Investment Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Community investment as a percentage of pre-tax profit or revenue, per LBG benchmarks

dataPoints.name: Attribution Adjusted Value
dataPoints.interpretation: Net social value after deadweight, attribution, displacement, and drop-off adjustments

/company-profiles.title: Company Profiles
/company-profiles.description: Provides comprehensive ESG company profiles aggregating ESG scores, controversy flags, CDP disclosure status, board composition, SBTi commitment status, and historical engagement notes across thousands of investee companies. Serves as the central data hub for issuer-level ESG intelligence across the platform.

calculationEngine.formula: ESG_profile = 0.40×E_score + 0.35×S_score + 0.25×G_score
calculationEngine.brief: Environmental score integrates carbon intensity, energy mix, water risk, and biodiversity exposure. Social score covers labour practices, supply chain standards, community impact, and product safety. Governance score reflects board independence, audit quality, remuneration alignment, and shareholder rights. Controversy adjustment: each active controversy reduces composite by severity-weighted penalty (Minor: −1, Moderate: −5, Severe: −15 points).
