Replaces traditional Excel-based financial models with an interactive, real-time engine covering all ITC/PTC scenarios, DSCR covenants, and LP/GP structures typical of 50–500 MW US solar IPP deals.

/renewable-portfolio-intelligence.title: Renewable Portfolio Intelligence
/renewable-portfolio-intelligence.description: Institutional-grade portfolio analytics for 50-asset renewable energy portfolios spanning Solar PV, Wind Onshore, Wind Offshore, Hydro, and Geothermal. Covers portfolio VaR, efficient frontier, correlation matrix, peer benchmarking, vintage/geography diversification, attribution, and ESG/taxonomy alignment across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.formula: σ_port = √(wᵀΣw); VaR₉₅ = σ_port × 1.645 × TotalRevenue; EF: min wᵀΣw s.t. w·μ = μ_target, Σw = 1
calculationEngine.brief: Portfolio variance computed from 5×5 technology covariance matrix using seeded returns (resource, regulatory, merchant price, counterparty, operational risks). Efficient frontier traces 50 portfolios from minimum-variance to maximum-Sharpe via parametric sweep of target return. DSCR weighted average uses outstanding debt as weights. EU Taxonomy screening uses Art.10 criteria (climate change mitigation) with DNSH assessment for water, biodiversity, waste, and pollution.

dataPoints.name: Portfolio P50 IRR
dataPoints.formula: Asset-weighted average of individual project IRRs
dataPoints.interpretation: Blended equity IRR across portfolio; higher for offshore wind (13–18%) vs utility solar (9–13%)

dataPoints.name: Portfolio VaR (95%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual revenue at risk at 95% confidence — combines resource, price, and operational volatility across portfolio

dataPoints.name: WACI (Revenue)
dataPoints.interpretation: Weighted Average Carbon Intensity — SFDR PAI 1 metric; near-zero for operating RE portfolio but relevant for construction phase

dataPoints.name: EU Taxonomy Alignment
dataPoints.interpretation: Share of portfolio revenue classified as taxonomy-aligned; renewable generation qualifies under Art.10; requires DNSH pass on 5 environmental objectives

dataPoints.name: Sharpe Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Risk-adjusted return vs risk-free rate; target Sharpe >1.0 for diversified RE portfolio; offshore-heavy portfolios typically higher

dataPoints.name: Correlation (Solar-Wind)
dataPoints.interpretation: Low correlation between solar and wind improves diversification; offshore wind-solar correlation even lower (~0.10) due to different weather dependencies

acronyms.def: Maximum expected loss at a given confidence level (95% or 99%) over a defined horizon — here applied to annual portfolio revenue
SFDR PAI 1 — revenue-weighted Scope 1+2 GHG intensity; central TCFD metric for portfolio carbon risk assessment
EU Taxonomy requirement — economic activity must not significantly harm 5 other environmental objectives while contributing to the primary objective
Actual generation / (installed capacity × 8,760 hrs); solar utility typically 22–28%, wind onshore 30–42%, wind offshore 40–55%
Markowitz mean-variance locus of portfolios with maximum return for each level of risk — identifies optimal technology allocation

dataLineage.source: 50 seeded RE assets (technology, capacity MW, vintage, geography, DSCR)
dataLineage.flow: Portfolio aggregation engine
dataLineage.output: Blended IRR, VaR, WACI, taxonomy alignment, DSCR distribution

/renewable-portfolio-intelligence.valueSummary: Designed for RE-focused infrastructure fund managers, institutional LPs conducting portfolio due diligence, and ESG/taxonomy reporting teams. /solar-resource-performance.title: Solar Resource & Performance Analytics
/solar-resource-performance.description: Engineering-grade solar resource assessment and operational performance analytics with NASA POWER live API integration. Covers GHI/DNI/DHI irradiance analysis, PR/CUF/availability metrics, PVsyst-style loss waterfall (13 loss factors), Arrhenius degradation modelling, weather normalization via P-value regression, and soiling analytics across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: OLS Weather Normalization + Arrhenius Degradation + PVsyst Loss Waterfall
calculationEngine.formula: PR_norm = PR_actual × (T_ref − 25) / (T_act − 25) × (GHI_ref/GHI_act); Δη(t) = A·exp(−Ea/RT)·√t + EFC·k_cyc
calculationEngine.brief: Performance Ratio: actual generation / (GHI × nameplate capacity × module efficiency); normalized using temperature coefficient (−0.35%/°C from 25°C STC). OLS regression decomposes actual production into GHI-explained variance (R²) and residual (operational underperformance). Arrhenius calendar aging: Δη_cal = A × exp(−Ea / (R × T_avg)) × √(operating_years); Cycle aging: Δη_cyc = EFC × k_cyc; Combined: √(Δη_cal² + Δη_cyc²). Loss waterfall applies 13 sequential loss factors from GHI_ref to POA to DC to AC output.

dataPoints.name: Performance Ratio (PR)
dataPoints.interpretation: Core operational KPI; IEC 61724-1 defines PR; >80% is strong; below 75% signals inverter or soiling issue

dataPoints.name: Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF)
dataPoints.interpretation: Equivalent to capacity factor; 20–28% typical for fixed-tilt utility solar in high-irradiance locations

dataPoints.name: Temperature Coefficient Loss
dataPoints.interpretation: Modern mono-PERC: −0.35%/°C; TOPCon: −0.28%/°C; higher losses in hot climates (India, MENA, Australia)

dataPoints.name: Soiling Loss
dataPoints.interpretation: Largest variable loss in arid regions (MENA, India); automated cleaning triggers when soiling loss > cleaning cost breakeven (~3–5%)

dataPoints.name: Degradation Rate
dataPoints.formula: Arrhenius calendar aging + EFC cycle aging
dataPoints.interpretation: Industry median 0.50%/yr (mono-PERC); Arrhenius component dominates for utility-scale; LID corrected in first-year by P stabilization

dataPoints.name: P90 GHI (10-year)
dataPoints.formula: Inter-annual variability from NASA POWER historical data
dataPoints.interpretation: P90 10-year (P90/√10) used by lenders; single-year P90 is −6 to −12% depending on climate zone

dataPoints.name: NASA POWER GHI
dataPoints.interpretation: Free NASA satellite-derived irradiance; 1-degree spatial resolution; validated against ground stations at ±8% annual accuracy for most locations

userInteraction: Select a location preset (Phoenix/Chicago/Madrid/Dubai/Chennai/Santiago/Custom) in the left Site panel; set capacity MW, tilt, azimuth, and mounting configuration (Fixed/SAT/DAT)
Choose module technology in the left Module panel (Mono PERC / Bifacial PERC / TOPCon / HJT / CdTe); enable Bifacial Gain and set bifacial factor and albedo

Navigate to "PR Analysis" tab for the IEC 61724-1 loss waterfall: POA irradiance → temperature losses → soiling → wiring → mismatch → shading → inverter → bifacial gain → Net Yield
Check "Energy Yield" tab for monthly MWh production and the 25-year revenue projection with PPA escalation; "Technology Compare" tab ranks all 5 technologies by 25-yr lifetime energy and $/Wp value

Open "Degradation" tab for the Arrhenius calendar aging curve (LFP/NMC/NCA) — configure annual degradation rate and LID in the left panel; "Soiling & Loss" shows monthly soiling vs rainfall and cleaning schedule optimization
Review "P50/P90" tab for lognormal probabilistic yield: P50/P75/P90/P99 values and uncertainty source decomposition (interannual GHI, satellite uncertainty, degradation, soiling)
Navigate to "Tilt Optimization" (optimal tilt = lat × 0.87 + 4°) and "Tracker vs Fixed" for SAT/DAT gain quantification and incremental NPV economics
Check "Shading Analysis" tab for GCR-dependent hourly shading model and "Inverter Performance" for CEC/Euro-weighted efficiency curve and clipping analysis

Open "NASA POWER Live" tab to fetch live monthly GHI data for the selected lat/lon via NASA POWER API; compare satellite vs NASA data in "Satellite vs Ground" tab; download "Summary Report" for IE submission
references: IEC 61724-1:2021 — Photovoltaic System Performance — Part 1: Monitoring
IEC 61853-2:2016 — PV Module Performance Testing and Energy Rating
NASA POWER v2.0 — Prediction of Worldwide Energy Resources API Documentation
NREL — Review and Status of Degradation Rates of Crystalline Silicon PV (Jordan et al., 2022)
PVsyst 7.x — Technical Reference Manual (Photovoltaic System Software)

acronyms.def: Total solar radiation on a horizontal surface = Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) × cos(zenith) + Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI)
IEC 61724-1 key metric: actual AC output / (GHI × system rating); normalises for irradiance — allows fair comparison across sites
Annual MWh generated / (nameplate MW × 8,760 hours) — equivalent to capacity factor; used in Indian RE financing
Irradiance incident on the tilted module surface after transposition from GHI using Perez or Hay-Davies model
First-year mono-PERC power loss (1–2%) due to boron-oxygen complex activation; modern LeTID resistant cells reduce to <0.5%
Synthetic year assembled from best-representative months across long-term dataset; P50 energy yield basis for most project finance models

dataLineage.flow: Monthly GHI + temperature parsing
dataLineage.output: Site-specific P50 GHI, inter-annual variability σ, TMY construction

dataLineage.source: User-defined system parameters (capacity, tilt, azimuth, losses)
dataLineage.flow: 13-factor loss waterfall calculation
dataLineage.output: DC/AC energy estimate, PR, CUF, soiling-adjusted yield

dataLineage.source: Historical production data (user upload or seeded)
dataLineage.flow: OLS regression: production ~ GHI × temperature
dataLineage.output: R², normalized PR, weather-adjusted underperformance identification

/solar-resource-performance.valueSummary: Essential for solar asset managers, technical advisors (independent engineers), and lenders conducting resource assessment. Replicates the PVsyst + SolarAnywhere workflow for pre-construction yield studies and operational performance benchmarking — with free NASA POWER irradiance data as a rapid-access alternative to paid satellite data providers (SolarAnywhere, Solargis).

/ppa-revenue-analytics.title: PPA & Revenue Analytics Engine
/ppa-revenue-analytics.description: Comprehensive power purchase agreement analytics and merchant revenue risk engine for renewable energy projects. Covers PPA pricing (fixed/indexed/proxy revenue swap), merchant price exposure, VPPA/CfD structuring, curtailment risk, counterparty ECL under IFRS 9, and revenue at risk (RaR) Monte Carlo across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: PPA Pricing + Merchant VaR + VPPA Mark-to-Market
calculationEngine.formula: RaR₉₅ = σ_rev × 1.645 × E_gen; VPPA_MTM = (VPPA_strike − P_spot) × E_gen; ECL = PD × LGD × EAD
calculationEngine.brief: Revenue variance comes from 3 independent sources: (1) volume risk — generation Monte Carlo (Box-Muller); (2) price risk — merchant price volatility (GBM); (3) curtailment risk — grid congestion model. PPA Revenue = min(contracted_volume, generation) × PPA_price. Merchant Revenue = max(0, generation − PPA_volume) × spot_price. VPPA mark-to-market = (strike − settlement_price) × generated_MWh; negative when spot > strike (VPPA buyer receives settlement). IFRS 9 ECL: PD from S&P transition matrix, LGD from security/guarantee structure, EAD = undiscounted PPA receivables.

dataPoints.name: PPA Price (fixed)
dataPoints.formula: Negotiated; benchmarked vs LCOE + developer margin
dataPoints.interpretation: Contracted energy price for PPA tenor (10–20yr); must exceed LCOE for positive equity return; $45–55/MWh typical for new US utility solar PPA (2024)

dataPoints.name: Merchant Revenue Share
dataPoints.interpretation: Residual revenue after PPA obligation; 100% merchant projects use Price Cap Contract (CfD) as downside protection

dataPoints.name: VPPA Settlement
dataPoints.interpretation: Virtual/financial PPA — no physical delivery; corporate buyer receives/pays settlement when spot diverges from strike; eliminates additionality risk for RE100 buyers

dataPoints.name: Curtailment Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Grid-instructed curtailment for congestion management; increases with RE penetration; basis risk if curtailment is concentrated

dataPoints.name: Counterparty ECL
dataPoints.interpretation: Expected credit loss on PPA receivables; investment-grade offtaker (BBB+): ~0.2% ECL; sub-IG: 1.5–4% ECL; collateral/LC reduces LGD

dataPoints.name: Revenue at Risk (95%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum annual revenue shortfall at 95% confidence; sum in quadrature of volume, price, and curtailment variance components

userInteraction: Configure the deal in the left PPA Structure panel: contract type (fixed/indexed/proxy revenue swap/VPPA), tenor years, strike price $/MWh, contracted volume %, and escalation %

Navigate to "Curtailment Intelligence" for hourly and monthly curtailment pattern charts, anti-curtailment strategy effectiveness comparison, and curtailment trend forecast

Use "Negotiation Toolkit" for term sheet generation and priority matrix; check "Carbon & RECs" for SREC/bundled REC market pricing; review "Intelligence Report" for the deal Proceed/Negotiate/Pass recommendation
references: LevelTen Energy — RE Quarterly PPA Price Index 2024
EFET — Master Agreement for Power Purchase and/or Transmission of Electricity (2022)
IFRS 9 Financial Instruments — Expected Credit Loss (IASB 2014)
FERC Order 888 / PURPA — Must-take provisions and avoided cost rates
Wood Mackenzie — Corporate PPA Market Monitor (Q3 2024)

acronyms.def: Long-term contract for electricity sale from RE project to offtaker at agreed price; cornerstone of project finance debt sizing
Financial PPA with no physical delivery — settlement based on (strike − spot) × generation; used by corporates for RE100 claims without physical delivery obligation
UK/EU mechanism: generator receives top-up (or repays) difference between strike and reference price; eliminates merchant price risk; used in EU and UK offshore wind
IFRS 9 forward-looking credit loss = PD × LGD × EAD; applied to PPA receivables for counterparty default risk provisioning
Analogous to VaR — maximum annual revenue loss at given confidence level (95%); captures combined volume × price × curtailment risk
1 REC = 1 MWh of renewable generation; priced separately from energy ($0.50–8.00/MWh depending on state RPS); significant revenue component in RPS-compliance states

dataLineage.source: PPA contract terms (price, tenor, volume, indexation, floor/cap)
dataLineage.flow: Revenue model: contracted + merchant + REC + ancillary
dataLineage.output: Annual revenue breakdown, DSCR under PPA + merchant scenarios

dataLineage.source: Monte Carlo: generation σ, merchant price GBM, curtailment γ distribution
dataLineage.flow: Combined revenue variance model (sum in quadrature)
dataLineage.output: P10/P50/P90 annual revenue, Revenue at Risk at 95% confidence

dataLineage.source: Counterparty credit rating + collateral structure (LC, escrow, parent guarantee)
dataLineage.flow: IFRS 9 ECL: S&P transition PD × LGD × EAD
dataLineage.output: Expected credit loss provision on PPA receivables, DSCR adjusted for ECL

/ppa-revenue-analytics.valueSummary: Designed for project finance bankers, independent power producers, and corporate renewable energy buyers structuring PPA and VPPA transactions. Covers the full revenue risk spectrum from fully-contracted PPA to fully-merchant exposure, with IFRS 9 ECL provisioning, VPPA mark-to-market, and curtailment analytics that replicate the commercial structuring analysis for 100–500 MW solar and wind deals.

/bess-grid-analytics.title: BESS & Grid Services Analytics
/bess-grid-analytics.description: Comprehensive battery energy storage system (BESS) financial and technical analytics. Covers LCOS optimization, revenue stacking (arbitrage/frequency regulation/capacity/demand charge), Arrhenius + cycle degradation modelling, dispatch optimization (greedy price arbitrage), FERC Order 841 compliance, and co-location solar+BESS ITC analysis across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: LCOS + Arrhenius/Cycle Degradation + Greedy Dispatch Optimization
calculationEngine.formula: LCOS = (CAPEX + ΣOPEX_t/(1+r)^t + ΣReplace_t/(1+r)^t) / ΣE_discharge_t/(1+r)^t; Δη(t) = √(Δη_cal² + Δη_cyc²)
calculationEngine.brief: LCOS (Levelized Cost of Storage) accounts for CAPEX, O&M, capacity augmentation, and replacement over project life, discounted against energy discharged. Arrhenius calendar aging: Δη_cal = A × exp(−Ea/(R×T_avg)) × √t, where Ea = 0.5eV, A = 0.024, R = 8.314e-5 eV/K. Cycle aging: Δη_cyc = EFC × k_cyc, k_cyc = 2.0e-5. Combined: √(Δη_cal² + Δη_cyc²). Dispatch: sort 24h price array; charge lowest 4h (charge rate = C-rate × capacity); discharge highest 4h. Revenue stacking: arbitrage + frequency regulation (CAISO AS or ERCOT SCED) + capacity (ISO capacity market or BTM demand charge reduction).

dataPoints.name: LCOS
dataPoints.interpretation: 4-hour Li-ion: $110–160/MWh (2024); 2-hour: $140–200/MWh; 8-hour: $80–120/MWh; declining at ~15%/yr with LFP cost trajectory

dataPoints.name: Arbitrage Revenue
dataPoints.interpretation: Highly variable by market; ERCOT 2023: $40–90k/MW-yr; CAISO: $20–50k/MW-yr due to solar cannibalization reducing mid-day prices

dataPoints.name: Frequency Regulation Revenue
dataPoints.formula: Regulation capacity price × MW committed × availability
dataPoints.interpretation: Fast-response BESS earns performance-adjusted regulation revenue; CAISO RegUp/RegDn: $5–25/MW-hr depending on season

dataPoints.name: Capacity Revenue
dataPoints.formula: Capacity auction clearing price × ICAP MW
dataPoints.interpretation: BESS qualifies as capacity resource under FERC 841; PJM BRA clearing 2023: $34.13/MW-day; ISO-NE FC: $28–55/kW-yr

dataPoints.name: Degradation (10-yr)
dataPoints.formula: Arrhenius calendar + cycle aging at target EFC/yr
dataPoints.interpretation: LFP chemistry: lower calendar aging than NMC; 1 EFC/day LFP: ~15% at 10 yr; NMC at 1.5 EFC/day: ~25% at 10 yr

dataPoints.name: Round-trip Efficiency
dataPoints.interpretation: AC-coupled systems lose efficiency at PCS; DC-coupled co-location avoids PCS losses for solar charging; key LCOS driver

dataPoints.name: ITC on BESS (IRA)
dataPoints.interpretation: IRA 2022 key change: standalone BESS now ITC eligible (≥3 hr storage); co-location with solar also eligible; same bonus adder stack as solar

userInteraction: Configure the BESS system in the left System Configuration panel: chemistry (LFP/NMC/NCA), capacity MWh, power MW, C-rate target, and operating temperature
Enable co-location toggle in the left panel to model Solar + BESS co-location — ITC basis updates to reflect grid charge fraction reduction under IRA §48E rules
Open "LCOS Waterfall" tab to see the PNNL-methodology cost decomposition: CAPEX component → O&M NPV → augmentation NPV → replacement NPV → LCOS $/MWh

Navigate to "Revenue Stacking" tab for annual revenue breakdown across arbitrage, frequency regulation, capacity market, and demand charge reduction; adjust market (CAISO/ERCOT/PJM/MISO/ISO-NE) in the Market panel
Check "Dispatch Optimizer" tab for the greedy 24-hour dispatch schedule: charge 4 cheapest hours → discharge 4 most expensive hours; view SOC profile and daily revenue
Open "Degradation Model" tab for the Arrhenius calendar aging + EFC cycle aging combined curve; "Chemistry Compare" tab benchmarks LFP vs NMC vs NCA on 10/15/20-year capacity fade
Review "Co-location (Solar+BESS)" tab for DC-coupled vs AC-coupled comparison, clipping capture revenue, and ITC impact on combined project economics
Navigate to "Augmentation Plan" tab for the capacity top-up schedule to maintain usable MWh above 80% threshold; "FERC 841 Compliance" tab checks market access eligibility by ISO
Check "Frequency Regulation" and "Capacity Markets" tabs for per-market revenue rate benchmarks; "Project Finance" tab shows DSCR/IRR for the full BESS project
Use "Scenario Analysis" for combined price/degradation/EFC stress; "Long-Duration Storage" compares 8-hr BESS to flow battery/LAES/H2 storage; "Sensitivity Summary" shows NPV tornado chart
references: PNNL — Grid Energy Storage Technology Cost and Performance Assessment 2022 (LCOS Methodology)
FERC Order 841 — Electric Storage Participation in Markets Operated by Regional Transmission Organizations
NREL — Optimal Battery Storage Operations for PV Integration (2021)
BloombergNEF — BESS Outlook 2024 — Cost Trajectory and Revenue Stack
IEC 62619:2022 — Secondary Cells and Batteries Containing Alkaline or Other Non-Acid Electrolytes — Safety Requirements

acronyms.def: PNNL-defined metric: all-in discounted cost per discounted MWh discharged over system life — analogous to LCOE for generation assets
Standardized cycle count: 1 EFC = 1× full discharge from 100% to 0% SOC; used to track battery aging regardless of partial cycling depth
Battery energy level as % of usable capacity; operational range typically 10–90% to limit degradation; wider SOC = more energy but faster aging
Power relative to energy capacity: 1C = full charge/discharge in 1 hour; BESS typically 0.25C (4-hr) to 1C (1-hr); higher C-rate = more revenue stacking opportunity
2018 FERC order mandating ISO/RTOs to revise tariffs to remove barriers for electric storage resources; created capacity, energy, and AS market access for BESS
BESS sited on customer side of utility meter; primary value: demand charge reduction, TOU optimization, backup power — different regulatory framework from FTM

dataLineage.source: BESS technical inputs: capacity, C-rate, chemistry, cycle target, temperature
dataLineage.flow: Arrhenius calendar + cycle degradation model (year 1–25)
dataLineage.output: Annual capacity (MWh effective), EFC remaining, LCOS by year

dataLineage.source: Market price data (seeded hourly for CAISO/ERCOT/PJM/MISO/ISO-NE)
dataLineage.flow: Greedy dispatch: sort 24h prices → charge 4 lowest → discharge 4 highest
dataLineage.output: Arbitrage revenue, dispatch efficiency, round-trip loss accounting

dataLineage.source: Revenue stacking: arbitrage + freq reg + capacity + demand charge
dataLineage.flow: Project IRR / NPV / LCOS calculator
dataLineage.output: BESS project economics under combined revenue streams vs LCOS cost

/bess-grid-analytics.valueSummary: Designed for BESS developers, utilities evaluating storage procurement, and infrastructure funds assessing standalone or co-located battery projects. Covers the full analytical stack from Arrhenius degradation modelling to ISO-specific revenue stacking and FERC 841 compliance — replacing the combination of PNNL LCOS spreadsheets, manufacturer degradation tools, and ISO revenue calculators typically used in BESS project evaluation.

/renewable-ml-forecasting.title: ML Forecasting & Risk Engine
/renewable-ml-forecasting.description: Quantitative machine learning and probabilistic forecasting engine for renewable energy portfolio risk management. Implements Monte Carlo (Box-Muller), Bayesian Normal-Normal conjugate updating, OLS factor regression with seasonality, 3-state HMM ENSO climate regime detection (La Niña / Neutral / El Niño), portfolio VaR, ensemble forecasting, and long-range stress scenarios across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: Bayesian Conjugate Update + HMM Viterbi + Box-Muller Monte Carlo
calculationEngine.formula: μ₁ = σ₁²(μ₀/σ₀² + Σxᵢ/σ²); σ₁² = 1/(1/σ₀² + n/σ²); VaR₉₅ = σ_port × 1.645 × Revenue
calculationEngine.brief: Monte Carlo uses Box-Muller transform: z = √(−2 ln u₁) × cos(2π u₂); combined σ from 6 uncertainty sources (resource, wake, availability, degradation, curtailment, soiling) in quadrature. Bayesian Normal-Normal: posterior mean μ₁ = σ₁²(μ₀/σ₀² + Σxᵢ/σ_obs²); posterior σ₁² = 1/(1/σ₀² + n/σ_obs²). HMM: 3 states (La Niña/Normal/El Niño) with 3×3 calibrated transition matrix; Viterbi decoding finds most likely state path. OLS: β₁ = ΣΔXΔY / ΣΔX², R² from SS decomposition. Ensemble: weighted average of MC + Bayesian + OLS + HMM-adjusted forecasts; weights from information ratio minimisation.

dataPoints.name: Monte Carlo P90
dataPoints.formula: Box-Muller combined σ across 6 uncertainty sources
dataPoints.interpretation: P90 10-year exceedance (divide σ by √10 for multi-year); used for lender base case; combines resource, technical, and curtailment uncertainty in quadrature

dataPoints.name: Bayesian Posterior Mean
dataPoints.interpretation: Posterior shrinks toward observed data as number of observations grows; prior dominates with few observations; converges to sample mean with many observations

dataPoints.name: OLS R² (GHI factor)
dataPoints.interpretation: How much generation variance is explained by irradiance alone; high R² indicates good resource tracking; low R² signals operational issues or data quality problems

dataPoints.name: HMM Regime Probability
dataPoints.formula: Forward-backward algorithm; Viterbi for state sequence
dataPoints.interpretation: La Niña → lower solar irradiance in tropical Americas + Australia; El Niño → higher irradiance in same zones; NAO and IOD also modelled via toggles

dataPoints.name: Portfolio VaR (95%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Revenue at risk at 95% confidence; benefit of diversification measured by VaR reduction vs sum-of-individual VaRs; solar-wind correlation ~0.20 gives 15–25% VaR reduction

dataPoints.name: Ensemble Forecast MAPE
dataPoints.interpretation: Mean absolute percentage error; ensemble typically outperforms any individual model by 10–25% on MAPE; weighted ensemble uses information ratio optimisation

dataPoints.name: Degradation Trajectory
dataPoints.interpretation: 25-year production forecast with Arrhenius uncertainty bands; P10 degradation (pessimistic) used for lender DCF; replacement scenario NPV quantifies augmentation value

userInteraction: Set P50 GWh and 6 uncertainty sigma values in the left Uncertainty panel; open "Monte Carlo Dist." tab for the Box-Muller 1,000-run distribution — P10/P50/P90/P99 exceedance percentiles update in real time
Navigate to "Bayesian Posterior" tab — enter prior mean/σ and add observed production months; posterior mean and credible interval shrink toward the data as n grows (Normal-Normal conjugate update)

Open "OLS Regression" tab for the GHI factor model: β₁ (GHI slope), R² decomposition, residual time series analysis, and temperature interaction term; low R² flags operational underperformance vs resource variability

Check "HMM Regimes" tab for 3-state La Niña / Neutral / El Niño Markov regime detection; Viterbi path shows most likely climate state sequence; regime-adjusted P50 generation differs by ±5-15% by geography
Review "Forecast vs Actual" for model backtesting accuracy and "Model Comparison" for individual vs ensemble MAPE comparison across Monte Carlo, Bayesian, OLS, and HMM models
Open "Ensemble" tab for the information-ratio weighted model combination; "Feature Importance" shows which input factors (GHI, temperature, humidity, ENSO index) drive the most forecast variance

Navigate to "Portfolio VaR" tab — set the solar-wind correlation (default 0.20); portfolio VaR = σ_port × 1.645 × Revenue shows the diversification benefit vs sum of individual VaRs; covariance matrix displayed
Use "Stress Testing" tab with Severe (−15% GHI, +20% price vol) or Extreme (−25% GHI, La Niña + drought compounding) scenarios to stress-test DSCR breach probability and revenue at risk

Check "Seasonality" tab for monthly production patterns and ENSO-driven seasonal shifts; "Degradation" tab combines Arrhenius calendar aging with Monte Carlo uncertainty bands across the forecast horizon

Open "Uncertainty Decomp." to see variance attribution by source; "Long-Range Scenarios" for 2030/2040 climate-adjusted forecasts; "Live API" to fetch NASA POWER irradiance for the selected portfolio site
references: IPCC AR5 WG1 Chapter 14 — Climate Phenomena and their Relevance for Future Regional Climate Change (ENSO)
IEA — Solar PV Forecasting and Uncertainty Estimation (2020)
Gelman, A. et al. — Bayesian Data Analysis, 3rd Edition (CRC Press, 2013)
NREL — Probabilistic Energy Forecasting for Solar Power Systems (2022)
Diebold, F.X. & Mariano, R.S. — Comparing Predictive Accuracy, JBES 1995 (Forecast evaluation)

acronyms.def: 3-state probabilistic model (La Niña/Normal/El Niño) with transition matrix; Viterbi algorithm decodes most-likely state sequence from observed irradiance data
Pacific climate pattern with 3–7 year cycle; La Niña suppresses solar irradiance in tropical Americas; El Niño enhances it; material driver of inter-annual generation variability
Linear regression minimising SSRes = Σ(y − ŷ)²; here used to regress generation on GHI and temperature factors; R² measures explanatory power of irradiance
P50: 50% probability of exceeding (median); P90: 90% probability of exceeding (pessimistic); P90 = P50 − 1.28 × σ for normal distribution; lenders use P90 for debt sizing
Forecast accuracy metric: Σ|actual − forecast|/|actual| / n × 100; lower is better; <5% MAPE is excellent for day-ahead solar forecasting
Calibration metric: fraction of actual values falling within stated confidence interval; well-calibrated 90% PI should have PICP ≈ 90%

dataLineage.source: Portfolio technical specs (P50 GWh, capacity MW, technology mix)
dataLineage.flow: Monte Carlo Box-Muller × 1,000 runs with 6 uncertainty σ inputs
dataLineage.output: P10/P50/P90/P99 generation distribution, VaR, exceedance curve

dataLineage.source: 12 months of observed production data (user input)
dataLineage.flow: Bayesian Normal-Normal conjugate update
dataLineage.output: Posterior mean and σ, shrinkage toward data vs prior, updated P50/P90

dataLineage.flow: HMM 3-state transition matrix + Viterbi decoding
dataLineage.output: Regime-adjusted P50, probability of La Niña/Neutral/El Niño persistence, generation adjustment factor

/renewable-ml-forecasting.valueSummary: Designed for RE portfolio managers, quantitative risk analysts at infrastructure funds, and independent engineers needing probabilistic yield forecasting beyond deterministic P50 models. Provides the full probabilistic toolkit — Monte Carlo, Bayesian updating, OLS factor models, and ENSO regime detection — in a unified engine that replaces fragmented Python scripts and Excel Monte Carlo add-ins used in RE investment risk management.

/offshore-wind-resource.title: Offshore Wind Resource & Wake Loss Analytics
/offshore-wind-resource.description: Engineering-grade offshore wind resource assessment covering Weibull wind speed distribution fitting, Jensen wake model array loss calculation, AEP computation via power curve integration, P50/P90 probabilistic yield, IEC 61400-3 site characterisation, and extreme wind event analysis. Covers 8 benchmark offshore markets with 18 analytical tabs for pre-FEED resource assessment.

calculationEngine.methodology: Weibull AEP Integration + Jensen Wake Model + P90 Lognormal
calculationEngine.formula: AEP = 8760 × P_rated × ∫₀^∞ P(v)/P_rated × f(v,k,λ)dv; Wake: U_wake = U₀(1−(1−√(1−Ct))×(D/(D+2k×x))²); P90 = P50×exp(−1.282×σ)
calculationEngine.brief: Weibull distribution fit: shape k (1.8–2.4 for most offshore sites) and scale λ derived from mean wind speed; AEP integration numerically sums (power_curve × Weibull_pdf × frequency) over 25 m/s speed bins. Jensen top-hat wake model: wake velocity deficit U_deficit = U₀ × (1−√(1−Ct)) × (D/(D+2k×x))², k=0.04 offshore (stable atmosphere); total array wake loss = 1 − ΣU_wake/ΣU₀. P90 uses lognormal with interannual variability σ from ERA5/MERRA-2 historical long-term; combined uncertainty RSS across resource, wake, availability, and measurement bias.

dataPoints.name: Annual AEP (P50)
dataPoints.interpretation: Gross AEP before wake and availability losses; North Sea 12MW turbine at class I site: ~52 GWh/turbine/yr; capacity factor 45–55% for prime North Sea sites

dataPoints.name: Wake Loss
dataPoints.interpretation: Strongly dependent on spacing: 5D spacing → ~15% loss; 8D spacing → ~8% loss; offshore wakes persist further (lower TI) than onshore wakes

dataPoints.name: Capacity Factor (net)
dataPoints.interpretation: Best offshore CF: 52–58% (prime North Sea Grade A sites); US East Coast: 42–50%; floating sites (deep water high wind): 50–58%

dataPoints.name: P90/P50 ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: P90 haircut typically 6–12% for offshore; smaller than onshore because maritime wind is more stable; σ includes interannual, wake model, and measurement uncertainty

dataPoints.name: Extreme Wind (50yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: Site-specific 50-year return period wind speed for turbine structural class selection; IEC Class I turbines rated to U₅₀=50m/s; typhoon-prone sites may require Class S special

dataPoints.name: Wind Power Density
dataPoints.formula: WPD = ½ρv³; ρ≈1.23 kg/m³ at sea level
dataPoints.interpretation: Key site quality metric; IEC Class I: WPD >500 W/m²; Class III: <300 W/m²; higher WPD → better economics but higher structural loads

dataPoints.name: Turbine Spacing (optimal)
dataPoints.interpretation: Offshore wind turbine rows typically 8–10D downwind spacing, 5–6D cross-wind spacing; tighter spacing reduces cabling cost but increases wake losses

Check "AEP Calculator" tab for gross → wake-corrected → availability-adjusted net AEP; configure wake model (Jensen/Gaussian/No Wake) and losses in the left panel
Open "P50/P90" tab for lognormal probabilistic yield with full uncertainty decomposition: interannual variability, satellite vs mast bias, wake model uncertainty, and availability
Review "Seasonal Analysis" for monthly AEP profile and storm season resource; "Extreme Events" for 50-year return wind speed and IEC turbine class suitability assessment
Navigate "Environmental" tab for noise, shadow flicker, bird collision risk, and marine mammal impact; "Grid Connection" for cable loss estimate and AC vs HVDC preliminary assessment

DNVGL-ST-0437:2016 — Loads and Site Conditions for Wind Turbines
BOEM — Offshore Wind Resource Assessment — US East Coast (2023)

acronyms.def: Gross energy generated per year before wake and availability losses; P = ∫ P(v) × Weibull_pdf(v) dv × 8760; unit: GWh/yr
Standard deviation of wind speed / mean wind speed; offshore TI ≈ 5–8% (lower than onshore due to stable boundary layer); drives turbine fatigue load spectrum
Class I (v_avg ≥ 10m/s), Class II (≥ 8.5m/s), Class III (≥ 7.5m/s); determines design loads and structural specifications
½ρv³ W/m² — cubic relationship makes high-wind sites exponentially more valuable; key initial screening metric before full AEP calculation
Global hourly reanalysis dataset at 31km resolution; primary long-term wind resource data source for offshore pre-feasibility; validated against offshore met masts

dataLineage.source: Site coordinates + turbine selection → Weibull k/λ fit (seeded from region)
dataLineage.flow: Jensen wake model on array layout
dataLineage.output: Net AEP after wake + availability; P50/P90 from combined σ

dataLineage.source: Wind speed bins (0–25m/s) × Weibull pdf × power curve lookup
dataLineage.flow: Numerical AEP integration
dataLineage.output: Gross AEP, wind speed → power contribution histogram, capacity factor

dataLineage.flow: Lognormal P90 with uncertainty RSS

/floating-offshore-wind.title: Floating Offshore Wind LCOE & Technology Analytics
/floating-offshore-wind.description: Comprehensive floating offshore wind (FOW) technology and economics analytics. Covers LCOE breakdown by floater concept (Spar-Buoy, Semi-submersible, TLP, Barge), mooring system design, EPCI cost modelling, learning curve trajectory, supply chain risks, CfD/PPA structuring for deep-water sites, and country-by-country policy comparison across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: LCOE Learning Curve + Floater CAPEX Model + CfD Contract Analytics
calculationEngine.formula: LCOE = (CAPEX + NPV(OPEX)) / NPV(AEP); Floater_CAPEX = Turbine + Hull + Mooring + EPCI + Grid; Learning: LCOE(n) = LCOE₀ × n^(-b), b=log₂(LR)
calculationEngine.brief: LCOE for floating offshore wind (2024): Spar-Buoy $140–180/MWh (deepest water, Hywind, most deployed); Semi-submersible $120–160/MWh (broadest depth range, most commercial pipeline); TLP $130–170/MWh (smallest water-plane area, requires good seabed); Barge $110–140/MWh (shallowest, most cost-sensitive to wave conditions). LCOE learning rate assumed 14–18% per doubling of cumulative deployed capacity. EPCI (Engineering, Procurement, Construction, Installation) cost is 25–35% of total CAPEX for floating vs 15–20% for fixed-bottom due to installation complexity.

dataPoints.name: LCOE (floating, 2024)
dataPoints.interpretation: vs $60–90/MWh for fixed-bottom offshore; declining at 12–18%/yr as supply chain scales; target $60–80/MWh by 2035 per IEA Net Zero trajectory

dataPoints.name: CAPEX (floating)
dataPoints.interpretation: Hywind Scotland: ~$5,500/kW (2017 pioneer); Kincardine: ~$4,200/kW; target <$3,000/kW by 2030 with supply chain maturation

dataPoints.name: Water Depth Range
dataPoints.interpretation: Fixed-bottom limited to ~60m; floating unlocks 80% of global offshore wind resource in waters >60m depth

dataPoints.name: Mooring Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: Catenary mooring: heavier but cheaper at moderate depth; taut-leg: smaller footprint, requires anchors; polyester rope reduces weight at depth >300m

dataPoints.name: EPCI Cost Share
dataPoints.interpretation: Key advantage of floating: turbines assembled at quayside, towed to site (no expensive offshore crane vessel); reduces EPCI vs fixed-bottom by avoiding complex offshore heavy lifts

dataPoints.name: CfD Strike (floating)
dataPoints.interpretation: UK Allocation Round 5 (2024): floating offshore wind strike price ~£225/MWh; France (Bretagne): €200–225/MWh; EU Innovation Fund support available for first movers

userInteraction: Set site parameters in the left panel: water depth (50-2000m) and distance to shore; depth determines eligible floater concepts shown in "Floater Technology" tab

acronyms.def: Offshore wind turbines mounted on floating foundations anchored to seabed by mooring lines; enables deployment in waters >60m depth inaccessible to fixed-bottom monopiles/jackets
Full project delivery contract; for floating wind, EPCI includes port assembly, tow-out, mooring installation, cable pull-in; 25–35% of total CAPEX
Floating platform held by vertical taut tendons under tension; excellent motion characteristics; smaller footprint than Semi-sub; requires good uniform seabed for anchor piles
Revenue stabilisation mechanism: generator receives (strike − market_price) top-up or repays when market > strike; eliminates merchant price risk; UK AR5 strike for floating: ~£225/MWh
NASA/EU scale 1-9 measuring technology maturity; floating offshore wind hull systems at TRL 7-8 (commercial pilot); mooring/dynamic cable at TRL 8; supply chain at TRL 5-6

dataLineage.flow: LCOE = (CAPEX + NPV(OPEX)) / NPV(AEP)

/offshore-wind-finance.title: Offshore Wind Project Finance & CfD Analytics
/offshore-wind-finance.description: Institutional project finance model for offshore wind transactions covering CfD structure, Newton-Raphson IRR, DSCR/LLCR covenant analysis, P50/P90 lender case, Monte Carlo IRR distribution, LP/GP waterfall, EU Taxonomy and SFDR PAI alignment, and comparable transaction benchmarking across 18 analytical tabs. Supports UK CfD, European CF+ contracts, and US IRA PTC structures.

calculationEngine.methodology: Newton-Raphson IRR + CfD Revenue Model + DSCR Covenant Analysis
calculationEngine.formula: IRR: Σ CF_t/(1+IRR)^t = 0; CfD: Revenue_t = min(E_t × strike, E_t × spot + max(0, strike−spot) × E_t); DSCR = CFADS / DS_t
standards: UK Contracts for Difference (CfD) Allocation Round 5
S&P RE Project Finance Rating Criteria
calculationEngine.brief: Offshore wind CfD revenue: generator receives max(strike, market_price) per MWh for CfD duration (15 years, UK); strike price set at AR auction; reference price = SEMO/N2EX Day-ahead index. Post-CfD period: merchant or new contract. DSCR calculated on CFADS = Revenue − OPEX − tax − capex reserve; lender covenant: min DSCR ≥ 1.20× (cash trap at 1.10×). Construction finance: EPC lump-sum turn-key typical for offshore wind; interest during construction (IDC) modelled on S-curve drawdown. Newton-Raphson IRR tolerance 1e-8, 200 iterations on post-tax equity cash flows.

dataPoints.name: Equity IRR (offshore)
dataPoints.interpretation: Target equity IRR: 9–11% for CfD-contracted offshore wind (low risk); 12–14% for merchant/post-CfD; floating offshore: 12–16% to compensate technology risk

dataPoints.name: LCOE (offshore fixed)
dataPoints.interpretation: Best UK/Dutch offshore fixed-bottom 2024: $65–75/MWh; US East Coast: $80–100/MWh (higher labor + supply chain costs)

dataPoints.name: CfD Strike (fixed offshore)
dataPoints.interpretation: UK AR4 (2023) fixed-bottom strike: ~£73/MWh (2012 prices); AR5 2024: ~£82/MWh; US IRA PTC: $27.50/MWh base + adders for 10 years

dataPoints.name: DSCR (min, offshore)
dataPoints.interpretation: CfD-contracted wind: lenders comfortable at 1.20× minimum due to revenue certainty; merchant periods require 1.35–1.50×; offshore construction risk premium reflected in higher DSRA

dataPoints.name: Construction Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: US offshore: higher than Europe due to Jones Act vessel constraints and limited supply chain; monopile: $2,200–2,800/kW; jacket: $2,500–3,200/kW; transition piece and array cabling: $200–400/kW additional

dataPoints.name: Project Finance Tenor
dataPoints.interpretation: Offshore wind project finance tenor matches CfD duration (15yr) for maximum revenue certainty period; tail cash flows during merchant period used for refinancing or equity

acronyms.def: Revenue stabilisation: generator receives top-up (strike − market) per MWh when market < strike; repays when market > strike; UK mechanism; eliminates merchant price risk for 15-year term

Capitalised interest on drawn debt during construction period; funded by construction facility; added to total project debt at financial close; offshore IDC: 3–5% of debt amount due to 2–4yr construction
6-month debt service held in escrow; funded at financial close from equity; released on final maturity; provides lender liquidity buffer; offshore DSRA typically 6 months vs 3 months onshore

acronyms.full: Cash Flow Available for Debt Service
acronyms.def: EBITDA − maintenance capex − tax − working capital; primary metric for DSCR calculation; offshore CFADS also deducts major maintenance provision (blade replacement, gearbox overhaul)
Lump-sum turnkey contract typical for offshore wind; EPC contractor takes construction risk; typical EPC contract for offshore: $1.5–2.5 billion for 500–1,000 MW project

/offshore-grid-infrastructure.title: Offshore Grid & Cable Infrastructure Analytics
/offshore-grid-infrastructure.description: Engineering economics analytics for offshore wind grid connection covering AC vs HVDC technology selection, cable sizing and loss calculation, offshore substation platform design, grid code compliance by country, reliability and outage modelling, congestion and curtailment risk, and offshore hub topology design across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: Cable Loss (I²R) + AC vs HVDC Breakeven + Availability (MTBF) Model
calculationEngine.formula: P_loss = 3I²R×L; I = P/(√3×V); HVDC_loss = P_terminal + α×L; Breakeven: L* = (P_terminal − ΔP_terminal) / (R_AC − R_HVDC); Availability = 1 − f_fault × MTTR / 8760
calculationEngine.brief: AC cable losses: P_loss = 3 × I² × R per km × length; I = P_MW × 1000 / (√3 × V_kV); cable resistance R varies by conductor area (300mm² Cu ≈ 0.060 Ω/km; 1200mm² Cu ≈ 0.015 Ω/km). Reactive charging current limits AC cable length: 132kV → max ~80km; 220kV → max ~120km without reactive compensation. HVDC losses: fixed terminal conversion loss (~0.6% each end) + cable loss ~0.003%/km; HVDC breakeven vs AC ≈ 80–100km at 1GW. Offshore substation platform: jacket-mounted for 20–60m; monopile for <40m; floating for >60m water depth.

dataPoints.name: Cable Loss (AC 132kV)
dataPoints.interpretation: 66kV array cable: ~0.5% per km; 132kV export: ~0.3% per km; 220kV export: ~0.15% per km; HVDC export: ~0.003%/km + fixed terminal losses

dataPoints.name: HVDC Breakeven Distance
dataPoints.formula: AC total cost = HVDC total cost
dataPoints.interpretation: HVDC economically superior beyond ~80km at 1GW; at 500MW: ~100km; at 2GW: ~60km; DC cost higher at short distances due to converter station cost premium (~$150–250M per terminal)

dataPoints.name: Grid CAPEX (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Offshore substation (jacket platform + transformers): $150–400M; export cable supply + install: $0.5–2M/km (33–220kV AC), $1.5–4M/km (HVDC); onshore substation: $50–150M

dataPoints.name: Cable Reliability
dataPoints.interpretation: HVDC submarine cable: ~0.08 faults/100km/yr; repair takes 30–90 days; N-1 redundancy (spare cable) costs 20–40% premium but eliminates full outage risk

dataPoints.name: Offshore Substation
dataPoints.interpretation: Jacket platform (20–60m): $150–250M; includes GIS 220/33kV transformers, reactive compensation, protection, control, accommodation; second platform for N-1 redundancy costs 30% less than first

dataPoints.name: Grid Code Compliance
dataPoints.formula: Frequency range, reactive power, FRT, ROCOF
dataPoints.interpretation: Key requirements: fault ride-through (0.25s at 0V), reactive power capability Q/P ≥ 0.33, ROCOF immunity (UK: 1Hz/s), synthetic inertia (UK 2026+ mandate for grid-forming inverters)

userInteraction: Set farm capacity, distance to shore, and voltage level in the left Site panel; AC vs HVDC recommendation appears immediately in the quick stats bar
Open "Cable Sizing" tab to view current rating vs conductor area, resistance/reactance/capacitance table, and optimal conductor selection for the specified power and voltage
Navigate to "AC vs HVDC Analysis" for the technology comparison: losses by distance, cost crossover chart (~80km breakeven at 1GW), HVDC-LCC vs VSC vs AC comparison table
Check "Grid Loss Model" tab for annual energy loss calculation (I²R model), monetized loss at PPA price, and cable loss % sensitivity to loading factor
Open "Cable Cost Model" for CAPEX waterfall: cable supply → installation vessel → burial → protection → J-tubes → offshore substation → onshore substation; distance sensitivity curve
Review "Offshore Substation" tab for platform type comparison (jacket/monopile/floating based on depth), transformer configuration, and harmonic filter requirements
Navigate "Grid Code Compliance" for country-specific requirements (UK Grid Code, ENTSO-E NC RfG, NERC, AEMO); "Reliability & Outage" tab for MTBF-based availability and annual energy not delivered
Check "Curtailment & Congestion" tab for grid-caused curtailment by season and "Frequency Regulation" for synthetic inertia and FFR revenue potential
Open "Future-Proofing" tab for capacity expansion pathway, hydrogen export option, and digital substation upgrade; "Hub Topology" for energy island/centralized hub economics
Review "Country Grid" for grid connection regimes by country (UK OREI, German TSO, Dutch hub model, US BOEM/FERC); "Summary Report" for the grid infrastructure investment recommendation
references: CIGRÉ TB 379 — Update of Service Experience of HV Underground and Submarine Cable Systems (2013)
ENTSO-E — Network Code on Requirements for Grid Connection of Generators (RfG)
National Grid ESO — Grid Code CC.6 — Offshore Wind Connection (2024)
WindEurope — Offshore Wind Grid Infrastructure: Towards a Meshed Offshore Grid (2023)

acronyms.def: DC transmission technology; near-zero reactive power losses; economical for distances >80km or interconnections; two main types: LCC (thyristor) and VSC (IGBT); VSC preferred for offshore (no need for AC grid at both ends)
SF₆ (or SF₆-free) insulated switchgear used in offshore substations; compact form factor critical for space-constrained offshore platform topsides; IEC 62271 standard
Active power response within 1 second of frequency deviation; HVDC-connected offshore wind can deliver FFR via power ramp; UK ESO FFR market from 2022
Grid security standard: system must survive loss of any single element (cable, transformer, line) without disconnecting load; N-1 offshore substation requires redundant transformer/cable
dF/dt following a fault; UK requirement: distributed generators ride through 1Hz/s ROCOF; large HVDC-connected offshore wind must provide synthetic inertia (grid-forming inverters from 2026)

dataLineage.source: Farm capacity, distance, voltage level → cable cross-section sizing (thermal + voltage drop), cable type selection
dataLineage.flow: AC cable loss: I²R; HVDC loss: terminal + 0.003%/km; total loss comparison
dataLineage.output: Cable loss %, annual GWh loss, revenue impact, AC vs HVDC recommendation

dataLineage.source: Fault rate per 100km, MTTR, load factor → MTBF model
dataLineage.flow: Availability = 1 − (fault_rate × MTTR/8760)
dataLineage.output: Fleet availability %, annual energy not delivered, revenue risk from outage

dataLineage.source: Country grid code requirements (UK/EU/US/TW) + project parameters
dataLineage.flow: Compliance check against requirements
dataLineage.output: Grid code compliance status, required reactive power range, frequency response capability

/offshore-grid-infrastructure.valueSummary: Designed for offshore wind grid engineers, project developers, and transmission system operators evaluating offshore grid connection strategy. Covers the full grid infrastructure decision stack from cable sizing and AC vs HVDC selection through offshore substation design, grid code compliance, and reliability modelling — replacing the combination of PSCAD/DIgSILENT simulations and Excel cost models typically used in pre-FEED offshore grid studies.

/offshore-wind-om.title: Offshore Wind O&M & Asset Performance Management
/offshore-wind-om.description: Comprehensive offshore wind operations and maintenance analytics covering turbine component failure rates, weather-window vessel dispatch, OPEX modelling, predictive maintenance economics, blade erosion impact, fleet availability modelling, digital twin value assessment, and long-term OPEX trajectory across 18 analytical tabs for offshore wind asset managers.

calculationEngine.methodology: MTBF Availability Model + Arrhenius Blade Erosion + CBM Value Calculator
calculationEngine.formula: Availability = 1 − (λ_fail × MTTR / 8760) − weather_downtime − planned_maint; OPEX_total = Σ(λᵢ × Cost_repair_i) + Vessel_charter + Fixed_O&M
standards: ORE Catapult Offshore Wind O&M Report 2023
calculationEngine.brief: Turbine availability: technical availability = 1 − (failure_rate × MTTR / 8760); total availability includes weather downtime (% of days Hs > access limit per season), planned maintenance (~3–5 days/turbine/yr). Vessel dispatch: CTV for minor corrective (Hs < 1.5m), SOV for major corrective (Hs < 2.5m), HLV for crane-required (Hs < 1.0m). Blade erosion: leading edge erosion at tip speeds >80m/s; AEP loss 0.5–3%/yr depending on rain intensity and coating; repair cost vs AEP recovery NPV determines optimum intervention threshold.

dataPoints.name: Fleet Availability
dataPoints.interpretation: Best-in-class offshore O&M (e.g. Gemini, Hornsea 1): 96–97%; industry average: 93–95%; older turbines with early gearbox failures: 88–92%

dataPoints.name: OPEX (offshore)
dataPoints.interpretation: Fixed-bottom offshore: $70–100/kW/yr; floating (higher vessel cost): $90–130/kW/yr; reducing with digitalization and CTV/SOV pooling efficiency

dataPoints.name: Gearbox Failure Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Gearbox: highest cost component failure; mean replacement cost £500–800k offshore; MTTR 10–20 days depending on HLV availability; drives major O&M cost variability

dataPoints.name: Weather Window (CTV)
dataPoints.interpretation: North Sea average: 60–70% accessible days; summer peak: 85%; winter trough: 40–50%; SOV expands to 80–85% of days at Hs < 2.5m threshold

dataPoints.name: Blade Erosion AEP Loss
dataPoints.formula: ΔCp from leading edge roughness × AEP
dataPoints.interpretation: Sandy/rainy sites (UK North Sea, Taiwan): 1.5–3%/yr; milder sites: 0.5–1%/yr; protective coating (VorTex) reduces erosion rate 60–80%; repair cost vs AEP recovery typically positive NPV after ~5yr service

dataPoints.name: Predictive Maint Saving
dataPoints.interpretation: Condition-based monitoring (CMS vibration + oil particle count + thermal imaging) enables 2–4 week early fault detection vs reactive failure; reduces MTTR from 20 to 5 days for gearbox; OPEX saving $10–25/kW/yr

userInteraction: Set fleet parameters in the left panel: number of turbines, age, rated MW, and select turbine model (different models have different component failure rates)
Configure maintenance strategy toggle (Corrective Only / Preventive / Predictive/CBM) and enable remote monitoring; "Availability Model" tab updates live with the selected strategy
Open "Failure Rate Database" tab for the component failure rate table: gearbox, main bearing, generator, blade, pitch, yaw, transformer, power electronics — with MTTR days and annualized cost $/kW
Navigate to "Weather Window" tab for seasonal access analysis and optimal maintenance campaign timing; "Vessel Dispatch" shows 12-month CTV/SOV/HLV utilization and vessel pooling economics
Check "Maintenance Cost" waterfall: major corrective → minor corrective → preventive → remote monitoring → vessel → port → insurance → overhead; "OPEX Breakdown" shows $/kW/yr by component
Open "Blade Erosion" tab for the leading edge erosion model: tip speed vs erosion rate, annual AEP loss, repair campaign cost vs AEP recovery NPV, erosion protection coating upgrade economics
Navigate "Predictive Maintenance" for CBM vs time-based comparison: availability uplift, false alarm rate, implementation cost NPV, and payback period
Check "Component Lifecycle" for replacement schedule (gearbox: 10-15yr; blades: 20yr; PCS: 8-12yr) and "Spare Parts" for strategic inventory EOQ analysis with lead time and stockout probability
Open "Digital Twin" tab for architecture assessment and value quantification; "Fleet Benchmarking" compares your fleet KPIs vs 8 comparable offshore wind farms
Review "OPEX Trajectory" for 25-year cost profile including warranty, LTSA, and post-warranty ad-hoc periods; "Annual Performance" shows 12-month dashboard with turbine-level ranking
references: ORE Catapult — Offshore Wind Operations & Maintenance Report 2023
DNVGL-RP-0416:2016 — Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, and Safety (RAMS) for Wind Turbines
IEC 61400-26-1:2019 — Wind Energy Generation Systems — Availability for Wind Turbines
SPARTA — System Performance, Availability and Reliability Trend Analysis (ORE Catapult offshore wind database)
Mishnaevsky, L. — Leading Edge Erosion of Wind Turbine Blades: Understanding, Prevention and Protection (Coatings, 2021)

acronyms.def: Average operating time between component failures; MTBF = 1/failure_rate; higher MTBF = more reliable component; offshore wind gearbox MTBF ≈ 7–20 years
Average time to restore a failed turbine including logistics, vessel mobilization, and repair; offshore MTTR 5–30 days depending on component and vessel availability
Small high-speed vessel (12–24 passengers) for daily crew rotation to offshore turbines; access limited to Hs < 1.5–2.0m; charter rate $5–15k/day; most common O&M vessel type
Walk-to-work vessel with gangway and accommodation (40–120 crew); operates in Hs < 2.5–3.0m; charter rate $25–60k/day; enables maintenance campaigns far from port

Maintenance triggered by actual measured degradation (vibration, oil particles, temperature) rather than fixed time intervals; requires CMS (Condition Monitoring System) sensors; reduces unplanned downtime
Surface roughness of turbine blade leading edge caused by rain erosion, insect accumulation, and UV degradation; increases drag, reduces lift; measured as equivalent sand roughness; drives AEP losses

dataLineage.source: Fleet parameters + turbine model → component failure rates + MTTR (seeded by turbine type)
dataLineage.flow: Availability model: 1 − (fail_rate × MTTR/8760) − weather_downtime − planned
dataLineage.output: Fleet availability %, lost production GWh, revenue impact $M

dataLineage.source: Site Hs distribution (seeded) × CTV/SOV access threshold → weather window % by month
dataLineage.flow: Vessel dispatch optimisation: CTV for minor, SOV for major, HLV for crane work
dataLineage.output: Vessel utilization %, charter cost $M, optimal vessel fleet composition

dataLineage.source: Blade erosion model: tip speed + rain rate → erosion rate → AEP loss %
dataLineage.flow: Repair NPV: ΔRevenue(AEP recovery) − repair cost → break-even year
dataLineage.output: Annual AEP loss %, optimal repair trigger year, coating upgrade NPV

/offshore-wind-om.valueSummary: Designed for offshore wind asset managers, O&M service providers, and infrastructure fund asset management teams. Covers the full offshore wind operational analytics stack from turbine reliability and vessel dispatch through blade erosion management, predictive maintenance economics, and digital twin implementation — providing the operational due diligence framework for buying, selling, or managing operating offshore wind assets.

/wind-repowering-intelligence.title: Wind Repowering & Life Extension Intelligence
/wind-repowering-intelligence.description: Decision analytics for onshore wind repowering and life extension investments. Covers incremental IRR of repowering vs continuing operations, AEP uplift from larger rotor diameter, grid connection re-use value, brownfield vs greenfield NPV comparison, decommissioning economics, country-specific permitting regimes, and full project finance for repowered assets across 18 analytical tabs.

calculationEngine.methodology: Incremental IRR + AEP Uplift + Brownfield NPV Bridge
calculationEngine.formula: IRR_incr: Σ(CF_repower_t − CF_continue_t)/(1+IRR)^t = 0; AEP_uplift = P_rated_new × CF_new × 8760 − P_rated_old × CF_old × 8760; Grid_value = NPV(new_connection_cost_avoided)
standards: IEA Wind TCP Task 26 Social Acceptance
calculationEngine.brief: Incremental IRR of repowering calculated on the difference between repowering cash flows (negative CAPEX + decommissioning in year 0-2, then new revenue 20yr) and continuation cash flows (existing revenue with rising OPEX). AEP uplift driven by larger rotor: modern 5MW/175m turbine achieves 35–50% CF vs old 1.5MW/77m at 25–35%; net uplift = 40–80% more annual energy from same land. Grid re-use: avoided new connection cost (£50–200/kW for onshore) is quantifiable value driving repowering economics. Permitting fast-track: Germany EEG §16b grants 10% bonus for repowering at same site; UK NSIP threshold (>350MW) rarely triggered; simplified EIA in most EU countries.

dataPoints.name: Incremental IRR (repower)
dataPoints.interpretation: Repowering IRR highly sensitive to grid connection reuse (saves $50–200/kW) and permitting fast-track; best cases (Germany §16b bonus + high wind): 18–22% incremental IRR

dataPoints.name: AEP Uplift
dataPoints.formula: Old CF% vs new CF% × same land area
dataPoints.interpretation: Modern 5MW/175m turbine at 35% CF vs old 1.5MW/77m at 25% CF: 2.5× nameplate × 1.4× CF = 3.5× more energy from roughly same site footprint

dataPoints.name: Grid Re-use Value
dataPoints.interpretation: Existing grid connection (substation + line) is a major off-balance-sheet value for repowering; new onshore grid connection: £40–150/kW onshore; proximity to load centers increases this value

dataPoints.name: Life Extension OPEX Premium
dataPoints.formula: Additional inspection, refurbishment vs new-build OPEX
dataPoints.interpretation: Life extension (5-15yr beyond design life) requires major structural inspection, blade refurbishment, gearbox overhaul; additional cost $15–25/kW/yr; worthwhile only when no viable repowering permit

dataPoints.name: Decommissioning Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: Net decommissioning cost after scrap value: typically $30–80/kW (onshore); scrap steel value partially offsets removal cost; foundation removal cost varies by foundation type and local regulation

dataPoints.name: Repowering Permit Timeline
dataPoints.interpretation: Germany §16b: 18–30 months for same-site repowering with simplified procedure; UK: 2–4 years (NSIP for >350MW); US: varies by state (2–5yr); Denmark: simplified procedure for same-site

userInteraction: Enter existing fleet parameters in the left panel: number of old turbines, rating kW, age, current CF%, and remaining permit life; configure repowering strategy (Full/Partial/Life Extension/Hybrid)
Select new turbine rating and hub height in the left Repowering Strategy panel; AEP uplift calculation updates in the "AEP Uplift" tab showing gross and net generation improvement
Open "Overview" tab for the decision recommendation card (Repower Now / Extend Life / Wait / Decommission) based on incremental IRR vs hurdle rate and permit status
Navigate to "Repowering Economics" tab for the full CAPEX vs NPV(future revenue) comparison: old fleet NPV, new fleet NPV, incremental NPV, and incremental IRR calculation

Check "Full vs Partial Repower" tab for the decision matrix by turbine performance tier; "Life Extension" tab shows 5/10/15yr extension NPV vs repowering alternative with structural certification requirements
Open "Grid Re-use Value" tab for existing grid connection value quantification: avoided new connection cost, queue position benefit, and grid congestion relief value

Navigate "Revenue Bridge" for the cash flow waterfall: current revenue → lost during construction → new revenue → net incremental revenue; "Permitting & Regulatory" tab shows country-specific fast-track regimes

Review "Environmental" tab for net environmental benefit (fewer larger turbines = lower bird risk, smaller footprint); "Construction Planning" for repowering campaign timeline and crane mobilization cost
Check "Financial Model" for the 20-year project P&L and "IRR vs Brownfield NPV" for the breakeven analysis: minimum PPA price / CF% for positive incremental IRR; optimal timing sensitivity
Review "Case Studies" for 8 repowering comparables (Germany Altmark, Netherlands IJsselmeer, Denmark Lolland, etc.); "Decision Summary" for the final investment recommendation with full rationale
references: WindEurope — Repowering and Lifetime Extension of Wind Turbines Report (2023)
German Renewable Energy Act (EEG) 2023 — §16b Repowering of Wind Energy Installations
NREL — Land-Based Wind Turbine Repowering Feasibility Study (2022)
DNV — Wind Energy Life Time Extension: Structural Integrity and Safety (RP-0073)
IEA Wind TCP Task 26 — Research Recommendations for Wind Power Social Acceptance

acronyms.def: Replacement of old wind turbines with modern, larger turbines at same site after end of design life; typically doubles or triples nameplate capacity and AEP on same land footprint
Continued operation beyond original design life (typically 20yr) after structural assessment and certification; requires major component overhaul; typically 5–15yr additional life
German repowering incentive: 10% bonus on feed-in premium for repowering at same site within 5km; simplified permit under BImSchG §16 for same-location repowering
Additional annual energy generation from repowered vs original turbines at same site; driven by larger rotor (higher CF) and higher nameplate capacity; key economic driver for repowering

Development at an existing site with prior use; repowering is brownfield vs greenfield (new site); brownfield advantages: existing road access, grid connection, community relationships, permits history
UK planning regime for energy projects >350MW onshore wind requiring development consent order (DCO); most repowering projects below threshold and use local planning authority instead

dataLineage.source: Existing fleet parameters + new turbine selection → AEP uplift calculation (CF × capacity × 8760)
dataLineage.flow: Incremental cash flow: (new_revenue − old_revenue) − (repower_capex + decom_cost)
dataLineage.output: Incremental IRR, breakeven PPA price, NPV vs life extension alternative

dataLineage.source: Grid connection parameters + local grid cost benchmarks
dataLineage.flow: Avoided cost NPV at project discount rate
dataLineage.output: Grid re-use value $M, incremental IRR uplift from grid reuse

dataLineage.source: Country permit timeline + EEG §16b / UK planning / US state process
dataLineage.flow: Permit risk-adjusted probability weighting
dataLineage.output: Expected permit timeline, fast-track probability, regulatory risk score

/wind-repowering-intelligence.valueSummary: Designed for wind energy asset managers, infrastructure fund portfolio managers, and developers evaluating the 15,000+ MW of ageing onshore wind assets globally reaching end of design life in 2024–2030. Provides the full decision analytics for repowering — from incremental IRR calculation and AEP uplift quantification through grid re-use value, decommissioning economics, country permitting pathways, and project finance — enabling data-driven repower vs extend vs decommission decisions.

/green-hydrogen-lcoh.title: Green Hydrogen LCOH Engine
/green-hydrogen-lcoh.description: Levelised Cost of Hydrogen engine modelling electrolyser CAPEX, electricity cost, capacity factor, efficiency and stack replacement to target IEA <$2/kgH2 by 2030.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOH = (CAPEX×CRF + OPEX) / Annual_H2_Production
calculationEngine.brief: Capital recovery factor applied to electrolyser CAPEX; electricity cost represents 60-70% of LCOH at current tariffs.

dataPoints.name: Electrolyser CAPEX
dataPoints.interpretation: Lower CAPEX directly reduces LCOH; target <$300/kW by 2030 for cost parity.

dataPoints.name: Electricity Cost Share
dataPoints.interpretation: Dominant cost driver; green H2 viability requires renewable electricity <$30/MWh.

dataPoints.name: System Efficiency
dataPoints.interpretation: PEM efficiency 50–60 kWh/kgH2; alkaline 50–55 kWh/kgH2 at stack level.

userInteraction: Define electrolyser technology (PEM/ALK/SOEC) and capacity (MW)
Input electricity price ($/MWh) and annual capacity factor (%)
Apply capital recovery factor to CAPEX and add fixed/variable OPEX
Divide total annual cost by annual H2 production to yield LCOH ($/kgH2)

acronyms.def: All-in cost per kg of hydrogen produced over project lifetime
Annualisation factor = r(1+r)^n/((1+r)^n-1)
Electrolyser technology using solid polymer electrolyte
Mature low-cost electrolyser technology using liquid KOH electrolyte

dataLineage.flow: → LCOH sensitivity model
dataLineage.output: $/kgH2 by scenario

dataLineage.flow: → learning rate curve
dataLineage.output: Cost reduction vs cumulative GW deployed

/green-hydrogen-lcoh.valueSummary: Green hydrogen LCOH is dominated by electricity cost (60-70%); achieving <$2/kgH2 requires <$30/MWh renewable electricity and electrolyser CAPEX below $300/kW, consistent with IEA Net Zero by 2050 milestones.

/hydrogen-project-finance.title: Green Hydrogen Project Finance
/hydrogen-project-finance.description: Project finance waterfall for green hydrogen assets covering electrolyser, compression, storage and distribution CAPEX with H2 price scenario analysis and IRR sensitivity to electricity costs.

calculationEngine.formula: IRR: NPV(FCF, r)=0; DSCR=EBITDA/DebtService
calculationEngine.brief: H2 price scenarios ($2-8/kg) drive revenue; electricity cost sensitivity is the primary IRR lever for green H2 projects.

dataPoints.name: Total CAPEX
dataPoints.interpretation: Electrolyser typically 40-50% of total CAPEX; BOP and compression add 25-35%.

dataPoints.name: Project IRR
dataPoints.interpretation: Merchant projects require >10% IRR; contracted offtake with H2 price floor can reach bankable 8-10%.

dataPoints.name: DSCR
dataPoints.interpretation: Lenders typically require minimum DSCR of 1.25x; 1.40x target for investment-grade rating.

userInteraction: Structure CAPEX waterfall across electrolyser, compression, storage and distribution
Model H2 offtake price under base ($4/kg), bull ($6/kg) and bear ($2/kg) scenarios
Run IRR sensitivity grid across electricity price ($20-80/MWh) and capacity factor (40-95%)
Size project bond (tenor 15-20yr) and calculate DSCR covenant compliance

acronyms.def: EBITDA divided by total annual debt service; key project finance covenant
Non-electrolyser project components: transformers, cooling, piping, civil works
Total upfront investment cost including contingency and owners costs
Discount rate at which project NPV equals zero

dataLineage.flow: → revenue model
dataLineage.output: $/kg by year and scenario

dataLineage.flow: → LCOH model
dataLineage.output: PPA price and volume profile

/hydrogen-market-intelligence.title: Global Hydrogen Market Intelligence
/hydrogen-market-intelligence.description: Global hydrogen market dashboard tracking production by pathway, demand by sector, country project pipelines and cost trajectory from 2020 to 2050.

calculationEngine.formula: Green share = Green_capacity / Total_H2_capacity × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Industry accounts for 54% of H2 demand, transport 17%, power 10%; green H2 share below 1% in 2023 but scaling rapidly.

dataPoints.name: Global H2 Demand
dataPoints.interpretation: Industrial feedstock (ammonia, refining) dominates at 54%; new demand in transport and power is nascent.

dataPoints.name: Green H2 Project Pipeline
dataPoints.interpretation: Only ~5% of announced projects reach FID; policy certainty and offtake contracts are key gating factors.

dataPoints.name: Cost Trajectory
dataPoints.interpretation: Green H2 cost declines track electrolyser and renewable electricity cost curves; IEA targets <$2/kg by 2030 in high-resource regions.

userInteraction: Map current H2 production by pathway: grey (SMR), blue (SMR+CCS), green (electrolysis), pink (nuclear)
Analyse demand sectors: industry 54%, transport 17%, power 10%, other 19%
Track country project pipelines by stage (announced/FID/construction/operational)
Model cost trajectory 2020-2050 using learning rates and renewable electricity price curves
references: IRENA Green Hydrogen: A Guide to Policy Making 2020

acronyms.def: Dominant grey hydrogen production technology using natural gas
Formal commitment to proceed with project construction and financing
Unit of electrolyser or renewable power capacity
Million metric tonnes; unit for annual hydrogen production volumes

/hydrogen-storage-transport.title: Hydrogen Storage and Transport Economics
/hydrogen-storage-transport.description: Comparative economics of hydrogen storage and transport pathways: compressed gas, liquid H2, liquid organic hydrogen carriers and ammonia, covering cost, energy penalty and pipeline repurposing.

calculationEngine.formula: Total_cost = Storage_cost($/kgH2) + Transport_cost($/kgH2/1000km) × distance
calculationEngine.brief: Ammonia is lowest cost for long-distance shipping (>3,000 km); compressed gas optimal for short-haul (<500 km); pipeline repurposing reduces cost 50-70%.

dataPoints.name: Compressed Gas Storage Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: 700 bar tanks used for transport; 350 bar for stationary; cost scales with pressure rating and tank volume.

dataPoints.name: Liquid H2 Transport Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: Liquefaction energy penalty 25-35% of H2 HHV; boil-off 0.2-0.3%/day adds cost for long voyages.

dataPoints.name: Ammonia Reconversion Efficiency
dataPoints.interpretation: NH3 cracking to H2 adds $0.5-1.5/kgH2; often used directly as fuel/feedstock to avoid reconversion loss.

userInteraction: Compare four storage/transport pathways: compressed gas, liquid H2, LOHC, ammonia
Calculate storage cost ($/kgH2) and transport cost ($/kgH2/1000km) for each pathway
Apply energy penalty and infrastructure CAPEX to derive total delivered cost
Assess pipeline repurposing potential: cost reduction 50-70% vs new hydrogen pipelines

acronyms.def: Chemical hydrogen storage using reversible hydrogenation of organic molecules
Total energy released by combustion including condensation of water vapour
Hydrogen carrier: 17.6 wt% H2; well-established shipping and storage infrastructure
H2 lost through evaporation in cryogenic liquid storage tanks
