/power-to-x-finance.title: Power-to-X Project Finance
/power-to-x-finance.description: Project finance for Power-to-X conversion chains: green H2 to green ammonia, methanol, e-SAF and direct reduced iron, with EU subsidy modelling and carbon credit revenue stacking.

calculationEngine.formula: PtX_LCOP = LCOH × conversion_ratio + CAPEX_converter×CRF/output + OPEX
calculationEngine.brief: Conversion efficiency chain from renewable electricity to final product determines cost competitiveness; carbon credit revenue and EU H2Global subsidies bridge the gap to fossil incumbent prices.

dataPoints.name: Green Ammonia LCOP
dataPoints.interpretation: Green NH3 at $800/t is 2-3× grey NH3 at $250-350/t; requires $400-600/t carbon price or subsidy for parity.

dataPoints.name: e-SAF Production Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: e-SAF is 3-5× conventional jet fuel; EU mandate 2% by 2030, 6% by 2035 creates guaranteed demand.

dataPoints.name: H2Global Subsidy Impact
dataPoints.interpretation: EU H2Global auction mechanism bridges import/offtake price differential; first auctions cleared 2023.

userInteraction: Map full conversion chain efficiency from renewable electricity to PtX product
Model EU H2Global and Innovation Fund subsidy eligibility and auction clearing price
Stack carbon credit revenue (CBAM savings, voluntary carbon market) on top of product revenue
Calculate project IRR with and without subsidy to determine bankability threshold

acronyms.def: Conversion of renewable electricity via hydrogen to synthetic fuels and feedstocks
Synthetic aviation fuel produced from green H2 and captured CO2
Green steel intermediate produced by reducing iron ore with H2 instead of coal
EU carbon price on imports of carbon-intensive goods, creating demand for green feedstocks

dataLineage.flow: → revenue model
dataLineage.output: Clearing price and volume by PtX product

dataLineage.flow: → revenue stacking model
dataLineage.output: VCM and compliance price by year

/power-to-x-finance.valueSummary: Power-to-X economics require EU H2Global subsidies plus carbon credit revenue to reach bankable IRR of 8-10%; e-SAF and green ammonia are the nearest-term bankable PtX applications given EU regulatory mandates through 2035.

/blue-hydrogen-ccs.title: Blue Hydrogen with CCS Economics
/blue-hydrogen-ccs.description: Economic and lifecycle analysis of blue hydrogen production via SMR with carbon capture, covering capture cost, CO2 transport and storage, lifecycle emissions versus grey H2, and US 45Q tax credit valuation.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOH_blue = LCOH_SMR + CCS_cost($/tCO2) × emission_intensity(tCO2/kgH2)
standards: IEA CCUS in Clean Energy Transitions
calculationEngine.brief: CCS adds $0.5-1.5/kgH2 at $60-100/tCO2 capture cost; 45Q credit of $85/tCO2 captured significantly improves US project economics.

dataPoints.name: CO2 Capture Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: Post-combustion amine scrubbing dominates; pre-combustion (IGCC) offers higher capture rates but greater CAPEX.

dataPoints.name: Capture Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: 95%+ capture requires advanced solvents and larger equipment; residual emissions from SMR process gas remain.

dataPoints.name: Lifecycle Emissions vs Grey H2
dataPoints.interpretation: Upstream methane leakage (>2%) can erode blue H2 emission advantage; supply chain integrity critical for lifecycle claim.

userInteraction: Model SMR CAPEX and OPEX as baseline blue H2 cost before CCS addition
Add CCS cost (capture + compression + transport + storage) at $/tCO2 and multiply by capture intensity
Apply US 45Q credit ($85/tCO2 for geological storage) to calculate net economics
Compare lifecycle emissions (well-to-gate) versus grey H2 and green H2 across capture rate scenarios
references: IEA CCUS in Clean Energy Transitions 2021
Global CCS Institute Blue Hydrogen State of the Art 2021
US Treasury 45Q Tax Credit Final Guidance 2023

acronyms.def: Natural gas reforming producing H2 and CO2; primary grey/blue H2 production route
Capture of CO2 at source, transport via pipeline and permanent geological storage
US federal tax credit: $85/tCO2 for geological storage, $60/tCO2 for utilisation
Pre-combustion CCS route with higher capture rates and thermal efficiency

dataLineage.flow: → emissions intensity model
dataLineage.output: tCO2/kgH2 by feedstock quality

dataLineage.flow: → project economics
dataLineage.output: $/kgH2 tax credit value by capture rate

/blue-hydrogen-ccs.valueSummary: Blue hydrogen with 90%+ CCS reduces lifecycle emissions to 3-5 kgCO2e/kgH2 versus 10-12 for grey H2; US 45Q credit at $85/tCO2 reduces blue H2 LCOH by $0.4-0.7/kg, making US projects broadly competitive with grey H2 before 2030.

/green-hydrogen-ammonia-carbon.title: Green Ammonia and Carbon-Neutral Fuels
/green-hydrogen-ammonia-carbon.description: Economics and carbon intensity analysis of green ammonia and carbon-neutral shipping fuels, covering electrified Haber-Bosch, cost benchmarks, IMO 2023 GHG fuel mix targets and shipping fuel comparison.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOP_NH3 = LCOH($/kgH2) / 0.178 + Haber-Bosch_CAPEX×CRF/output + OPEX
standards: Ammonia Energy Association Green NH3 Roadmap
calculationEngine.brief: Green ammonia at 17.6 wt% hydrogen content; electrified Haber-Bosch CAPEX $400-800/tNH3/yr; carbon intensity 0.0 tCO2e/t vs 1.6 for grey ammonia.

dataPoints.name: Green NH3 Production Cost
dataPoints.interpretation: Cost dominated by green H2 input (60-70%); Haber-Bosch plant CAPEX and N2 supply represent 20-30%.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Intensity vs Grey NH3
dataPoints.interpretation: Grey NH3 at 1.6 tCO2e/t is the displacement target; blue NH3 achieves 0.2-0.4 tCO2e/t with 90% CCS.

dataPoints.name: IMO Fuel Mix Compliance
dataPoints.interpretation: IMO 2023 strategy targets 20-30% GHG reduction by 2030, 70-80% by 2040; green ammonia and methanol are primary compliant fuels.

userInteraction: Model green NH3 cost from LCOH input through electrified Haber-Bosch conversion
Compare shipping fuel options: green NH3 vs green methanol vs liquid H2 vs bio-LNG on $/GJ and gCO2e/MJ
Apply IMO 2023 GHG Fuel Intensity (GFI) framework to assess fleet compliance cost
Calculate carbon premium per tonne of cargo for green ammonia vs conventional fuels
references: Ammonia Energy Association Green Ammonia Technology Roadmap 2022
IRENA Innovation Outlook: Electrofuels — Ammonia Chapter 2021

acronyms.def: IMO metric: well-to-wake CO2e emissions per MJ of energy
IMO committee setting GHG and pollution standards for shipping
IMO technical efficiency standard for existing ships in operation
Industrial process combining N2 and H2 to synthesise ammonia at high pressure and temperature

dataLineage.flow: → compliance model
dataLineage.output: GFI by fuel type and ship class

dataLineage.flow: → cost model
dataLineage.output: $/tNH3 by trade route and year

/green-hydrogen-ammonia-carbon.valueSummary: Green ammonia is the leading carbon-neutral shipping fuel candidate under IMO 2023 GHG strategy; at $800/tNH3 it is 2-3× the price of conventional bunker fuel but delivers zero well-to-wake emissions, with cost parity achievable below $500/tNH3 by 2035 per IRENA learning curves.

/bess-project-finance.title: BESS Project Finance
/bess-project-finance.description: 4-hour battery energy storage system project finance covering CAPEX waterfall, multi-stream revenue stacking, degradation modelling to 80% end-of-life and augmentation cost for IRR optimisation.

calculationEngine.formula: Revenue = CM_rev + FFR_rev + Arb_rev; IRR: NPV(Revenue - OPEX - Augment - DebtService) = 0
calculationEngine.brief: Revenue stacking across capacity market, frequency response and energy arbitrage is essential for bankable BESS IRR; degradation to 80% SoH at year 10 triggers augmentation cost.

dataPoints.name: 4-hr BESS CAPEX
dataPoints.formula: System_CAPEX = Cells + BMS + PCS + EPC + O&C
dataPoints.interpretation: Cell cost (40-50% of system) tracking 15% annual decline; target <$150/kWh system cost by 2030 for merchant viability.

dataPoints.name: Revenue Stack ($/kW/yr)
dataPoints.formula: R = CM + FFR + Arb; each stream independently modelled
dataPoints.interpretation: Capacity market provides base revenue; FFR/DCR premium services add $40-80/kW/yr; arbitrage dependent on price spread volatility.

dataPoints.name: Degradation to 80% SoH
dataPoints.interpretation: Calendar ageing + cycle ageing; LFP degrades 10-15% slower than NMC per equivalent cycle count.

userInteraction: Size BESS system (MW/MWh) and calculate CAPEX waterfall: cells, BMS, PCS, EPC, grid connection, owners costs
Model revenue by stream: capacity market (contracted), FFR/DCR (tendered), energy arbitrage (price spread)
Apply degradation model to forecast SoH and schedule augmentation at 80% SoH threshold
Calculate project IRR with and without augmentation cost; test sensitivity to revenue stream pricing
references: National Grid ESO Balancing Mechanism Reports
FERC Order 841 Energy Storage Participation in Markets 2018

acronyms.def: Grid-scale electrochemical energy storage using lithium-ion or alternative battery chemistry
Grid ancillary service responding to frequency deviations within 1 second
Battery capacity as percentage of nameplate; augmentation typically triggered at 80%
Bidirectional inverter converting DC battery to AC grid power

dataLineage.flow: → revenue model
dataLineage.output: £/kW/yr clearing price by delivery year

dataLineage.flow: → CAPEX model
dataLineage.output: $/kWh by chemistry and year

/bess-project-finance.valueSummary: BESS project finance is bankable in markets with stacked revenue streams totalling >$120/kW/yr; CAPEX trajectory to <$200/kWh by 2030 combined with rising capacity market prices improves merchant IRR to 10-14% under BNEF base case assumptions.

/grid-flexibility-markets.title: Grid Flexibility Market Analytics
/grid-flexibility-markets.description: Analytics platform for grid flexibility markets covering frequency response services, reserve markets, capacity market by technology, demand-side response aggregation and flexibility value quantification.

calculationEngine.formula: Value_flex = Σ(service_volume × clearing_price) - Opportunity_cost
standards: National Grid ESO Electricity Market Reform
calculationEngine.brief: Flexibility value varies by market structure; FFR/DCR commands $50-150/MW/hr premium over energy-only dispatch; capacity market provides long-term revenue certainty.

dataPoints.name: FFR Procurement Volume
dataPoints.formula: Procured via competitive tender; volume set by SO
dataPoints.interpretation: GB FFR market cleared at £6-12/MW/hr in 2023; technology-neutral open to BESS, demand response and hydro.

dataPoints.name: Capacity Market Clearing Price
dataPoints.formula: Competitive auction; T-1 and T-4 auctions annually
dataPoints.interpretation: GB T-4 auction 2023 cleared at £63/kW/yr; new build BESS required £75+/kW/yr for FID in most assessments.

dataPoints.name: DSR Aggregation Value
dataPoints.interpretation: Commercial and industrial DSR aggregation delivers 50-200 kW per site; aggregator margins 20-30% of gross revenue.

userInteraction: Map frequency response services by market: FFR/DCR (GB), FCAS (AU), RegUp/RegDown (ERCOT)
Analyse reserve market procurement volumes and clearing prices by technology type
Model capacity market participation for new-build BESS, gas peakers and demand response
Quantify DSR aggregation economics: load shift potential, revenue per MW and aggregator cost structure
references: National Grid ESO Electricity Market Reform Consultation 2023

acronyms.def: Automated response to grid frequency deviation delivered within 1 second
GB grid service: automated frequency response within 1 second to ±0.5 Hz deviation
Flexible electricity demand adjustment by commercial/industrial consumers in response to grid signals
Australian NEM ancillary service framework for frequency regulation and contingency reserves

dataLineage.flow: → revenue model
dataLineage.output: $/MW/hr by service and market

dataLineage.flow: → aggregation model
dataLineage.output: MW available by sector and region

/grid-flexibility-markets.valueSummary: Grid flexibility markets are growing rapidly with >50% BESS share in GB FFR by 2023; DSR aggregation unlocks 10-30 GW of latent flexibility in developed markets, with value of flexibility estimated at $80-200/MW/yr across stacked service streams.

/ldes-investment.title: Long Duration Energy Storage Investment Analytics
/ldes-investment.description: Investment analytics for long duration energy storage technologies including pumped hydro, CAES, vanadium and iron-air flow batteries, hydrogen storage and gravity systems across 4-100 hour duration.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOS = (CAPEX×CRF + OPEX) / (Annual_cycles × Discharged_energy_per_cycle)
standards: BNEF Long-Duration Energy Storage Market Outlook
calculationEngine.brief: LCOS ($/kWh/cycle) enables cross-technology comparison; pumped hydro lowest LCOS for 100hr storage; flow batteries competitive at 8-24hr with declining vanadium electrolyte costs.

dataPoints.name: Pumped Hydro LCOS
dataPoints.interpretation: Lowest LCOS for large-scale, long-life (50yr) projects; constrained by geography; 1,600 GW global installed capacity.

dataPoints.name: Vanadium Flow Battery LCOS
dataPoints.interpretation: Electrolyte retains value (resale/reuse); stack replacement at year 10; scalable capacity independent of power rating.

dataPoints.name: Iron-Air Battery LCOS
dataPoints.formula: Similar to VFB; Fe electrolyte at <$5/kg
dataPoints.interpretation: 100-hour duration with Earth-abundant materials; Form Energy targeting $20/kWh system cost at scale; pilot deployments 2024-2025.

userInteraction: Compare LCOS across technologies at 4hr, 12hr, 24hr, 48hr and 100hr discharge durations
Apply round-trip efficiency (RTE) penalty to calculate effective delivered energy cost
Assess market value of long-duration storage: capacity adequacy, seasonal arbitrage, transmission deferral
Screen investment opportunities by technology readiness level (TRL 6-9) and cost trajectory
references: LDES Council Net-Zero Power: The Clean Energy System 2021
BNEF Long-Duration Energy Storage Market Outlook 2023
NREL Grid-Scale Battery Storage Cost and Performance 2022

acronyms.def: Energy storage systems with discharge duration >4 hours; typically 8-100+ hours
All-in cost per kWh of energy discharged over system lifetime
Ratio of energy output to energy input; pumped hydro 70-85%, flow batteries 65-80%
Energy stored as compressed air in underground caverns; discharge via expansion turbine

dataLineage.flow: → LCOS model
dataLineage.output: CAPEX and OPEX by technology and year

dataLineage.flow: → investment screen
dataLineage.output: $/kW-year value by duration and market

/ldes-investment.valueSummary: Long-duration energy storage is essential for deep decarbonisation with >80% variable renewable penetration; LCOS for 100-hour storage must reach $0.05/kWh/cycle or below for broad economic deployment, a threshold pumped hydro already meets and iron-air targets by 2030.

/battery-tech-supply-chain.title: Battery Supply Chain Risk Analytics
/battery-tech-supply-chain.description: Critical mineral supply chain risk analytics for battery technologies covering lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese and graphite, country concentration risk, price volatility and NMC vs LFP vs solid-state chemistry comparison.

calculationEngine.formula: SCRI = Σ(CRM_weight × HHI_country × price_vol × substitutability_score)
calculationEngine.brief: HHI concentration index applied to country production share; DRC produces 70% of cobalt (HHI ~5,000); lithium 50% Chile+Australia; supply risk highest for cobalt and natural graphite.

dataPoints.name: Cobalt Country Concentration
dataPoints.interpretation: HHI of ~5,000 indicates extreme concentration; DRC political risk rated B- by S&P; ESG concerns in artisanal mining.

dataPoints.name: Lithium Price Volatility
dataPoints.interpretation: LCO/LFP divergence: LFP eliminates cobalt; NMC reduces cobalt 50-80% vs NMC-111; solid-state eliminates liquid electrolyte.

dataPoints.name: Battery Chemistry Comparison
dataPoints.interpretation: LFP dominates stationary storage (<$80/kWh cell); NMC dominates EV long-range; solid-state batteries TRL 5-7, commercial by 2027-2030.

userInteraction: Map critical mineral supply chain by commodity: Li, Co, Ni, Mn, graphite, copper
Calculate HHI concentration index by country for mining, processing and refining stages
Model price volatility and supply disruption scenarios for each critical mineral
Compare NMC 811, LFP and solid-state electrolyte chemistries on cost, energy density and supply risk
references: IEA Critical Minerals Report 2023: Role in Clean Energy Transitions
BNEF Electric Vehicle Outlook 2023 — Battery Technology Chapter
European Commission Critical Raw Materials Act 2023

acronyms.def: Market concentration measure: sum of squared market shares × 10,000; >2,500 = highly concentrated
Li-ion battery cathode chemistry; high energy density; NMC 811 uses 80% Ni, 10% Mn, 10% Co
Li-ion cathode chemistry; lower energy density but higher cycle life, lower cost, no cobalt
EU-designated materials with high economic importance and supply concentration risk

dataLineage.flow: → HHI model
dataLineage.output: Mine output by country and mineral

dataLineage.flow: → chemistry comparison
dataLineage.output: $/kWh by chemistry and year

/battery-tech-supply-chain.valueSummary: Battery supply chain risk is dominated by cobalt (DRC 70%) and natural graphite (China 65%) concentration; LFP chemistry eliminates cobalt risk and is now preferred for stationary storage at <$80/kWh cell cost, while solid-state batteries represent a 2027-2030 transformative shift per IEA CRM analysis.

/ev-v2g-grid-integration.title: EV Vehicle-to-Grid Integration Finance
/ev-v2g-grid-integration.description: Fleet electrification and Vehicle-to-Grid integration finance covering EV fleet cost modelling, V2G grid service revenue, battery degradation cost from V2G cycling, aggregation economics and smart charging optimisation.

calculationEngine.formula: Net_V2G = Grid_service_revenue - Battery_degradation_cost - Aggregation_cost
calculationEngine.brief: V2G battery degradation cost ($0.02-0.08/kWh discharged) must be exceeded by grid service revenue; FFR/DCR service revenue $0.05-0.15/kWh makes V2G marginally positive for high-cycle-life LFP batteries.

dataPoints.name: Fleet Electrification TCO
dataPoints.formula: TCO_EV = CAPEX_EV + Energy + Maintenance - Residual - V2G
dataPoints.interpretation: EV fleet TCO achieves parity with ICE at diesel >$1.20/litre; lower maintenance cost (40% reduction) offset by higher vehicle CAPEX.

dataPoints.name: V2G Grid Service Revenue
dataPoints.interpretation: UK V2G trial achieved £100-200/vehicle/yr in grid services; scales with EV battery capacity and available discharge hours.

dataPoints.name: V2G Battery Degradation
dataPoints.interpretation: LFP batteries show 2-3× lower degradation per cycle than NMC; V2G with LFP adds minimal degradation at <20% DoD discharge.

userInteraction: Model fleet electrification CAPEX, charging infrastructure and TCO versus ICE baseline
Estimate V2G capacity available (kW per vehicle × fleet size × availability factor)
Calculate grid service revenue from FFR, DCR and energy arbitrage using V2G capacity
Net V2G revenue against battery degradation cost and aggregation platform cost to determine net benefit
references: National Grid ESO Vehicle-to-Grid Technology Overview 2022
Ofgem Smart and Flexible Grids Call for Input 2022

acronyms.def: Technology enabling EV batteries to discharge power back to the electricity grid
All-in vehicle cost: purchase, fuel/energy, maintenance, insurance less residual value
Percentage of battery capacity discharged in each cycle; deeper DoD accelerates degradation
Electricity network operator managing local distribution grid including EV charging and V2G

dataLineage.flow: → V2G capacity model
dataLineage.output: kWh and charge/discharge window by vehicle type

dataLineage.flow: → revenue model
dataLineage.output: £/MW/hr by service type and time-of-day

/ev-v2g-grid-integration.valueSummary: V2G is financially positive for LFP-battery EV fleets with >5kW bidirectional capability and access to FFR/DCR markets; net annual V2G revenue of $100-200/vehicle exceeds degradation cost for LFP chemistry, creating a new fleet revenue stream per IEA EV Outlook 2023 projections.

/virtual-power-plant.title: Virtual Power Plant Economics
/virtual-power-plant.description: Virtual Power Plant economics covering aggregated DER dispatch (rooftop solar, BESS, EV, DSR), multi-stream revenue from energy and ancillary markets, aggregation cost modelling and comparison versus gas peaker plants.

calculationEngine.formula: VPP_profit = Σ_t(dispatch_decision_t × (price_t - marginal_cost_t)) - Aggregation_OPEX
calculationEngine.brief: VPP optimises dispatch across energy arbitrage, FFR, reserve and capacity markets simultaneously; ML-based dispatch improves revenue 15-25% over rule-based dispatch per NREL analysis.

dataPoints.name: Aggregated DER Capacity
dataPoints.formula: C_VPP = Σ(C_solar + C_BESS + C_EV + C_DSR) × availability
dataPoints.interpretation: Typical residential VPP: 5kW solar + 10kWh BESS per home; 10,000 homes = 50MW/100MWh aggregate capacity.

dataPoints.name: VPP vs Peaker LCOE
dataPoints.interpretation: VPP cost advantage over gas peakers is confirmed in US, AU and GB markets; 2-hour capacity value critical to comparison.

dataPoints.name: Aggregation Platform Cost
dataPoints.formula: Platform_cost = Software + Comms + Metering + Staff / Annual_MWh
dataPoints.interpretation: Aggregation cost declining with scale; >100MW portfolios achieve $3-5/MWh; key margin lever for VPP operators.

userInteraction: Define VPP portfolio composition: rooftop solar, residential/commercial BESS, EV fleet, industrial DSR
Model dispatch optimisation across energy, FFR, reserve and capacity market revenue streams
Calculate aggregation platform cost (software, communications, metering, customer incentives)
Compare VPP economics versus equivalent gas peaker on LCOE, emissions and response time
references: NREL Virtual Power Plants: Opportunities and Challenges 2023
Rocky Mountain Institute The Economics of Virtual Power Plants 2022

acronyms.def: Aggregation of distributed energy resources managed as a single dispatchable entity
Small-scale generation, storage or flexible load connected to the distribution network
Software platform coordinating dispatch of aggregated distributed energy resources
Australian rule-maker for wholesale electricity and gas markets including VPP frameworks

dataLineage.flow: → VPP capacity model
dataLineage.output: kW and availability by asset type and location

dataLineage.flow: → dispatch optimiser
dataLineage.output: $/MWh by market and 30-min interval

/virtual-power-plant.valueSummary: VPPs are cost-competitive with gas peakers in markets with high renewable penetration and flexible ancillary service markets; aggregated DER portfolios of 100MW+ achieve $3-5/MWh aggregation cost and $80-120/MWh LCOE, 20-40% below new-build gas peaker cost per Rocky Mountain Institute analysis.

/renewable-lca-epd.title: Renewable Energy LCA and EPD Analytics
/renewable-lca-epd.description: Lifecycle assessment and Environmental Product Declaration analytics for renewable energy technologies covering carbon footprint by technology, ISO 14040/44 compliance, carbon payback period and EPD database integration.

calculationEngine.formula: LCA_CI(gCO2e/kWh) = Σ(lifecycle_stage_emissions) / Total_lifetime_generation
standards: JRC Science for Policy: LCA of Electricity Generation
calculationEngine.brief: Carbon footprint: solar PV 20-50 gCO2e/kWh, wind 7-15, nuclear 4-12, gas 400-490, coal 800-1050; system boundary must include manufacturing, O&M, decommissioning and fuel cycle.

dataPoints.name: Solar PV Carbon Intensity
dataPoints.formula: LCA boundary: Si purification through decommissioning
dataPoints.interpretation: Variation driven by grid mix for manufacturing (c-Si monocrystalline lower than polycrystalline); improving with clean manufacturing energy.

dataPoints.name: Wind Carbon Intensity
dataPoints.formula: LCA: steel + concrete + blades + installation + O&M
dataPoints.interpretation: Onshore wind 7-11 gCO2e/kWh; offshore 8-15 gCO2e/kWh due to marine installation and cable; tower material dominates lifecycle impact.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Payback Period
dataPoints.interpretation: Solar PV CPP 1-4yr depending on irradiation and grid mix displaced; wind CPP 0.5-1yr; nuclear CPP 1-2yr including fuel cycle.

userInteraction: Define system boundary per ISO 14040/44: cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave or cradle-to-cradle
Calculate lifecycle GHG intensity for selected technology using NREL or JRC harmonised LCA data
Produce EPD-compliant output under relevant PCR (Product Category Rules) for energy products
Calculate carbon payback period against regional grid emission intensity displaced
references: ISO 14040:2006 Life Cycle Assessment — Principles and Framework
JRC Science for Policy Report: LCA of Electricity Generation Technologies 2021
NREL Harmonised Life Cycle Assessment for Utility-Scale Electricity Generation 2021

acronyms.def: Systematic environmental impact analysis from raw material extraction through end-of-life
ISO 14025 standardised environmental performance report based on LCA
Standardised rules defining LCA methodology for a specific product category
CO2-equivalent metric for greenhouse gas emissions over 100-year horizon per IPCC AR6

dataLineage.flow: → LCA model
dataLineage.output: Background process emissions by region

dataLineage.flow: → technology benchmarks
dataLineage.output: gCO2e/kWh by technology and year

/renewable-lca-epd.valueSummary: Renewable energy lifecycle carbon intensities are 10-100× lower than fossil fuels: solar PV 20-50 gCO2e/kWh, wind 7-15, nuclear 4-12 versus gas 400-490 and coal 800-1050; carbon payback periods of 0.5-4 years confirm strong climate benefit over 25-30 year project lifetimes per NREL and JRC harmonised LCA data.

/sustainability-linked-instruments.title: Sustainability-Linked Instruments Analytics
/sustainability-linked-instruments.description: Analytics platform for Sustainability-Linked Loans, Bonds, Derivatives and Insurance covering SPT calibration, KPI universe, margin ratchet modelling, second-party opinion scoring and ICMA/LMA/ISDA framework compliance.

calculationEngine.formula: SPT_ambition = (Baseline_KPI - Target_KPI) / Baseline_KPI × 100%; benchmark against sector leaders and science-based trajectories
calculationEngine.brief: SPTs must be material, ambitious relative to sector benchmarks and aligned to science-based trajectories; ICMA requires external review confirming ambition level; margin ratchet of 5-50bps is market standard.

dataPoints.name: SPT Ambition Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Ambitious SPTs benchmarked at top 15-25% of sector peers; SBTi alignment adds 1 score point; 2030 interim targets required for long-dated instruments.

dataPoints.name: Margin Ratchet
dataPoints.formula: Rate_adj = ±ratchet_bps if KPI_outcome vs SPT target
dataPoints.interpretation: SLL margin step-up 5-25bps on SPT miss; SLB coupon step-up 25-50bps; asymmetric ratchets (penalty>reward) gaining market acceptance.

dataPoints.name: Second-Party Opinion Score
dataPoints.interpretation: SPO score >75/100 typically required for ESG index inclusion; ISS and Sustainalytics account for 60%+ of global SPO market.

userInteraction: Select 1-5 KPIs from ICMA-recommended universe (500+ metrics) aligned to issuer material ESG topics
Calibrate SPT ambition level against sector science-based trajectory and top-quartile peer performance
Model margin ratchet economics: issuer cost savings on SPT achievement vs penalty on miss
Obtain second-party opinion and structure external verification protocol (annually, by accredited verifier)
references: LMA/APLMA/LSTA Sustainability Linked Loan Principles March 2023

acronyms.def: Loan with interest rate adjusted based on borrower achievement of pre-set sustainability performance targets
Bond with coupon step-up if issuer misses pre-agreed sustainability performance targets at observation dates
Pre-agreed, measurable ESG milestone that triggers coupon/margin adjustment in SL instruments
External review by accredited provider assessing SL framework alignment with ICMA/LMA principles

dataLineage.flow: → SPT selection
dataLineage.output: 500+ metrics by sector and ESG pillar

dataLineage.flow: → ambition benchmarking
dataLineage.output: Target levels and timelines by sector

/sustainability-linked-instruments.valueSummary: Sustainability-linked instruments have grown to >$1.5 trillion outstanding (2023) and require rigorous SPT calibration at sector top-quartile or science-based trajectory level; margin ratchets of 25-50bps on SLBs and 10-25bps on SLLs are market standard, with ICMA 2023 Principles tightening SPT ambition requirements to reduce greenwashing risk.

/nuclear-lcoe-economics.title: Nuclear LCOE Economics
/nuclear-lcoe-economics.description: Levelised cost analysis for large nuclear and SMR technologies covering overnight cost trends, historical overruns, capacity factors, decommissioning provisions and fuel cost components.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOE = (Overnight Cost × FCR + O&M + Fuel + Decom) / (CF × 8760 × Capacity)
standards: IEA Projected Costs of Generating Electricity 2020
calculationEngine.brief: Full lifecycle cost model normalised to $/MWh of generation.

dataPoints.name: Large Nuclear LCOE
dataPoints.interpretation: Benchmark range for new-build large light-water reactors in OECD markets.

dataPoints.name: SMR Projected LCOE
dataPoints.formula: NOAK factory cost × learning rate / (CF × capacity)
dataPoints.interpretation: Projected NOAK range assuming 10–15% learning rate on factory-fabricated units.

dataPoints.name: Capacity Factor
dataPoints.interpretation: Nuclear fleet average CF; highest of any generating technology.

userInteraction: Establish overnight capital cost estimate using Vogtle/Hinkley reference class data
Apply fixed charge rate to annualise capital cost
Add O&M, fuel cycle and decommissioning provision components
Compute LCOE and benchmark against SMR and large-nuclear ranges
references: IEA Projected Costs of Generating Electricity (2020)
World Nuclear Association — Economics of Nuclear Power
NEA — Unlocking Reductions in the Construction Costs of Nuclear

acronyms.def: All-in cost per MWh over plant lifetime
Annualisation factor applied to overnight capital cost
Reactor design ≤300 MWe using factory fabrication
Ratio of actual to maximum possible generation

dataLineage.flow: Overnight cost → FCR → annualised capital
dataLineage.output: LCOE $/MWh by technology

/nuclear-lcoe-economics.valueSummary: Nuclear LCOE ranges from $90–$160/MWh for large LWRs; SMRs target $60–$100/MWh at NOAK scale driven by 10–15% factory-learning rates and 90–95% capacity factors.

/smr-project-finance.title: SMR Project Finance
/smr-project-finance.description: Project finance structuring for small modular reactors covering factory fabrication economics, FOAK-to-NOAK learning, regulatory pathways across NRC/ONR/CNSC, IRA production tax credits and government guarantee structures.

calculationEngine.formula: NOAK Cost = FOAK Cost × N^(log(1−LR)/log(2))
calculationEngine.brief: Wright's Law applied to nth-of-a-kind factory unit cost reduction.

dataPoints.name: FOAK-to-NOAK Learning Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated cost reduction per doubling of cumulative factory output.

dataPoints.name: IRA Production Tax Credit
dataPoints.interpretation: Advanced nuclear PTC under Inflation Reduction Act for facilities commencing construction pre-2033.

dataPoints.name: DOE Loan Guarantee Coverage
dataPoints.formula: Guaranteed Debt = Total Project Cost × Coverage Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Title XVII loan guarantee programme for innovative clean energy projects.

userInteraction: Assess FOAK capital cost envelope and learning-rate pathway to NOAK
Map regulatory pathway (NRC DC/COL, ONR GDA, CNSC pre-licensing)

/nuclear-decommissioning.title: Nuclear Decommissioning Finance
/nuclear-decommissioning.description: Financial analytics for nuclear decommissioning covering unit cost ranges, segregated fund adequacy, cost escalation drivers, SAFDM vs DECON strategy comparison and NDF/NDA fund performance.

calculationEngine.formula: FAR = PV(Fund Assets) / PV(Estimated Decommissioning Cost)
calculationEngine.brief: Ratio of discounted fund assets to discounted liabilities; regulatory minimum typically 100%.

dataPoints.name: Unit Decommissioning Cost
dataPoints.formula: DCost = Labour + Waste Disposal + Engineering + Contingency
dataPoints.source: NDA UK / NRC US Cost Studies
dataPoints.interpretation: Wide range reflects reactor type, site complexity, waste classification and national labour costs.

dataPoints.name: Fund Adequacy Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Regulatory benchmark; sub-100% funds require remediation plans.

dataPoints.name: Cost Escalation Factor
dataPoints.formula: Escalation = Labour CPI × 0.6 + Waste Disposal Inflation × 0.4
dataPoints.interpretation: Labour and low-level waste disposal dominate escalation above general inflation.

userInteraction: Estimate total decommissioning liability using reference-unit cost benchmarks

PV of fund assets divided by PV of decommissioning liability

/nuclear-fuel-cycle.title: Nuclear Fuel Cycle Economics

calculationEngine.formula: FCC = (U₃O₈ + Conversion + Enrichment_SWU + Fabrication + Used_Fuel_Mgmt) / (BU × Capacity)
calculationEngine.brief: Sum of front-end and back-end per-MWh costs at given burnup.

dataPoints.interpretation: High-assay low-enriched uranium (5–20% U-235) required for many advanced reactor designs.

/advanced-reactor-finance.title: Advanced Reactor Finance

dataPoints.interpretation: Wide uncertainty reflects pre-FOAK status; shrinks with ARDP demonstration results.

/geothermal-lcoe-economics.title: Geothermal LCOE Economics
/geothermal-lcoe-economics.description: Levelised cost analysis for geothermal power covering hydrothermal vs EGS technologies, exploration dry-well risk, drilling cost by depth, reservoir productivity, flash/binary/dry-steam plant types and capacity factor benchmarks.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOE = (Exploration + Drilling + Surface Plant + O&M) / (CF × 8760 × Capacity)
calculationEngine.brief: Full lifecycle cost model including dry-well exploration write-off and reservoir decline provisions.

dataPoints.name: Geothermal LCOE Range
dataPoints.formula: LCOE = Annualised Total Cost / Annual Generation
dataPoints.interpretation: Hydrothermal $40–$70/MWh; EGS $80–$100/MWh reflecting higher drilling and stimulation costs.

dataPoints.name: Exploration Dry-Well Cost
dataPoints.formula: Dry-Well Cost = Drilling Day Rate × Depth + Mobilisation
dataPoints.interpretation: Key risk factor; dry-well probability 30–50% in frontier areas drives project financing structure.

dataPoints.name: Capacity Factor
dataPoints.interpretation: Among the highest of any renewable technology; reservoir management critical to maintaining output.

userInteraction: Assess resource quality via temperature gradient and permeability data
Model drilling programme cost by well count, depth and dry-well probability
Select plant technology (dry-steam, flash, binary ORC) matching resource enthalpy

dataPoints.interpretation: Geothermal direct use avoids electricity conversion losses entirely; no parasitic load beyond pumping.

/enhanced-geothermal-systems.title: EGS Project Analytics

calculationEngine.formula: LCOE_EGS(t) = Base_LCOE × (1 − LR)^(log₂(Cumulative_Wells(t)))
standards: MIT — The Future of Geothermal Energy 2006

dataPoints.name: Flow Rate Target
dataPoints.formula: Flow Rate = Reservoir Permeability × ΔP / Fluid Viscosity
dataPoints.interpretation: Minimum commercial threshold; FORGE achieving 5–8 kg/s at Utah site as of 2023.

dataPoints.name: Temperature Target
dataPoints.formula: T_reservoir = Surface Temperature + Geothermal Gradient × Depth
dataPoints.interpretation: Minimum reservoir temperature for viable flash or binary power generation.

dataPoints.name: EGS LCOE Target
dataPoints.formula: Target = DOE EGS Shot Learning Curve Projection
dataPoints.interpretation: Requires 90% reduction in stimulation and drilling costs from current $100–$200/MWh baseline.

Design hydraulic stimulation programme with induced seismicity monitoring protocol

/fi-net-zero-pathways.title: FI Net-Zero Pathway Analytics

calculationEngine.formula: PDR = (Baseline Emissions − Current Emissions) / Baseline Emissions × 100
standards: NZBA — Guidelines for Climate Target Setting for Banks 2021

dataPoints.name: PCAF Data Quality Score
dataPoints.formula: DQ = Weighted average of holding-level data quality scores
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher DQ scores indicate proxy/estimation-based emissions; DQ1 uses audited primary data.

dataPoints.source: MSCI Climate Value-at-Risk / SBTI SBTi Tool

dataPoints.interpretation: Power generation SC criterion for climate change mitigation; applies to solar, wind, hydro, nuclear and gas with CCS.

dataPoints.interpretation: Nuclear classified as transitional under separate Complementary Delegated Act; eligible until 2045 for existing, 2040 for new.

/green-securitization.description: Green securitisation analytics covering green ABS (solar/EV/PACE), green RMBS (energy-efficient mortgages), green CLO (green loans), use-of-proceeds waterfall, SPV structuring, greenium quantification and EU Green Bond Standard applicability.

calculationEngine.formula: Greenium = YTM_Conventional_Comparable − YTM_Green_Equivalent (bps)
standards: EBA — Discussion Paper on Green Bonds (2020)
calculationEngine.brief: Yield differential between green and conventional ABS/RMBS/CLO tranches with matched risk profiles.

dataPoints.name: Greenium (Green Premium)
dataPoints.formula: Greenium = YTM_Conv − YTM_Green on matched-maturity, same-credit tranches
dataPoints.source: ICMA STS Green ABS / Bloomberg data
dataPoints.interpretation: Investor demand premium for labelled green securitisations; varies by asset class and ESG mandate penetration.

dataPoints.name: Solar ABS Green Eligibility
dataPoints.formula: Eligibility = Loan Purpose × Taxonomy SC Check
dataPoints.interpretation: Solar consumer loans and leases are core eligible asset class; EU GBS requires Taxonomy alignment.

dataPoints.name: EV Lease ABS Green Eligibility
dataPoints.interpretation: BEV and FCEV lease receivables fully eligible; PHEV eligibility dependent on utility factor methodology.

userInteraction: Screen underlying asset pool for green eligibility by asset class (solar/EV/PACE/mortgages/green loans)
Structure SPV with use-of-proceeds waterfall separating green and non-green tranches
Apply ICMA GBP or EU GBS framework for second-party opinion and pre-issuance disclosure
Quantify expected greenium and model impact on all-in financing cost vs conventional securitisation
references: EBA — Discussion Paper on the Role of Environmental Risks in the Prudential Framework (2020)
SFA — ESG in Securitisation Market Practice Guidelines (2022)

acronyms.def: Securitisation backed by consumer loans, leases or other receivables
Securitisation backed by residential mortgage loans
Securitisation backed by a portfolio of leveraged or green corporate loans
US financing mechanism for energy efficiency and renewable improvements repaid via property tax
Bankruptcy-remote legal entity holding securitised assets
EU regulatory framework for verified green bonds aligned to EU Taxonomy

dataLineage.source: Loan-level asset pool data + ICMA/EU GBS eligibility criteria
dataLineage.flow: Use-of-proceeds waterfall → greenium pricing model
dataLineage.output: Green securitisation structuring and analytics dashboard

/green-securitization.valueSummary: Green securitisation commands a 5–10 bps greenium across solar ABS, EV lease ABS and energy-efficient RMBS; EU Green Bond Standard applicability requires full EU Taxonomy alignment of the underlying asset pool, raising the bar versus ICMA GBP self-labelling.

/blended-finance-structuring.title: Blended Finance Structuring Analytics
/blended-finance-structuring.description: Blended finance structuring analytics covering first-loss tranche sizing, concessional capital catalytic ratio, DFI/MDB co-investment structures, and OECD DAC blended finance taxonomy. Quantifies the leverage ratio of private capital mobilised per unit of public/concessional capital deployed.

calculationEngine.formula: Leverage Ratio = Private Capital Mobilised / Concessional Capital Deployed; First-Loss Buffer = Expected Loss × (1 + Stress Factor)
calculationEngine.brief: Measures private capital catalysed per dollar of concessional/public capital using tranche waterfall and expected loss allocation

dataPoints.name: Catalytic Leverage Ratio
dataPoints.formula: Private capital mobilised / concessional capital deployed
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher ratios indicate greater mobilisation efficiency; OECD target >3x for emerging markets

dataPoints.name: First-Loss Tranche Size
dataPoints.formula: Expected portfolio loss × stress multiplier / total facility size
dataPoints.interpretation: Absorbs initial losses protecting senior private investors; typically 8-20% of facility

dataPoints.name: Concessional Capital Share
dataPoints.formula: DFI/MDB concessional tranche / total capital stack
dataPoints.interpretation: Share above 25% may crowd out private capital; below 10% may be insufficient to de-risk

userInteraction: Define blended finance objective (SDG/climate alignment, sector, geography)
Size concessional/first-loss tranches using expected loss and stress scenarios
Model private capital leverage ratio under base, optimistic, and stress assumptions
Apply OECD DAC taxonomy classification and generate term sheet summary
references: OECD (2023) Blended Finance Principles for Unlocking Commercial Finance for the SDGs
CONVERGENCE (2023) State of Blended Finance Report
G20 Sustainable Finance Working Group — MDB Capital Adequacy Framework 2022
World Bank (2023) Maximising Finance for Development — Cascade Approach

acronyms.def: Government-backed institution providing finance to private sector in developing countries
International institution providing development loans, e.g. World Bank, ADB, AfDB
OECD committee setting blended finance taxonomy and ODA reporting standards
Ring-fenced legal entity used to hold blended finance assets and tranches

dataLineage.flow: Historical deal terms → leverage ratio benchmarks by sector and region
dataLineage.output: Peer comparison for tranche sizing

dataLineage.flow: Concessional pricing, tenor, grace period → waterfall model inputs
dataLineage.output: First-loss and mezzanine tranche calibration

dataLineage.flow: Activity classification codes → concessional finance eligibility flags
dataLineage.output: ODA attribution and reporting output

/blended-finance-structuring.valueSummary: Quantifies private capital leverage, optimal tranche sizing, and OECD DAC taxonomy classification for blended finance structures targeting emerging-market climate and SDG investments.

/transition-finance-engine.title: Transition Finance Credibility Engine
/transition-finance-engine.description: Transition finance credibility engine for high-emitting sectors including steel, cement, shipping, aviation, and oil & gas. Assesses transition taxonomy eligibility, Paris-aligned capex share, and transition assessment readiness score against the ICMA Climate Transition Finance Handbook.

calculationEngine.formula: TA-Score = 0.3×Strategy + 0.25×CapexAlignment + 0.25×Targets + 0.2×Governance; Paris CapEx Share = Climate CapEx / Total CapEx
standards: EU Platform on Sustainable Finance — Transition Finance Report
Science Based Targets initiative Corporate Net-Zero Standard
calculationEngine.brief: Composite scoring of strategic ambition, Paris-aligned capital allocation, science-based target quality, and governance for transition credibility assessment

dataPoints.name: Transition Assessment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores above 70 indicate credible transition; 50-70 requires monitoring; below 50 raises greenwashing risk

dataPoints.name: Paris-Aligned CapEx Share
dataPoints.interpretation: IEA NZE scenario requires >50% green capex share by 2025 for high-emitters; current gap quantified

dataPoints.name: Scope 1+2 Intensity Trajectory
dataPoints.interpretation: Must align with sector decarbonisation pathway; steel SBTi pathway requires -4.2%/yr

userInteraction: Classify sector and identify applicable transition pathway (SBTi, IEA, EU TEG sector)
Score transition strategy, capex alignment, targets, and governance using ICMA four-pillar framework
Map Paris-aligned capex share and intensity trajectory against benchmark decarbonisation pathway
Generate transition finance eligibility determination and issuer TA-Score with peer comparison
references: IEA (2023) Net Zero by 2050 — Sector Transition Roadmaps
EU Platform on Sustainable Finance (2022) Transition Finance Report

acronyms.def: ICMA four-pillar framework evaluating strategic, capex, target, and governance dimensions of transition credibility
Initiative validating corporate emissions reduction targets consistent with Paris Agreement
IMO operational efficiency metric for shipping expressed as gCO2/tonne-nautical mile
Investment in long-term assets; Paris-aligned share measures green vs brown capex allocation

dataLineage.flow: CapEx breakdown, Scope 1+2 data, net-zero target details → scoring inputs
dataLineage.output: ICMA four-pillar TA-Score

dataLineage.flow: Approved targets by sector → Paris-alignment benchmark trajectory
dataLineage.output: CapEx gap analysis vs pathway

dataLineage.flow: Technology deployment milestones and investment requirements → credibility benchmarks
dataLineage.output: Sector-specific eligibility determination

/capital-markets-taxonomy.title: Capital Markets EU Taxonomy Analytics
/capital-markets-taxonomy.description: EU Taxonomy alignment analytics for capital markets products including equity funds, bond indices, and ETFs. Calculates taxonomy-aligned revenue, capex, and opex by portfolio holding, computes GAR and BTAR metrics, and assesses Article 8/9 SFDR eligibility.

calculationEngine.formula: Fund Taxonomy Alignment = Σ(Holding Weight × Holding Taxonomy %) ; GAR = Taxonomy-Aligned Exposures / Total Covered Assets
standards: EBA Green Asset Ratio Delegated Act 2022
calculationEngine.brief: Weighted-average taxonomy alignment across holdings using revenue/capex/opex metrics, aggregated to fund level for disclosure

dataPoints.name: Fund Taxonomy Alignment (Revenue)
dataPoints.source: EU Taxonomy company disclosures / Clarity AI
dataPoints.interpretation: SFDR Art 9 funds target >80%; Art 8 funds typically 15-40%; regulatory minimum in PAIS template

dataPoints.name: Green Asset Ratio
dataPoints.interpretation: Banks must disclose GAR; target trajectory to 2030 set in transition plans; sector leaders >20%

dataPoints.name: DNSH Pass Rate
dataPoints.formula: Holdings passing all DNSH criteria / holdings screened
dataPoints.interpretation: Measures portfolio quality beyond headline alignment; low rate signals hidden ESG risks

userInteraction: Map portfolio holdings to EU Taxonomy NACE activity codes and climate objective eligibility
Retrieve revenue/capex/opex taxonomy alignment % from company disclosures or estimation models
Calculate weighted fund-level alignment and GAR/BTAR metrics
Assess SFDR Article 8/9 eligibility and generate PAI template inputs
references: European Commission (2022) Delegated Acts — Climate and Environmental Taxonomy
ESMA (2023) Guidelines on Funds Names Using ESG or Sustainability Factors
EBA (2022) Green Asset Ratio ITS and Reporting Templates

acronyms.def: EBA metric: taxonomy-aligned exposures as % of total covered banking book assets
Broader version of GAR including all counterparties subject to NFRD/CSRD
EU Taxonomy criterion requiring activities not to significantly harm any of six environmental objectives
EU standard industrial classification system used to map activities to Taxonomy eligibility

dataLineage.output: Fund-level weighted alignment metrics

dataLineage.source: MSCI / Sustainalytics / Clarity AI taxonomy data
dataLineage.flow: Estimated alignment for non-disclosing companies → gap filling for portfolio coverage
dataLineage.output: PAIS template and GAR inputs

dataLineage.output: Regulatory disclosure outputs

/taxonomy-ml-classifier.title: Taxonomy ML Classifier
/taxonomy-ml-classifier.description: ML-powered EU Taxonomy NACE activity classifier that maps NACE codes to eligible Taxonomy activities, automates DNSH screening criteria checking, and scores substantial contribution threshold compliance. Trained on the EU Taxonomy Compass dataset covering 67 climate mitigation activities across 6 environmental objectives.

calculationEngine.formula: eligibility_score = P(eligible | NACE_code) × SC_threshold_score × DNSH_pass_rate
calculationEngine.brief: A gradient-boosted classifier trained on the EU Taxonomy Compass maps NACE 4-digit codes to eligible activities with a probability score. Substantial contribution (SC) scores are calculated against quantitative thresholds (e.g., GHG intensity <100 gCO2e/kWh for electricity generation). DNSH screening checks the six DNSH criteria for each activity against available data, flagging gaps where evidence is insufficient.

dataPoints.name: Taxonomy Eligibility Rate
dataPoints.source: EU Taxonomy Compass + company NACE codes
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of revenues from NACE activities that are listed in the Taxonomy; does not imply alignment.

dataPoints.name: Taxonomy Alignment Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Subset of eligible revenue that also meets SC thresholds and passes all DNSH checks; the key reported KPI under SFDR and Taxonomy regulation.

dataPoints.name: DNSH Pass Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of eligible activities passing all 6 DNSH criteria; low rates indicate environmental risk exposure in the portfolio.

userInteraction: Upload company NACE codes or connect company database
Run ML classifier to map activities to Taxonomy eligibility
Review DNSH screening results and substantial contribution scores
Export Taxonomy KPI report (eligibility %, alignment %, CapEx/OpEx split)
references: Climate Delegated Act (EU) 2021/2139 – Technical Screening Criteria
EU Taxonomy Compass – eligible activities database

acronyms.def: EU Taxonomy criterion: an economic activity must make a substantial positive contribution to at least one environmental objective
Detailed EU Taxonomy thresholds and conditions that define substantial contribution and DNSH for each eligible activity
EU statistical classification of economic activities used as the basis for EU Taxonomy activity mapping

dataLineage.source: Company NACE codes → EU Taxonomy Compass activity list
dataLineage.flow: ML classifier → SC threshold scoring → DNSH criteria check
dataLineage.output: Taxonomy KPIs: eligibility %, alignment %, CapEx/OpEx breakdown

/taxonomy-ml-classifier.valueSummary: Used by portfolio managers, sustainability controllers, and SFDR/Taxonomy report authors to automate the complex NACE-to-activity mapping process and generate audit-ready Taxonomy alignment KPIs.

/esrs-datapoint-navigator.title: ESRS Datapoint Navigator
/esrs-datapoint-navigator.description: Comprehensive navigator for all 1,144 ESRS mandatory and voluntary datapoints across E1-E5, S1-S4, and G1 topic standards, mapped to materiality assessment outcomes, data collection complexity ratings, and phased reporting requirements. Supports first-year CSRD reporters in scoping their disclosure obligations under EU Delegated Regulation 2023/2772.

calculationEngine.formula: obligation_weight = mandatory_flag × materiality_relevance × phase-in_discount
calculationEngine.brief: Each of the 1,144 datapoints is classified as mandatory irrespective of materiality, mandatory if material, or voluntary. Materiality relevance links each datapoint to the IRO sub-topics assessed in the double materiality assessment. Phase-in discounts reflect the 3-year grace period for certain datapoints under Article 37 of the Delegated Regulation.

dataPoints.name: Mandatory Datapoints in Scope
dataPoints.formula: COUNT(datapoints | materiality_flag = TRUE OR always_mandatory = TRUE)
dataPoints.interpretation: Defines minimum reporting scope; typical large-cap scope is 300–600 mandatory datapoints in year one.

dataPoints.name: Data Collection Complexity Score
dataPoints.formula: weighted average of collection_effort across in-scope datapoints
dataPoints.interpretation: Score >3.5 indicates significant new data infrastructure investment required; used to prioritise gap remediation.

dataPoints.name: Phase-In Coverage Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Tracks progress against the 3-year phase-in plan; <50% in year 2 signals collection risk.

userInteraction: Run double materiality assessment to determine material topics
Filter navigator to in-scope ESRS topics and mandatory datapoints
Assess data collection complexity and identify gaps
Generate phased reporting roadmap and data collection plan

acronyms.def: EU mandatory sustainability reporting standards adopted under CSRD
EU Taxonomy and ESRS principle requiring activities not harm other environmental objectives
CSRD-required process assessing both financial and impact materiality of ESG topics

dataLineage.source: EFRAG ESRS Set 1 Annex → datapoint list
dataLineage.flow: DMA outcome → obligation filter → phase-in schedule mapping
dataLineage.output: Scoped disclosure obligation register with collection complexity ratings

/esrs-datapoint-navigator.valueSummary: Used by CSRD reporting managers and external auditors to scope disclosure obligations, plan data collection programmes, and track ESRS compliance readiness.

/carbon-integrity-mrv-analytics.title: Carbon Credit MRV Analytics
/carbon-integrity-mrv-analytics.description: Carbon credit MRV (Monitoring, Reporting, Verification) analytics covering Verra VCS, Gold Standard, and ACR methodology compliance scoring, additionality assessment, permanence risk quantification, and buffer pool requirements.

calculationEngine.formula: MRV Score = 0.3×Monitoring + 0.3×Reporting + 0.25×Verification + 0.15×Additionality; Buffer Contribution = Permanence Risk × Total Credits
standards: Gold Standard for the Global Goals v3.0
calculationEngine.brief: Multi-dimensional MRV quality score combining monitoring plan rigour, reporting completeness, third-party verification quality, and additionality demonstration strength

dataPoints.name: MRV Compliance Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores above 80 qualify for premium registry listing; 65-80 standard; below 65 remediation required before registration

dataPoints.name: Additionality Score
dataPoints.formula: Performance test + barrier analysis + common practice assessment
dataPoints.interpretation: Regulatory additionality (score >85), financial additionality (IRR without credits < hurdle), common practice (<5% sector penetration)

dataPoints.name: Buffer Pool Contribution
dataPoints.formula: Permanence risk score × total estimated credits
dataPoints.interpretation: Reversal buffer protects buyers; higher risk projects (REDD+) contribute 10-20%; permanence projects <5%

userInteraction: Input project type, methodology, monitoring plan, and verification body qualifications
Score monitoring plan against methodology-specific requirements (frequency, equipment, sampling)
Assess additionality using regulatory, financial barrier, and common practice tests
Calculate permanence risk, buffer pool contribution, and net credits available for issuance
references: Verra (2022) VCS Standard v4.0 and AFOLU Requirements
Gold Standard (2022) Gold Standard for the Global Goals Principles and Requirements
American Carbon Registry (2023) ACR Standard v15
ICROA (2023) Code of Best Practice for Voluntary Carbon Markets

acronyms.def: System for measuring GHG reductions/removals, reporting them, and having them independently verified
Verra-administered voluntary carbon market standard; largest registry by volume

acronyms.full: Agriculture, Forestry and Other Land Use
acronyms.def: VCS project category covering land-based carbon projects with specific permanence and leakage rules
Winrock-administered voluntary and compliance carbon registry operating since 1996

dataLineage.flow: Baseline scenario, monitoring plan, additionality arguments → MRV scoring inputs
dataLineage.output: Compliance gap analysis

dataLineage.flow: Independent verification findings → verification sub-score
dataLineage.output: MRV overall score

dataLineage.flow: Historical buffer contributions and reversal events → permanence risk calibration
dataLineage.output: Buffer pool requirement

/carbon-integrity-mrv-analytics.valueSummary: Provides rigorous MRV quality scoring and additionality assessment for voluntary carbon credits, enabling buyers to distinguish high-integrity credits and sellers to identify compliance gaps.

/ccts-offset-registration.title: Carbon Credit Registry & Offset Registration Analytics
/ccts-offset-registration.description: Carbon credit registry and offset registration analytics comparing Verra, Gold Standard, CAR, and ACR registries. Covers serialisation standards, retirement tracking, double-counting prevention, and ICROA Code of Best Practice compliance.

calculationEngine.formula: Registry Score = 0.25×Serialisation + 0.25×Retirement + 0.25×DoubleCount + 0.25×Transparency; Double-Count Risk = 1 - Σ(exclusive_registry_flags)
standards: ICROA Code of Best Practice v2.0
calculationEngine.brief: Assesses registry-level and transaction-level integrity across serialisation uniqueness, retirement permanence, double-counting controls, and public transparency

dataPoints.name: Registry Integrity Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Verra and Gold Standard score 80-90; newer registries 60-75; ICVCM CCP label requires >75

dataPoints.name: Double-Counting Risk Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Risks arise from corresponding adjustments absence (Article 6.2), bilateral use, and re-issuance across registries

dataPoints.name: Retirement Integrity Rate
dataPoints.formula: Credits with confirmed irreversible retirement / total retired credits
dataPoints.interpretation: Near 100% required; gaps indicate potential re-use; Verra public retirement database enables verification

userInteraction: Map credit serialisation structure across Verra, Gold Standard, CAR, ACR, and Climate Action Reserve
Check cross-registry uniqueness and corresponding adjustment status for host country credits
Verify retirement records are publicly accessible, irreversible, and vintage-matched to claimed reductions
Score registry against ICROA Code of Best Practice and ICVCM Core Carbon Principles
references: ICROA (2023) Code of Best Practice for Voluntary Carbon Markets v2.0
ICVCM (2023) Core Carbon Principles Assessment Framework

acronyms.def: North American carbon registry operating in voluntary and compliance markets with focus on US-based projects

acronyms.full: Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market
acronyms.def: Governance body setting Core Carbon Principles for high-integrity credit label
Demand-side initiative setting corporate claims standards for voluntary carbon market use
ICVCM-defined quality threshold for voluntary carbon credits covering governance, emissions impact, and sustainable development

dataLineage.source: Registry serialisation databases (Verra, GSF, CAR, ACR)
dataLineage.flow: Serial number ranges, project IDs, vintage, retirement records → integrity checks
dataLineage.output: Double-counting and retirement integrity scores

dataLineage.flow: Host country authorisation and adjustment records → sovereign double-counting risk
dataLineage.output: Article 6 compliance flag

dataLineage.output: Registry integrity ranking

/carbon-removal-markets.title: Carbon Removal Market Analytics
/carbon-removal-markets.description: Carbon removal market analytics covering DAC, BECCS, enhanced weathering, biochar, soil carbon, and ocean alkalinity enhancement. Models cost curves (DAC $300-1000/t, biochar $50-200/t), permanence tiers, and CDR procurement platform dynamics.

calculationEngine.formula: Levelised Cost of CDR = (CAPEX × CRF + OPEX) / Annual CO2 Removed; Permanence-Adjusted Price = Spot Price × (1 / (1 - Reversal Risk))
standards: Oxford Principles for Net Zero Aligned Carbon Offsetting
calculationEngine.brief: Cost curve analysis across CDR pathways weighted by permanence tier, scalability potential, and co-benefit profile

dataPoints.name: Weighted Average CDR Cost
dataPoints.formula: (Σ volume_i × cost_i) / Σ volume_i across portfolio
dataPoints.interpretation: Blended cost depends heavily on pathway mix; pure DAC portfolios $400-800/t; biochar-weighted $80-150/t

dataPoints.name: Permanence-Adjusted Supply
dataPoints.interpretation: Permanence factors: geological (1.0), biochar (0.85), enhanced weathering (0.90), soil carbon (0.60)

dataPoints.name: Pipeline Growth Rate
dataPoints.formula: (Current year pipeline - prior year) / prior year × 100
dataPoints.interpretation: CDR market growing rapidly; advance purchase commitments critical for scale-up financing

userInteraction: Map CDR pathway portfolio across geological, biological, and ocean-based removal categories
Build cost curves using technology-specific CAPEX/OPEX and capacity data
Apply permanence tier discounting to compute permanence-adjusted carbon value
Model procurement platform supply/demand dynamics and forward price scenarios
references: IPCC AR6 WG3 (2022) Chapter 12 — Cross-sectoral Perspectives on Carbon Dioxide Removal
Oxford Principles for Net Zero Aligned Carbon Offsetting (2024 revision)
Carbon180 (2023) CDR State of the Market Report
Frontier Climate (2023) Offtake and Advance Purchase Framework

acronyms.def: Technology mechanically capturing CO2 from ambient air; highest cost but highest permanence CDR pathway

acronyms.full: Bioenergy with Carbon Capture and Storage
acronyms.def: Combines biomass energy generation with geological CO2 storage for net-negative emissions
Deliberate removal of CO2 from atmosphere through biological, chemical, or geological processes
Spreading crushed silicate rock on agricultural land to accelerate natural CO2 mineralisation

dataLineage.flow: Project capacity, cost, permanence, and vintage data → cost curve inputs
dataLineage.output: Pathway-level cost and supply analytics

dataLineage.source: Frontier / Stripe / Microsoft advance purchase data
dataLineage.flow: Forward commitment volumes and prices → demand signal and price discovery
dataLineage.output: Forward price curve and market depth

dataLineage.flow: Technical and sustainable CDR potential by pathway → scalability ceiling analysis
dataLineage.output: Market ceiling and pathway mix optimisation

/carbon-removal-markets.valueSummary: Provides comprehensive CDR market analytics enabling procurement optimisation across pathways, with permanence-adjusted pricing and cost curve visibility for corporate net-zero buyers.

/beccs-project-finance.title: BECCS Project Finance Analytics
/beccs-project-finance.description: BECCS project finance analytics covering negative emissions credit revenue, CCS CAPEX/OPEX, biomass supply chain risk, 45Q tax credit (USA), and EU ETS interaction for carbon-negative bioenergy projects.

calculationEngine.methodology: BECCS Levelised Cost of Negative Emissions
calculationEngine.formula: LCONE = (CAPEX × CRF + OPEX - Electricity Revenue - 45Q) / Annual Net CO2 Negative Emissions; Net Emissions = CO2 Stored - Biomass Supply Chain Emissions
standards: IEA BECCS in Clean Energy Transitions 2021
calculationEngine.brief: Levelised cost of negative emissions accounting for energy revenue, policy credits, and full supply chain carbon accounting

dataPoints.name: LCONE
dataPoints.formula: (CAPEX×CRF + OPEX - Energy Rev - 45Q) / Net CO2 negative
dataPoints.interpretation: Current LCONE $80-200/tCO2; 45Q credit ($85-180/t) can make projects cash-positive; EU ETS drives additional revenue

dataPoints.name: 45Q Tax Credit Value
dataPoints.formula: IRA 2022 enhanced 45Q for captured and geologically stored CO2
dataPoints.interpretation: IRA raised 45Q from $50 to $85/t geological; $60/t utilisation; direct pay option available for 5 years

dataPoints.name: Biomass Supply Chain Emissions
dataPoints.formula: Well-to-gate lifecycle emissions per MWh biomass input
dataPoints.interpretation: Must be below 70% GHG saving vs fossil baseline per RED II; typically 0.1-0.3 tCO2/MWh for sustainable sources

userInteraction: Model BECCS plant CAPEX (CCS component $800-1,200/kW additional) and OPEX structure
Calculate 45Q tax credit eligibility and IRA direct pay option value over project life
Assess biomass supply chain GHG intensity against RED II sustainability criteria
Compute LCONE sensitivity to CO2 storage capacity, electricity price, and carbon credit prices
references: IEA (2021) BECCS in Clean Energy Transitions
US IRS (2023) 45Q Credit for Carbon Oxide Sequestration — IRA Updated Guidance
EU RED II Directive 2018/2001 — Biomass Sustainability Criteria
Global CCS Institute (2023) State of the CCS Industry Report

acronyms.def: Cost metric for CDR pathways equivalent to LCOE for power generation; $/tCO2 net removed
Technology capturing CO2 from industrial sources or atmosphere and storing geologically
EU directive setting sustainability criteria for bioenergy including lifecycle GHG thresholds
Annualisation factor converting upfront CAPEX to annual cost based on discount rate and project life

dataLineage.flow: Technology cost assumptions by feedstock and CCS type → CAPEX/OPEX model inputs
dataLineage.output: LCONE calculation

dataLineage.source: US IRS 45Q guidance and IRA text
dataLineage.flow: Credit rates, eligible technologies, direct pay rules → tax credit revenue stream
dataLineage.output: After-tax project IRR

dataLineage.flow: Feedstock sustainability certification → lifecycle GHG calculation and net negativity verification
dataLineage.output: Net CO2 removal per MWh

/beccs-project-finance.valueSummary: Enables rigorous BECCS project finance modelling incorporating 45Q incentives, RED II sustainability constraints, and full supply chain carbon accounting to determine project viability and LCONE.

/bioenergy-lcoe-economics.title: Bioenergy LCOE Economics
/bioenergy-lcoe-economics.description: Bioenergy LCOE analysis covering dedicated biomass, co-firing, biogas, and biomethane pathways. Models feedstock cost ($/GJ), conversion efficiency, RED II sustainability certification, and LCOE benchmarking against wind and solar. Data from IRENA.

calculationEngine.formula: LCOE = (CAPEX × CRF + Fixed OPEX) / AEP + Variable OPEX + Feedstock Cost / Efficiency; Feedstock Cost Share = (Feedstock $/GJ / Efficiency) / LCOE
standards: EU RED II Directive — Bioenergy Sustainability
calculationEngine.brief: Levelised cost decomposition separating capital, fixed operating, and feedstock components to identify cost reduction levers

dataPoints.name: Dedicated Biomass LCOE
dataPoints.interpretation: Range $80-160/MWh depending on feedstock cost; competitive vs gas peaker but above solar/wind

dataPoints.name: Feedstock Cost Share
dataPoints.interpretation: Dominant cost driver; pellet prices $200-300/t (2023 volatility); supply chain diversification critical

dataPoints.name: RED II GHG Saving
dataPoints.formula: Life-cycle GHG saving vs 35.7 gCO2e/MJ fossil fuel comparator
dataPoints.interpretation: Minimum 70% required for existing plants post-2021; 80% for new plants post-2026; determines subsidy eligibility

userInteraction: Calculate LCOE for each bioenergy pathway (dedicated, co-firing, biogas, biomethane) using technology-specific parameters
Decompose LCOE into CAPEX, fixed OPEX, variable OPEX, and feedstock cost shares
Apply RED II GHG saving calculation to verify sustainability certification eligibility
Benchmark bioenergy LCOE against wind and solar comparators and identify competitiveness conditions
references: IRENA (2023) Renewable Power Generation Costs Report
EU RED II Directive 2018/2001 — Bioenergy Sustainability Criteria and GHG Calculation
IEA (2023) World Energy Outlook — Bioenergy Chapter
Ember (2023) Global Electricity Review — Bioenergy Cost Trends

acronyms.def: All-in cost of generating one MWh over project lifetime, enabling cross-technology comparison
Total electricity output per year (MWh), determined by capacity and capacity factor
EU directive requiring minimum GHG savings for bioenergy qualifying for renewable energy targets
Bioenergy cogeneration plant producing both electricity and heat, improving overall efficiency

dataLineage.flow: Technology-specific CAPEX, OPEX, capacity factor data by region → LCOE calculation
dataLineage.output: LCOE benchmarking table

dataLineage.source: Feedstock market data (S&P Commodity Insights, Argus)
dataLineage.flow: Pellet, chip, and biogas feedstock prices by region → feedstock cost component
dataLineage.output: LCOE sensitivity to feedstock price

dataLineage.flow: Lifecycle emission factors by feedstock and conversion pathway → sustainability check
dataLineage.output: RED II eligibility determination
