/sustainability-linked-finance.title: Sustainability-Linked Finance Analytics
/sustainability-linked-finance.description: Sustainability-Linked Loan (SLL) and Bond (SLB) analytics covering SPT calibration methodology, KPI universe (carbon intensity, renewable energy %, water use, LTIFR), margin ratchet modelling, and LMA/ICMA Principles compliance.

calculationEngine.formula: SPT Ambition = (Base Year KPI - Target KPI) / Base Year KPI × 100; Ratchet NPV = Σ[(P(Miss) × Step-Up bps × Notional) / (1+r)^t]; Compliance Score = SPTs meeting LMA/ICMA calibration criteria / total SPTs
standards: ICMA KPI Registry for Sustainability-Linked Finance
calculationEngine.brief: SPT ambition scoring and margin ratchet NPV calculation combining KPI materiality, calibration ambition, and probability-weighted coupon adjustment

dataPoints.name: SPT Ambition Score
dataPoints.formula: Assessed against sector decarbonisation pathway pace and historical peer performance
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores below 60 raise greenwashing concerns; ICMA requires SPTs to represent material improvement, not business-as-usual

dataPoints.name: Ratchet NPV (Borrower)
dataPoints.formula: Σ[P(Hit) × Step-Down bps × Notional / (1+r)^t] - Σ[P(Miss) × Step-Up bps × Notional / (1+r)^t]
dataPoints.source: Monte Carlo SPT achievement probability model
dataPoints.interpretation: Positive NPV indicates borrower expects to achieve SPTs; symmetric ratchets (±5 bps) typical per LMA market convention

dataPoints.name: KPI Materiality Score
dataPoints.formula: Relevance of selected KPIs to core business model and sustainability strategy
dataPoints.interpretation: Material KPIs specific to issuer sector; carbon intensity most common (35%); renewable energy share (28%); water use (15%)

userInteraction: Select material KPIs from ICMA KPI Registry matched to issuer sector and sustainability strategy
Calibrate SPTs against historical performance trajectory, sector SBTi pathway, and peer benchmarks
Model margin ratchet NPV under base, optimistic, and stress SPT achievement scenarios
Score LMA/ICMA Principles compliance across five components: KPI selection, SPT calibration, loan characteristics, reporting, verification
references: LMA/APLMA/LSTA (2023) Sustainability-Linked Loan Principles — Updated Guidance
ICMA (2023) Sustainability-Linked Bond Principles and Appendix 1 — KPI Registry
ESMA (2024) Guidelines on SLB Naming and Disclosure

acronyms.def: Loan with margin ratchet tied to borrower achieving pre-agreed KPI-based SPTs; proceeds unrestricted
Bond equivalent to SLL; coupon step-up if SPT missed; proceeds not ring-fenced unlike green bonds
Pre-agreed measurable target for a KPI; triggers margin ratchet if met or missed at observation date
Common social KPI in SLL/SLB: number of injuries causing lost time per million work hours

dataLineage.flow: Universe of sector-appropriate KPIs with calibration guidance → KPI selection and materiality scoring
dataLineage.output: KPI shortlist and materiality score

dataLineage.flow: Historical KPI performance trends → SPT baseline and ambition calibration
dataLineage.output: SPT ambition score

dataLineage.flow: Market convention for ratchet size, KPI mix, verification agent standards → compliance benchmarking
dataLineage.output: Principles compliance score and peer comparison

/sustainability-linked-finance.valueSummary: Provides rigorous SLL/SLB analytics integrating ICMA KPI materiality scoring, SPT ambition calibration against sector pathways, and margin ratchet NPV modelling to prevent greenwashing and optimise SLF structures.

/dme-ml-materiality.title: DME ML Materiality
/dme-ml-materiality.description: NLP-driven double materiality assessment using regulatory filings, news feeds, and stakeholder data to identify financially material and impact-material ESG topics. Applies TF-IDF and BERT sentence embeddings for topic clustering, then maps clusters to ESRS/ISSB topic taxonomy. Outputs a ranked materiality heatmap and audit-ready evidence log.

calculationEngine.formula: score = α·financial_materiality + (1-α)·impact_materiality
calculationEngine.brief: Financial materiality scores are derived from regulatory filing keyword frequency weighted by investor sentiment signals; impact materiality scores aggregate stakeholder survey responses and NGO/media controversy intensity. BERT sentence embeddings cluster semantically similar disclosures to resolve synonym fragmentation. The dual-axis heatmap plots each topic on financial significance (y) vs impact significance (x).

dataPoints.name: Materiality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Topics scoring >60 are considered material for ESRS/ISSB disclosure; topics >80 warrant standalone section treatment.

dataPoints.name: Topic Coverage Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Measures completeness of the materiality scan against all 84 ESRS sub-topics; <70% signals gaps in data inputs.

dataPoints.name: Evidence Confidence
dataPoints.interpretation: Low confidence (<0.4) topics should be reviewed manually before filing; high confidence (>0.8) can be disclosed as assessed.

userInteraction: Upload regulatory filings, news corpus, or connect live feed
Select ESRS or ISSB topic taxonomy for mapping
Run ML pipeline and review materiality heatmap
Export evidence log and materiality assessment table for CSRD filing
references: ISSB S1 – General Requirements for Sustainability-related Financial Disclosures

acronyms.def: ESRS term for the three axes of double materiality assessment
Statistical NLP measure of word relevance within a document corpus
Transformer-based NLP model for contextual sentence embeddings

dataLineage.source: Regulatory filings (10-K, annual report PDFs) → text corpus
dataLineage.flow: TF-IDF vectorisation → BERT clustering → ESRS topic mapping
dataLineage.output: Materiality score per ESRS sub-topic

/dme-ml-materiality.valueSummary: Used by sustainability managers and CSRD reporting officers to systematically identify and evidence material topics, replacing manual stakeholder workshop processes with auditable ML-assisted outputs.

/dme-nlp-engine.title: DME NLP Engine
/dme-nlp-engine.description: Dynamic Materiality Engine NLP pipeline for named entity recognition of ESG topics in corporate disclosures, real-time sentiment scoring, controversy detection, and greenwashing flag scoring. Integrates spaCy NER, FinBERT sentiment classification, and GDELT news feed for media-disclosure divergence analysis.

calculationEngine.formula: greenwashing_score = w1·commit_gap + w2·sentiment_divergence + w3·controversy_intensity
calculationEngine.brief: spaCy NER identifies ESG entity mentions (companies, places, initiatives) and routes them to FinBERT for domain-specific financial sentiment scoring. Sentiment divergence is computed as the signed difference between corporate disclosure tone and media/NGO coverage tone for the same topic. Controversy intensity uses GDELT GoldsteinScale and QuadClass for event severity weighting.

dataPoints.name: Sentiment Divergence Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Positive divergence means company claims more positively than media; >0.3 triggers greenwashing flag review.

dataPoints.name: Greenwashing Flag Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >70 indicate high greenwashing risk requiring disclosure review; used in ESG controversy screening.

dataPoints.name: Controversy Event Count
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher event counts (>5 per quarter) signal media-narrative misalignment with published disclosures.

userInteraction: Connect corporate disclosure corpus (PDFs, XML) and GDELT live feed
Run NER pipeline to extract ESG entity mentions and events
Review sentiment divergence dashboard and greenwashing flag scores
Export controversy report and remediation recommendations
references: FinBERT: Financial Sentiment Analysis with BERT (Araci 2019)

acronyms.def: NLP technique to identify and classify named entities in text into categories such as company names, locations, ESG topics
BERT model fine-tuned on financial news and regulatory filings for domain-specific sentiment analysis

acronyms.full: Global Database of Events, Language, and Tone
acronyms.def: Open real-time database of world events extracted from global news media

dataLineage.source: Corporate sustainability reports + GDELT news stream
dataLineage.flow: spaCy NER → FinBERT sentiment → divergence score → greenwashing flag
dataLineage.output: Per-topic sentiment divergence and greenwashing risk score

/dme-nlp-engine.valueSummary: Used by ESG analysts, compliance teams, and fund managers to automate controversy screening and verify consistency between corporate claims and external media evidence.

/esg-ratings-uplift.title: ESG Ratings Uplift Analytics
/esg-ratings-uplift.description: Analyses gaps between current ESG scores and peer benchmarks across MSCI, Sustainalytics, CDP, and ISS rating systems, quantifies the impact of controversies, and models achievable score uplift through disclosure improvements. Accounts for the known 0.61 average Spearman correlation between major ESG data providers when synthesising cross-provider recommendations.

calculationEngine.formula: uplift = Σ(weight_i × disclosure_gap_i) + Σ(weight_j × controversy_discount_j)
calculationEngine.brief: Score gaps are decomposed into three drivers: disclosure coverage gaps (topics not reported), controversy discounts (active or resolved incidents), and management assessment gaps (policy vs practice divergence). Uplift potential for each driver is weighted by its contribution to the provider-specific scoring model. Provider divergence analysis uses Spearman rank correlation across concurrent ratings to identify systematic differences in scope and weighting.

dataPoints.name: ESG Score Gap vs Peer Median
dataPoints.interpretation: Negative values indicate underperformance vs sector peers; gaps >15 pts signal material rating risk.

dataPoints.name: Achievable Uplift Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Maximum score improvement achievable through disclosure gap closure alone, without operational change; sets realistic target for reporting improvement programmes.

dataPoints.name: Provider Divergence Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher values indicate greater inter-provider disagreement; >0.5 suggests the company sits in contested scoring territory requiring provider-specific investor outreach.

userInteraction: Connect ESG score feeds or manually enter current scores across providers
Select peer benchmark group (GICS sub-industry or custom)
Review score gap decomposition and controversy impact waterfall
Generate uplift roadmap with prioritised disclosure actions by provider
references: Berg, Koelbel, Rigobon (2022) Aggregate Confusion: The Divergence of ESG Ratings

acronyms.def: Three dimensions of non-financial corporate performance assessed by ESG rating agencies
MSCI/S&P industry classification system used for peer group construction in ESG benchmarking
Global ESG disclosure platform providing scores for climate, water, and forest topics

dataLineage.source: MSCI/Sustainalytics/CDP/ISS score feeds → company ESG scores
dataLineage.flow: Peer group median calculation → gap decomposition → uplift weighting
dataLineage.output: Prioritised ESG improvement roadmap with estimated score impact per action

/esg-ratings-uplift.valueSummary: Used by investor relations officers, sustainability managers, and ESG advisory teams to prioritise disclosure investments and set measurable ESG rating improvement targets.

/sector-sustainability-benchmark.title: Sector Sustainability Benchmarking
/sector-sustainability-benchmark.description: Benchmarks portfolio companies against GICS sector peers on a comprehensive set of ESG KPIs including carbon intensity, energy intensity, water use, waste generation, gender diversity, board independence, and supply chain audit coverage. Draws on MSCI ESG, S&P Global CSA, and CDP sector-level benchmarks.

calculationEngine.formula: benchmark_score = (company_kpi − peer_p25) / (peer_p75 − peer_p25) × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Each ESG KPI is normalised within the GICS sub-industry peer group using an interquartile range scaling so that scores reflect relative positioning (0=P25, 100=P75). Environmental KPIs are revenue-normalised (tCO2e/$M revenue, GJ/$M revenue) to remove size effects. Social KPIs use headcount normalisation. Combined benchmark scores weight KPIs by their materiality within each GICS sector based on SASB materiality map.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Intensity Percentile
dataPoints.formula: company tCO2e/M$ revenue vs peer distribution
dataPoints.interpretation: Above 75th percentile (lower intensity) indicates best-in-class climate performance; used in SBTi sector pathways.

dataPoints.name: ESG Composite Benchmark Score
dataPoints.formula: weighted avg of normalised KPI scores across E/S/G pillars
dataPoints.interpretation: Overall relative ESG performance within the sector; <40 signals laggard status, >70 signals sector leader.

dataPoints.name: Supply Chain Audit Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Reflects operational visibility and supply chain ESG risk management maturity; sector benchmark varies 20–80% by industry.

userInteraction: Select portfolio companies or upload company list
Choose GICS sector classification and KPI weighting scheme
Review benchmark heatmap and identify underperforming KPIs
Download sector benchmark report and set KPI improvement targets
references: MSCI ESG Ratings Key Issues by Sector

acronyms.def: S&P Global annual ESG survey used as the primary data source for DJSI index inclusion
Industry-specific sustainability accounting standards adopted into ISSB framework
Quantitative metric used to measure ESG performance against benchmarks

dataLineage.source: MSCI ESG + S&P CSA + CDP sector data → peer KPI distributions
dataLineage.flow: IQR normalisation → SASB materiality weighting → composite score
dataLineage.output: Company vs peer benchmark scorecard with percentile rankings

/sector-sustainability-benchmark.valueSummary: Used by portfolio managers, sustainability analysts, and corporate strategy teams to identify sector-relative ESG strengths and weaknesses and set science-based improvement targets.

/macro-esg-intelligence.title: Macro ESG Intelligence
/macro-esg-intelligence.description: Country-level macro ESG intelligence dashboard integrating ND-GAIN climate vulnerability, World Bank WGI governance indicators, UNDP HDI, policy tightening risk indices, NGFS physical risk GDP drag estimates, and BloombergNEF/IRENA green investment flow data. Supports sovereign ESG integration and country-level climate risk overlay for EM portfolios.

calculationEngine.methodology: Composite Country ESG Score with Climate Overlay
calculationEngine.formula: country_ESG = w_e·env_score + w_s·social_score + w_g·gov_score − climate_risk_discount
standards: NGFS Climate Scenarios GDP Impact Models
calculationEngine.brief: Environmental scores draw on ND-GAIN readiness and vulnerability indices (updated annually). Governance scores use WGI rule of law, control of corruption, and government effectiveness. Climate risk discounts are calibrated from NGFS REMIND/ENGAGE model GDP impact pathways at 1.5°C, 2°C, and 3°C. Policy tightening risk is scored by tracking carbon tax implementation pace vs NDC commitments using UNDP NDC Registry.

dataPoints.name: Composite Country ESG Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher scores indicate stronger macro ESG positioning; sovereign bond spreads show -0.3 correlation with country ESG scores in EM.

dataPoints.name: Physical Risk GDP Drag (2030)
dataPoints.formula: NGFS REMIND model GDP impact at country level
dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated % GDP loss by 2030 under NGFS Hot House World (3°C+); material for sovereign credit risk analysis.

dataPoints.name: Green Investment Flow (USD bn/yr)
dataPoints.interpretation: Annual clean energy investment flow; countries with >$50bn indicate strong transition momentum and policy effectiveness.

userInteraction: Select countries or region for macro ESG analysis
Choose NGFS scenario and time horizon for climate overlay
Review composite scores, policy risk, and green investment flows
Export country ESG scorecard for sovereign bond or EM equity analysis
references: NGFS Phase IV Climate Scenarios GDP Impact Estimates

acronyms.def: Country index measuring vulnerability to climate change and readiness to improve resilience
World Bank composite governance measurement covering 215 countries
Country-level climate commitments submitted to UNFCCC under the Paris Agreement

dataLineage.source: ND-GAIN + WGI + NGFS + BloombergNEF APIs → country-level datasets
dataLineage.flow: Score normalisation → climate discount calibration → composite score
dataLineage.output: Country ESG scorecards with climate risk overlay for sovereign analysis

/macro-esg-intelligence.valueSummary: Used by sovereign bond analysts, EM equity managers, and country risk officers to integrate macro ESG and climate risk factors into sovereign credit assessment and country allocation decisions.

/carbon-arbitrage-portfolio.title: Carbon Arbitrage Portfolio
/carbon-arbitrage-portfolio.description: Constructs and analyses long low-carbon / short high-carbon factor portfolios for carbon alpha generation, calculating carbon beta, net carbon exposure, and carbon carry costs. Uses MSCI Climate Value-at-Risk as a signal for cross-sectional carbon spread opportunities and models the portfolio-level impact of a rising internal carbon price.

calculationEngine.formula: carbon_alpha = Σ(w_i · r_i) where w_i = +1 if carbon_beta < median, -1 if carbon_beta > median
standards: Gorgen et al. (2020) Carbon Risk Factor
calculationEngine.brief: Carbon beta measures a stock's return sensitivity to carbon price shocks, estimated by regressing historical returns on ETS price changes over rolling 3-year windows. The long-short portfolio is dollar-neutral and constructed monthly via portfolio optimisation subject to sector neutrality constraints to isolate the pure carbon factor. Carbon carry cost is modelled as the expected annual cost of holding carbon-intensive positions under a shadow carbon price rising at $10/tCO2/year.

dataPoints.name: Portfolio Carbon Beta
dataPoints.interpretation: Positive carbon beta means portfolio benefits from rising carbon prices; target for low-carbon tilts is beta < 0.

dataPoints.name: Net Carbon Exposure (tCO2e/$M AUM)
dataPoints.interpretation: Lower is better for climate-aligned mandates; MSCI ACWI low-carbon index target is <50 tCO2e/$M revenue.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Alpha (annualised, %)
dataPoints.interpretation: Positive carbon alpha indicates the carbon factor generates excess returns; historical 2015-2024 average ~3-5% annualised.

userInteraction: Define carbon factor signal (carbon beta, WACI, CVaR)
Set long-short construction rules (sector neutrality, turnover constraints)
Review portfolio carbon exposure, beta, and carry cost projections
Backtest carbon alpha generation across carbon price regimes
references: Gorgen, Jacob, Nerlinger (2020) Carbon Risk Factor (HML Carbon)
Andersson, Bolton, Samama (2016) Hedging Climate Risk

acronyms.def: MSCI metric estimating expected equity valuation impact from climate transition and physical risks
Portfolio-level carbon intensity metric (tCO2e/$M revenue) weighted by portfolio allocation
Cap-and-trade carbon pricing mechanism; EU ETS is the world's largest

dataLineage.source: ETS price history + company WACI + MSCI CVaR → factor signals
dataLineage.flow: Carbon beta regression → long-short construction → carry cost model
dataLineage.output: Carbon alpha P&L, net exposure, and factor attribution report

/carbon-arbitrage-portfolio.valueSummary: Used by quant portfolio managers, climate-focused hedge funds, and ESG factor investors to monetise the carbon risk premium and hedge portfolio carbon exposure.

/carbon-institutions-taxonomy.title: Carbon Institutions Taxonomy
/carbon-institutions-taxonomy.description: Registry and methodology taxonomy for the voluntary carbon market, comparing Verra VCS, Gold Standard, ACR, CAR, and BioCarbon Fund across methodology families (AFOLU, energy, industry, waste), credit type taxonomy (avoidance vs removal), and quality label tiers from BeZero, Sylvera, and Calyx Global.

calculationEngine.formula: quality_score = registry_rigour_weight × methodology_permanence × additionality_score
standards: Gold Standard for the Global Goals v2.2
calculationEngine.brief: Each carbon credit is classified along four axes: registry governance quality, methodology family (AFOLU, energy, industrial, waste), credit type (emissions avoidance vs carbon removal), and vintage year. Quality tier ratings from BeZero, Sylvera, and Calyx Global are standardised to a 5-tier scale. Permanence risk is assessed separately for removal projects (forestry, soil) vs avoidance projects, given the materially higher reversal risk in nature-based solutions.

dataPoints.name: Methodology Quality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Score 5 (AA/AAA tier): eligible for high-integrity corporate net-zero claims; Score <3: not recommended for scope 3 residual offsets.

dataPoints.name: Permanence Risk Rating
dataPoints.interpretation: Nature-based solutions have inherently higher permanence risk; technological removals (DACCS, BECCS) score Very Low.

dataPoints.name: Additionality Evidence Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores <50 indicate projects that may have occurred anyway; critical screen for high-integrity offset procurement.

userInteraction: Browse registry taxonomy and filter by methodology family
Compare quality tier ratings across BeZero/Sylvera/Calyx for selected credits
Assess permanence and additionality scores for procurement shortlist
Generate offset procurement policy compliance report
references: Gold Standard for the Global Goals v2.2
Integrity Council for Voluntary Carbon Markets (ICVCM) Core Carbon Principles 2023

acronyms.def: Verra's flagship carbon crediting standard; world's largest voluntary carbon market registry

acronyms.full: Agriculture, Forestry and Other Land Use
acronyms.def: Methodology family for land-based carbon projects; includes REDD+ and reforestation

acronyms.full: Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Markets
acronyms.def: Independent governance body setting Core Carbon Principles for high-integrity VCM credits

dataLineage.source: Verra/GS/ACR/CAR project registries + BeZero/Sylvera ratings APIs
dataLineage.flow: Taxonomy classification → quality tier standardisation → permanence risk scoring
dataLineage.output: VCM credit taxonomy database with multi-rater quality scores

/carbon-institutions-taxonomy.valueSummary: Used by carbon procurement officers, corporate sustainability teams, and impact investors to source, screen, and rank voluntary carbon credits for high-integrity offset and net-zero claims.

/regional-carbon-market-hub.title: Regional Carbon Market Hub
/regional-carbon-market-hub.description: Comparative analytics hub for major compliance carbon markets including EU ETS, UK ETS, California-Quebec, RGGI, Korea ETS, China NETs, Australia ERF, and Singapore carbon tax. Tracks current allowance prices, coverage rates, allocation methodology, MRV regime quality, and linking status for cross-market arbitrage and policy risk analysis.

calculationEngine.formula: price_gap = ETS_price_i − shadow_carbon_price_SCC × coverage_discount_i
standards: ICAP Emissions Trading Worldwide Status Report 2024
calculationEngine.brief: Allowance prices are quoted in local currency and EUR-equivalent for comparability. Coverage discount reflects the proportion of economy-wide emissions subject to the ETS (a 90% coverage ETS has less leakage risk than a 40% coverage scheme). Shadow price gap measures the difference between current allowance price and the IMF/World Bank social cost of carbon ($85/tCO2 in 2030 terms), indicating policy ambition shortfall.

dataPoints.name: Allowance Price (€/tCO2e)
dataPoints.formula: market spot price in EUR equivalent
dataPoints.interpretation: EU ETS ~€60-70 (2024); prices >€80 generally needed to drive fuel switching away from coal in power generation.

dataPoints.name: Market Coverage (% of nat. GHG)
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher coverage reduces carbon leakage risk and improves policy effectiveness; EU ETS covers ~45% of EU GHG emissions.

dataPoints.name: Policy Risk Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Score >70 indicates high regulatory uncertainty; significant for long-dated ETS forward pricing and project carbon revenue assumptions.

userInteraction: Select carbon markets to compare from global registry
Review price, coverage, and MRV quality metrics side-by-side
Analyse linking status and cross-market price convergence potential
Export carbon market risk report for regulatory policy modelling
references: World Bank State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2024

acronyms.def: Cap-and-trade mechanism where total emissions are capped and allowances can be traded between participants
Framework for quantifying and auditing GHG emissions under compliance carbon markets
China's national ETS covering the power sector; largest ETS by covered emissions volume

dataLineage.source: ICAP + World Bank + ICE/EEX price feeds → market parameters
dataLineage.flow: Currency normalisation → coverage calculation → policy risk scoring
dataLineage.output: Cross-market carbon price and policy comparison dashboard

/regional-carbon-market-hub.valueSummary: Used by carbon market traders, climate policy advisors, corporate treasury teams, and project developers to compare compliance carbon market dynamics and assess cross-market arbitrage and policy risk.

/climate-credit-pricing.title: Climate Credit Pricing Analytics
/climate-credit-pricing.description: Comprehensive carbon credit and ETS allowance pricing analytics covering supply/demand balance modelling, policy risk pricing, VCM benchmark prices by methodology and vintage, and corporate internal carbon price (ICP) adoption tracking. Models the gap between current market prices and shadow carbon prices consistent with Paris Agreement pathways.

calculationEngine.methodology: ETS Allowance Price & VCM Benchmark Modelling
calculationEngine.formula: fair_value = f(net_long_position, banking_demand, policy_signal, MAC_curve_intersection)
standards: EU ETS Market Stability Reserve Regulation 2018/842
calculationEngine.brief: ETS allowance fair value is estimated by intersecting the marginal abatement cost (MAC) curve with the capped supply path, adjusted for banking demand (which typically adds €5-15/tCO2 premium in the EU ETS). VCM prices are benchmarked by methodology vintage using Trove Intelligence transaction data, normalised to 2024 USD. Corporate ICP data from CDP shows Fortune 500 average of $50/tCO2 with wide dispersion (P10=$5 to P90=$200).

dataPoints.name: ETS Allowance Fair Value (€/tCO2)
dataPoints.interpretation: Provides anchor for ETS allowance trading decisions; significant deviations from fair value indicate short-term supply/demand imbalances.

dataPoints.name: VCM Price by Methodology ($/tCO2)
dataPoints.formula: transaction-weighted median price by methodology family
dataPoints.interpretation: Nature-based avoidance ~$5-15; technological removal (DACCS) ~$200-600; gap reflects permanence and scalability differentials.

dataPoints.name: Corporate ICP Adoption Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: CDP data shows ~30% of Fortune 500 use ICP for internal decision-making; higher ICP adoption correlates with SBTi target setting.

userInteraction: Select ETS market and review supply/demand balance and price model
Browse VCM price benchmarks by methodology, vintage, and quality tier
Review corporate ICP distribution and shadow price gap analysis
Model portfolio-level carbon cost at current vs future shadow prices

acronyms.def: Carbon price companies use internally to guide investment and operational decisions; can be a shadow price or a fee-and-dividend scheme
Cost of reducing one additional tonne of CO2 equivalent; forms the basis for efficient carbon pricing
Market for carbon credits generated outside compliance schemes, purchased voluntarily by corporations and individuals

dataLineage.source: EEX/ICE ETS forward curves + Trove VCM transactions + CDP ICP survey
dataLineage.flow: MAC curve modelling → fair value estimation → ICP gap analysis
dataLineage.output: Carbon price analytics dashboard for trading, procurement, and strategic planning

/climate-credit-pricing.valueSummary: Used by carbon traders, corporate treasury, climate strategists, and sustainability teams to price carbon risk, benchmark VCM procurement, and set internally consistent carbon pricing signals.

/global-taxonomy-interop-v2.title: Global Taxonomy Interoperability v2
/global-taxonomy-interop-v2.description: Advanced cross-taxonomy interoperability analytics comparing EU Taxonomy, ICMA Green Bond Principles, ASEAN Taxonomy, UK GTF, Singapore-Asia Taxonomy, and China Green Bond Standard across activity definitions, DNSH principles, and transition activity treatment. Quantifies cross-taxonomy alignment scores and identifies mutual recognition pathways.

calculationEngine.formula: alignment_score = Σ(activity_match_i × criteria_similarity_i × DNSH_equivalence_i) / n_activities
standards: ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance v2 (2023)
calculationEngine.brief: Activity-level alignment is assessed by comparing technical screening criteria (TSC) across taxonomies for matched activities. DNSH equivalence scoring checks whether a taxonomy's environmental safeguards achieve equivalent outcomes to EU DNSH criteria, even if the specific conditions differ. Transition activity divergence analysis maps how each taxonomy treats "amber" or "transition" activities with time-limited eligibility, a key point of regulatory fragmentation.

dataPoints.name: Cross-Taxonomy Alignment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: EU-ASEAN alignment ~55%; EU-UK ~85%; low scores indicate significant investor confusion risk in cross-border green finance.

dataPoints.name: Transition Activity Divergence Index
dataPoints.interpretation: High divergence (>0.6) means transition activities acceptable in one taxonomy are ineligible in another; critical for cross-border issuance.

dataPoints.name: DNSH Mutual Recognition Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Proportion of DNSH conditions in taxonomy B that achieve equivalent environmental protection to EU DNSH; used for dual-label bond structuring.

userInteraction: Select two or more taxonomies to compare
Review activity alignment matrix and identify divergent TSC conditions
Analyse DNSH equivalence and transition activity treatment differences
Generate interoperability report for dual-label green bond issuance
references: EU Taxonomy Regulation 2020/852 Climate Delegated Act
ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance Version 2 (2023)

acronyms.def: UK government's domestic green finance taxonomy, aligned with but distinct from EU Taxonomy
ICMA voluntary guidelines for green bond issuance; widely used as minimum standard for green label
Detailed thresholds and conditions under taxonomies that activities must meet to qualify as green or sustainable

dataLineage.flow: Activity matching algorithm → criteria similarity scoring → DNSH equivalence analysis
dataLineage.output: Cross-taxonomy alignment report for green bond structuring and investor disclosure

/global-taxonomy-interop-v2.valueSummary: Used by green bond structurers, multi-jurisdictional issuers, and sustainable finance regulators to navigate taxonomy fragmentation and structure dual-label green instruments for global distribution.

/india-green-infra-finance.title: India Green Infrastructure Finance
/india-green-infra-finance.description: Analytics for India's green infrastructure finance landscape covering the National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP) green component, HAM/BOT project finance structures, IIFCL green bonds, India RE100 PPAs, SEBI Green Bond Framework 2023, SECI/NTPC tender pipeline, and VGF (viability gap funding) mechanisms.

calculationEngine.methodology: India Green Infrastructure Project Finance Modelling
calculationEngine.formula: project_IRR = (PPA_revenue + VGF_grant + carbon_revenue - OPEX) / EPC_cost
standards: Ministry of Finance VGF Scheme Guidelines
calculationEngine.brief: HAM (Hybrid Annuity Model) structures split capital cost between government (40% annuity) and private developer (60% equity + debt); this reduces demand risk vs pure BOT toll models for greenfield infrastructure. RE100 corporate PPA prices are modelled using SECI discovered rates plus wheeling/banking charges. VGF quantum is estimated using the standard MEA formula: VGF = max viable project cost - bankable project cost at 12% project IRR threshold.

dataPoints.name: Project IRR (post-VGF)
dataPoints.interpretation: IRRs >12% are required for private equity participation; VGF is sized to bridge the gap to this threshold.

dataPoints.name: SECI Tender Pipeline (GW)
dataPoints.formula: Announced + under-construction + commissioned capacity by technology
dataPoints.interpretation: Pipeline of 30–50GW annually; important for modelling sovereign green bond issuance size and RE sector credit risk.

dataPoints.name: Green Bond Yield Spread (bps)
dataPoints.interpretation: India green bonds typically trade at 10-30bps greenium in international markets; SEBI framework aims to deepen domestic market.

userInteraction: Select infrastructure sector (solar, wind, EV, water, rail)
Input project parameters (EPC cost, PPA tariff, capacity factor)
Review IRR with and without VGF and carbon revenue stacking
Analyse SECI tender pipeline and green bond issuance capacity
references: Ministry of Finance VGF Scheme Guidelines 2022
MNRE National Solar Mission Phase III Guidelines

acronyms.def: PPP model where government pays 40% as construction annuities and 60% is financed by developer; reduces demand risk
Capital grant to make commercially unviable but socially desirable infrastructure projects bankable
Central PSU acting as procurer and facilitator for utility-scale solar and wind tenders in India

dataLineage.source: SECI/NTPC tender data + SEBI green bond registry + MNRE statistics
dataLineage.flow: Project finance modelling → VGF sizing → IRR calculation → bond structuring
dataLineage.output: India green infrastructure investment analytics for DFIs, PE funds, and green bond issuers

/india-green-infra-finance.valueSummary: Used by infrastructure PE funds, DFIs (DEG, IFC, ADB), sovereign wealth funds, and India-focused green bond issuers to underwrite, structure, and monitor green infrastructure investments in India.

/solar-developer-carbon-finance.title: Solar Developer Carbon Finance
/solar-developer-carbon-finance.description: Carbon finance analytics for solar energy developers, covering CDM/VCS ACM0002 methodology application, avoided emission calculations (tCO2/MWh × grid emission factor), carbon revenue stacking on top of PPA income, India REC and carbon credit bundling, and CCTS (Carbon Credit Trading Scheme) eligibility assessment.

calculationEngine.formula: avoided_tCO2 = AEP × (EF_grid − EF_project) × capacity_factor × hours
calculationEngine.brief: Annual Energy Production (AEP) is estimated from P50 generation forecast. Grid emission factor (EF_grid) uses the combined margin factor (50% operating margin + 50% build margin) per CDM methodology. For India solar, CEA publishes state-wise grid emission factors annually (avg ~0.82 tCO2/MWh in 2022). Carbon revenue is layered on top of PPA income, improving project IRR by typically 1-3 percentage points depending on carbon price assumptions.

dataPoints.name: Avoided Emissions (tCO2e/yr)
dataPoints.source: CEA Grid Emission Factors + project AEP
dataPoints.interpretation: A 100MW solar plant at 25% CF and 0.82 tCO2/MWh avoids ~180,000 tCO2/yr; basis for CDM/VCS credit issuance.

dataPoints.name: Carbon Revenue Uplift (INR/unit)
dataPoints.interpretation: At $5/tCO2 VCM price and 0.82 credits/MWh, carbon revenue adds ~INR 0.35/kWh; material for merchant and lower-tariff projects.

dataPoints.name: CCTS Eligibility Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >70 indicate strong CCTS eligibility; India's CCTS may mandate grid-connected RE projects use domestic registry from 2025+.

userInteraction: Input project specifications (capacity, location, grid zone)
Calculate avoided emissions using CEA grid emission factor
Model carbon revenue at current and projected VCM/CCTS prices
Assess REC + carbon bundling options and CCTS registration pathway
references: CDM ACM0002 Grid-Connected Renewable Electricity Generation v19.0
BEE/CEA CO2 Baseline Database for Indian Power Sector 2022
MoEF Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) Framework 2023

acronyms.def: CDM/VCS methodology for grid-connected renewable electricity generation
Market-based instrument representing 1 MWh of renewable electricity generation in India
India's domestic carbon market framework under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022

dataLineage.source: CEA grid emission factors + project AEP forecast + VCM/REC prices
dataLineage.flow: ACM0002 avoided emission formula → credit volume → revenue model
dataLineage.output: Carbon finance revenue stack model for solar project IRR enhancement

/solar-developer-carbon-finance.valueSummary: Used by solar developers, project finance advisors, and carbon credit brokers to quantify and monetise carbon value in Indian solar projects and structure blended carbon + REC revenue streams.

/solar-manufacturer-carbon-finance.title: Solar Manufacturer Carbon Finance
/solar-manufacturer-carbon-finance.description: Carbon finance analytics for solar module manufacturers covering product lifecycle LCA carbon footprint (20-50 gCO2e/kWh lifetime), EPD certification, CBAM export risk assessment, scope 3 upstream emissions (polysilicon, wafer, glass), and carbon credit eligibility for manufacturing decarbonisation projects.

calculationEngine.methodology: Solar Module LCA Carbon Footprint Analysis
calculationEngine.formula: lifetime_CF = (mfg_emissions + BOS_emissions) / (AEP × system_lifetime_years)
calculationEngine.brief: Manufacturing carbon footprint is driven primarily by upstream polysilicon energy intensity (Chinese grid: ~5.5 kgCO2e/kg Si vs EU grid: ~1.8 kgCO2e/kg Si). EPD (Environmental Product Declaration) certification under EN 15804 provides third-party verified product carbon footprint for procurement specifications. CBAM exposure is calculated on direct embedded emissions in exported modules for EU-destined shipments, applying the EU ETS carbon price at the point of export.

dataPoints.name: Module Carbon Footprint (gCO2e/kWh)
dataPoints.interpretation: Best-in-class monocrystalline modules ~20-25 gCO2e/kWh; Chinese grid-powered production ~35-50 gCO2e/kWh.

dataPoints.name: CBAM Exposure (€/MW exported)
dataPoints.source: EU ETS price + production emission intensity
dataPoints.interpretation: Estimated annual CBAM cost for a 1GW manufacturer exporting to EU at €65/tCO2 and 35 tCO2e/MW module production: ~€2.3M/GW.

dataPoints.name: Scope 3 Upstream Intensity (kgCO2e/module)
dataPoints.interpretation: Polysilicon and glass dominate upstream scope 3; value chain decarbonisation requires supplier energy transition.

userInteraction: Select module technology and production location
Calculate lifecycle carbon footprint from LCA database or EPD
Assess CBAM exposure for EU export scenarios
Identify scope 3 hotspots and carbon reduction roadmap
references: IEA-PVPS Task 12 Methodology Guidelines for LCA of PV Systems (2020)

acronyms.def: Third-party verified document reporting the environmental impact of a product using standardised LCA methodology
EU mechanism imposing carbon cost on imports of carbon-intensive goods, effective from 2026
Methodology for evaluating the total environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction to end-of-life

dataLineage.source: IEA-PVPS LCA database + Exiobase EEIO + EU ETS price + supplier EPDs
dataLineage.flow: Module LCA calculation → CBAM exposure model → scope 3 hotspot analysis
dataLineage.output: Carbon footprint analytics for solar manufacturing decarbonisation and trade compliance

/solar-manufacturer-carbon-finance.valueSummary: Used by solar manufacturers, ESG procurement teams, green bond structurers, and EU customs/trade compliance officers to manage carbon regulatory risk and demonstrate product sustainability credentials.

/ceda-emission-factors.title: CEDA Emission Factors Database
/ceda-emission-factors.description: Comprehensive Environmentally-Extended Input-Output (EEIO) emission factors database covering 400 sectors across 149 countries, providing supply-chain emission intensity values (kgCO2e/USD) for scope 3 category mapping and enabling comparison between EEIO and process-based LCA approaches. Integrates Exiobase, WIOD, and CEDA 2025 v1.

calculationEngine.formula: scope3_emissions = Σ(spend_category_i × EF_i_kgCO2e_USD)
calculationEngine.brief: EEIO emission factors are derived by solving the Leontief input-output model with environmental extensions: EF = f × (I - A)^(-1), where f is the direct emissions coefficient vector and (I-A)^(-1) is the Leontief inverse. Country-sector pairs are matched to ISIC Rev.4 codes. Emission factors are given in kgCO2e per USD of purchases, deflated to a common base year using PPP-adjusted industry deflators.

dataPoints.name: Emission Intensity (kgCO2e/USD)
dataPoints.interpretation: Higher intensities in coal (8-10 kgCO2e/$), steel (2-4), cement (1.5-3); used for spend-based scope 3 category 1 estimates.

dataPoints.name: Scope 3 Category Coverage
dataPoints.formula: GHG Protocol scope 3 categories coverable by EEIO
dataPoints.interpretation: EEIO covers categories 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12 reasonably well; categories 10/13/14/15 require process LCA data.

dataPoints.name: EEIO vs Process LCA Divergence (%)
dataPoints.interpretation: Average divergence ~40-60%; EEIO systematically higher for manufacturing (upstream capture) vs process LCA which may truncate system boundary.

userInteraction: Select industry sector and country for emission factor lookup
Map purchase categories to ISIC codes for spend-based scope 3 calculation
Compare EEIO vs process LCA estimates for key spend categories
Export emission factor dataset for GHG inventory tool integration
references: CEDA 2025 v1 – Carnegie Mellon EIO-LCA Database
Wiedmann & Minx (2008) Definition of Carbon Footprint – Ecological Economics
GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard

acronyms.def: Macroeconomic input-output model augmented with environmental (GHG, energy, water) extensions to estimate supply-chain impacts
Multi-country input-output tables for 43 countries and 56 sectors; key source for international supply-chain emission factors
UN industry classification system used to map sectors across EEIO databases

dataLineage.source: Exiobase + WIOD tables + national emission inventories → EEIO model
dataLineage.flow: Leontief inverse computation → emission intensity per sector/country
dataLineage.output: kgCO2e/USD emission factor database for scope 3 spend-based estimation

/ceda-emission-factors.valueSummary: Used by corporate GHG inventory teams, scope 3 data providers, and consultants to generate spend-based scope 3 estimates where primary supplier data is unavailable, and to validate process-based LCA results.

/big-climate-database.title: Big Climate Database
/big-climate-database.description: Explorer for the Big Climate Database covering 2,700+ food system product lifecycle emission records across agriculture, processing, packaging, transport, and retail stages. Uses EU JRC EF3.0 methodology for environmental footprint calculation, enabling product carbon footprint comparison and supply chain emission hotspot identification.

calculationEngine.methodology: Food System LCA via EF3.0 Methodology
calculationEngine.formula: product_CF = Σ(stage_i_emissions) = agriculture + processing + packaging + transport + retail + FLOSS
calculationEngine.brief: EF3.0 characterisation factors cover 16 impact categories; carbon footprint uses GWP100 values from IPCC AR6 (CH4 fossil: 29.8, CH4 biogenic: 27.9, N2O: 273). Animal products dominate at 14.5 kgCO2e/kg beef, 2.9 kgCO2e/kg poultry, vs 1.4 kgCO2e/kg wheat. FLOSS (food loss and waste, supply chain) adds typically 15-25% to farmgate emissions for perishables.

dataPoints.name: Product Carbon Footprint (kgCO2e/kg)
dataPoints.interpretation: Beef: 14-100 kgCO2e/kg (land use variation); milk: 1.2-3.2 kgCO2e/L; tofu: 1.6-3.5 kgCO2e/kg; wheat: 0.3-1.1 kgCO2e/kg.

dataPoints.name: Land Use Change Emission Share (%)
dataPoints.source: Pendrill et al. (2022) LUC emission database
dataPoints.interpretation: For beef from deforestation-risk regions, LUC can constitute 40-80% of product footprint; key screen for EUDR compliance.

dataPoints.name: Supply Chain Stage Hotspot
dataPoints.interpretation: Agriculture dominates for animal products (>70%) and rice (>85%); packaging dominates for highly processed snack foods (>40%).

userInteraction: Search database by product category or supply chain stage
Compare carbon footprints across product variants and regions of origin
Identify supply chain stage hotspots for targeted reduction
Export product carbon footprint comparison for procurement policy or labelling
references: EU JRC Environmental Footprint EF3.0 Methodology Report (2019)
Poore & Nemecek (2018) Reducing Food's Environmental Impacts Science
FAO GLEAM 2.0 Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model

acronyms.def: EU JRC standardised LCA methodology for product environmental footprints; used for EU Green Claims Directive compliance
FAO model for livestock sector GHG emissions; used for product-level beef, dairy, and poultry footprints
EU regulation requiring supply chain due diligence on seven deforestation-linked commodities from 2025

dataLineage.source: EF3.0 LCA records + GLEAM 2.0 + Pendrill LUC database
dataLineage.flow: Stage emission aggregation → product carbon footprint → hotspot identification
dataLineage.output: Food product carbon footprint database for procurement, labelling, and supply chain decarbonisation

/big-climate-database.valueSummary: Used by food & beverage companies, retailers, sustainable procurement teams, and policy researchers to benchmark product carbon footprints and identify the highest-impact supply chain interventions.

/ai-data-live-platform.title: AI-Driven Live Climate Data Platform
/ai-data-live-platform.description: Real-time climate data ingestion platform integrating satellite imagery, IoT sensor streams, and regulatory filing feeds with AI-powered anomaly detection, automated PCAF DQ tier scoring, data lineage tracking, API endpoint management, and data freshness monitoring for enterprise-grade climate intelligence.

calculationEngine.formula: DQ_tier = f(source_type, verification_level, recency, coverage_completeness)
calculationEngine.brief: PCAF DQ tiers (1=highest, 5=lowest) are automatically assigned based on data source type (audited primary data = tier 1-2, modelled/estimated = tier 4-5), verification status, and temporal recency. Anomaly detection uses isolation forest algorithms on time-series data to flag outlier values >3σ from rolling 12-month mean. Data lineage is tracked using a directed acyclic graph (DAG) model from raw ingestion to derived KPI, enabling full audit trail for regulatory disclosure.

dataPoints.name: Data Freshness Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Score <60 triggers stale data warning; critical for real-time physical risk monitoring and daily portfolio carbon exposure.

dataPoints.name: PCAF DQ Score (portfolio-weighted)
dataPoints.interpretation: Lower scores are better (1=best); PCAF target for financed emissions is weighted avg DQ ≤ 3 for credible disclosure.

dataPoints.name: Anomaly Detection Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Rates >5% suggest systematic data quality issues; each flagged anomaly is routed for human review before inclusion in disclosures.

userInteraction: Configure data ingestion pipelines (satellite, IoT, API, filing)
Review data freshness dashboard and PCAF DQ tier scores
Investigate flagged anomalies and update data quality scores
Monitor API endpoint performance and lineage DAG for key metrics
references: PCAF Data Quality Assessment Framework v2.0 (2022)
ISO 8000-8: Data Quality – Concepts and Measuring
Liu, Ting, Zhou (2008) Isolation Forest – ICDM

acronyms.def: Data structure used to represent lineage flows from raw data sources to derived outputs without circular dependencies
Network of connected sensors and devices providing real-time environmental and operational data
PCAF framework for rating the reliability and specificity of GHG emissions data used in financed emissions reporting

dataLineage.source: Satellite feeds + IoT sensors + regulatory filing APIs → raw ingestion layer
dataLineage.flow: Anomaly detection → DQ tier scoring → lineage DAG construction
dataLineage.output: Clean, tagged, and auditable climate data with quality scores for regulatory disclosure

/ai-data-live-platform.valueSummary: Used by data engineering teams, ESG data managers, and chief sustainability officers to ensure climate data reliability, completeness, and audit-readiness for TCFD, SFDR, and CSRD disclosures.

/data-capture-hub.title: Climate Data Capture Hub
/data-capture-hub.description: Multi-source climate data ingestion hub supporting API connections, PDF OCR extraction, spreadsheet parsing, and web scraping. Provides structured extraction templates for TCFD, CSRD, and SFDR disclosure data, manual entry workflows with validation, and full audit trails for data provenance in sustainability reporting.

calculationEngine.formula: extraction_confidence = OCR_accuracy × template_match_score × field_validation_pass_rate
calculationEngine.brief: PDF OCR uses layout-aware models (LayoutLM/DocTR) to extract tabular data from sustainability reports with field-level confidence scoring. Template matching maps extracted fields to canonical ESG data model entities. Validation rules check data type, range, unit consistency, and temporal continuity. All extraction actions are logged with extractor identity, timestamp, and source document hash for regulatory audit trail.

dataPoints.name: OCR Extraction Accuracy
dataPoints.interpretation: Production systems achieve 85-95% accuracy on structured tables; narrative extraction lower at 60-75%; human review flags <80% confidence fields.

dataPoints.name: Template Coverage Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Measures how completely a submission fills the required data fields for a given standard; <70% triggers incomplete submission warning.

dataPoints.name: Data Provenance Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Score 100 = every data point linked to source document; required for third-party assurance (ISAE 3000/3410 limited assurance standard).

userInteraction: Upload or connect source documents (PDFs, spreadsheets, APIs)
Select extraction template (TCFD, CSRD ESRS, SFDR, GRI)
Review extraction results and validate flagged low-confidence fields
Publish extracted data to reporting module with provenance log
references: EFRAG ESRS Data Collection and Reporting Guidance

acronyms.def: Technology for converting printed or handwritten text in images and PDFs into machine-readable text
Microsoft AI model for understanding visually-rich document layouts including tables and forms for structured extraction
IAASB standard for third-party assurance of non-financial information including sustainability reports

dataLineage.flow: OCR extraction → template matching → validation → provenance logging
dataLineage.output: Structured, validated, and audit-trailed ESG data for regulatory reporting

/data-capture-hub.valueSummary: Used by sustainability reporting teams, external assurance providers, and ESG data managers to automate the tedious data collection process and ensure audit-ready provenance for regulatory filings.

/narrative-intelligence.title: ESG Narrative Intelligence Engine
/narrative-intelligence.description: NLP analysis engine for sustainability reports, earnings calls, and regulatory filings. Scores topic coherence for greenwashing risk detection, identifies commitment-vs-action gaps, assesses net-zero pledge credibility, and checks alignment between controversy events and corporate disclosures.

calculationEngine.methodology: Greenwashing Risk Scoring via NLP Coherence Analysis
calculationEngine.formula: greenwashing_risk = w1·topic_coherence_gap + w2·commitment_action_gap + w3·controversy_disclosure_gap
calculationEngine.brief: Topic coherence scoring uses LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to identify dominant themes in sustainability reports and checks for inconsistency between claimed priorities and allocated word/page real estate. Commitment-action gap detection compares quantitative pledges (net-zero date, SBTi target) against reported annual progress metrics using time-series consistency checking. Net-zero credibility scoring draws on the Oxford Net Zero Assessment criteria (coverage, interim targets, offsets disclosure, third-party verification).

dataPoints.name: Greenwashing Risk Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >70 warrant disclosure review; used in ESG controversy screening and regulatory compliance monitoring.

dataPoints.name: Net-Zero Pledge Credibility Score
dataPoints.formula: Oxford Net Zero criteria: coverage + interim + offsets + verification
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores <40 indicate high pledge credibility risk; correlates with future SBTi validation success probability.

dataPoints.name: Commitment-Action Gap Index
dataPoints.interpretation: Negative values indicate under-delivery vs pledges; <-0.3 triggers regulatory notification risk flag under EU Green Claims Directive.

userInteraction: Upload sustainability reports or connect disclosure API
Run NLP pipeline for topic coherence and greenwashing risk scoring
Review commitment-action gap analysis and net-zero credibility assessment
Export narrative intelligence report for investor engagement or regulatory filing

acronyms.def: Probabilistic topic modelling algorithm that identifies themes in a text corpus
AI field enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language
Corporate net-zero target standard aligned with limiting warming to 1.5°C; over 7,000 companies committed

dataLineage.source: Sustainability reports + earnings call transcripts + news feeds → text corpus
dataLineage.flow: LDA topic modelling → commitment extraction → controversy matching → greenwashing score
dataLineage.output: Greenwashing risk scores and net-zero credibility assessments for investor and regulatory use

/narrative-intelligence.valueSummary: Used by ESG analysts, sustainable finance regulators, and responsible investment teams to automate greenwashing detection and provide evidence-based credibility assessments of corporate sustainability claims.

/quantitative-nlp-research.title: Quantitative NLP Research Tools
/quantitative-nlp-research.description: Research toolkit for constructing ESG text-based quantitative factors from 10-K/annual report corpora, generating sentiment momentum signals, detecting regulatory filing change events, analysing green technology patent intensity, and mapping academic citation networks for climate risk models.

calculationEngine.methodology: Text Factor Construction for Systematic ESG
calculationEngine.formula: text_factor_return = long(high_ESG_sentiment_decile) - short(low_ESG_sentiment_decile)
standards: Loughran & McDonald (2011) Textual Analysis in Finance
Ke, Kelly, Xiu (2019) Predicting Returns with Text Data
NBER Working Paper ESG Text Factors 2023
calculationEngine.brief: ESG text factors are constructed by computing a rolling sentiment score from 10-K filings using the Loughran-McDonald financial dictionary augmented with ESG-specific term lists (green, sustainability, climate, transition). Regulatory filing change detection uses BERT-based semantic similarity to flag material changes between consecutive annual filings. Patent intensity for green technology uses CPC Y02 classification codes to identify Scope 3 innovation capacity signals.

dataPoints.name: ESG Sentiment Alpha (annualised)
dataPoints.interpretation: Academic studies show 2-5% annualised alpha from ESG text momentum; strongest in transition-exposed sectors (energy, materials).

dataPoints.name: Filing Change Materiality Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >60 indicate material filing changes warranting analyst review; strong predictor of subsequent earnings surprises and regulatory actions.

dataPoints.name: Green Patent Intensity Rank
dataPoints.interpretation: Companies in top quartile of green patent intensity show 15-25% lower carbon intensity 5 years forward; leading indicator of transition readiness.

userInteraction: Select filing corpus and ESG text factor specification
Run sentiment extraction and factor signal computation
Backtest factor strategy across sectors and time periods
Export factor scores for systematic portfolio construction
references: Loughran & McDonald (2011) When Is a Liability Not a Liability? JoF
Ke, Kelly, Xiu (2019) Predicting Returns with Text Data – RFS
USPTO CPC Y02 Green Technology Patent Classification Manual

acronyms.def: International patent classification system; Y02 codes cover climate change mitigation technologies

acronyms.full: Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval
acronyms.def: SEC database of US public company filings including 10-K annual reports
AI techniques for analysing and extracting information from text documents at scale

dataLineage.source: SEC EDGAR 10-K corpus + USPTO Y02 patent data + BERT model
dataLineage.flow: Sentiment scoring → factor construction → patent intensity ranking → backtest
dataLineage.output: Quantitative ESG text factors for systematic investment strategies

/quantitative-nlp-research.valueSummary: Used by quant researchers, systematic ESG fund managers, and alternative data analysts to construct investment signals from unstructured sustainability and regulatory text data.

/social-alternative-data.title: Social Alternative Data for ESG
/social-alternative-data.description: Aggregates and analyses social alternative data sources for ESG assessment, including employee satisfaction signals from Glassdoor/LinkedIn, consumer sentiment on sustainability claims, ESG controversy velocity on social media, and community impact proxies such as job postings in low-to-moderate income areas and local news sentiment.

calculationEngine.methodology: Social Signal Aggregation for ESG Scoring
calculationEngine.formula: social_ESG_score = w1·employee_sentiment + w2·consumer_trust + w3·community_proxy − w4·controversy_velocity
standards: SASB Human Capital Standards by Industry
PRI Reporting Framework Indicator SO3 – Community
calculationEngine.brief: Employee sentiment is derived from Glassdoor rating distributions (overall, CEO approval, culture) and LinkedIn follower engagement velocity. Controversy velocity uses Twitter/X and Reddit ESG topic trend analysis with exponential decay weighting (half-life 30 days) to separate acute from chronic controversies. Community impact proxies use Indeed/LinkedIn job posting geolocation against FFIEC LMI census tract maps.

dataPoints.name: Employee Sentiment Score
dataPoints.formula: weighted avg(Glassdoor overall, CEO approval, work-life balance)
dataPoints.interpretation: Score >70 correlates with lower turnover, higher productivity, and better SASB human capital disclosure scores; material for S-pillar ratings.

dataPoints.name: ESG Controversy Velocity
dataPoints.interpretation: Velocity >50 indicates accelerating controversy; predictive of negative ESG rating action within 90 days (accuracy ~65% in backtesting).

dataPoints.name: Community Impact Proxy Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Proxies for community investment quality; used in CRA (Community Reinvestment Act) analytics and impact investing screens.

userInteraction: Configure social data feeds (Glassdoor, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, local news)
Review employee, consumer, and community ESG signal dashboard
Analyse controversy velocity trends and controversy event timeline
Export social ESG scores for integration with primary ESG rating models
references: SASB Materiality Map – Human Capital by Industry
GRI 401-404 Employment and Human Capital Standards
FFIEC Community Reinvestment Act Data and Resources

acronyms.def: Census tract classification used in CRA assessment of bank community reinvestment activity
US law encouraging depository institutions to serve the credit needs of the communities in which they operate
Industry-specific sustainability accounting standards; human capital is a key SASB material topic across most industries

dataLineage.source: Glassdoor/LinkedIn/Twitter APIs + job posting databases + local news → aggregated social signals
dataLineage.flow: Sentiment scoring → controversy velocity → community proxy calculation
dataLineage.output: Social ESG scores for S-pillar analytics and controversy monitoring

/social-alternative-data.valueSummary: Used by ESG data providers, responsible investment teams, and HR/sustainability officers to incorporate real-time social alternative data into holistic ESG assessments and controversy monitoring workflows.

/metrics-data-architecture.title: ESG Metrics Data Architecture
/metrics-data-architecture.description: Platform canonical ESG data model defining the entity-metric-value-source-period architecture for time-series ESG data management. Covers unit harmonisation (MWh to GJ, tCO2e to kgCO2e), provider-agnostic schema, version control for reported vs restated values, and data lineage from raw source to published KPI.

calculationEngine.formula: normalised_value = raw_value × conversion_factor / intensity_denominator
calculationEngine.brief: The canonical model uses a star schema: entity (LEI, ISIN, SEDOL) → metric (GHG Protocol category, ESRS datapoint, ISSB metric) → value (magnitude, unit, decimal precision) → source (provider, methodology, DQ tier) → period (fiscal year, calendar year, quarterly). Unit harmonisation uses SI base units as canonical form with conversion factors from ISO 80000. Restatement versioning tracks original reported value, restatement reason code (boundary change, methodology change, correction), and restatement date.

dataPoints.name: Schema Coverage Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Target >95% coverage; gaps indicate data model gaps requiring new metric definitions or source integration.

dataPoints.name: Unit Harmonisation Accuracy
dataPoints.interpretation: Must be 100% for regulatory disclosure; unit errors are the most common data quality failure in ESG reporting.

dataPoints.name: Restatement Frequency
dataPoints.interpretation: High restatement frequency (>3/year) indicates immature data collection processes; tracked as a data quality governance metric.

userInteraction: Browse canonical metric library and map to reporting standard requirements
Review data model schema and unit conversion rules
Track restatement history and version control for audited metrics
Export canonical ESG dataset for reporting tool integration

acronyms.def: 20-character ISO 17442 identifier for legal entities involved in financial transactions; foundation for ESG entity matching
XML-based standard for machine-readable financial and sustainability data; mandated under ESEF for EU issuers
PCAF tier-based system for rating confidence in GHG emissions data from audited primary (tier 1) to estimated (tier 5)

dataLineage.source: Multiple ESG data providers + company disclosures → raw ingestion
dataLineage.flow: Entity matching → unit harmonisation → version control → canonical schema
dataLineage.output: Provider-agnostic ESG time-series database for regulatory reporting and analytics

/metrics-data-architecture.valueSummary: Used by ESG data engineers, CDOs, and sustainability reporting leads to ensure data consistency, auditability, and provider-agnostic portability across the ESG reporting technology stack.

/reference-data-explorer.title: ESG Reference Data Explorer
/reference-data-explorer.description: Explorer for ESG reference data including legal entity identifiers (LEI, ISIN, CUSIP, SEDOL), GICS sector taxonomy, country/region classification, currency conversion, index constituent history, and corporate action impact on ESG time series. Sources include GLEIF Global LEI Index and ESMA FIRDS security database.

calculationEngine.formula: entity_match_score = Σ(identifier_match_i × weight_i) / total_weight
standards: ESMA Financial Instruments Reference Data System (FIRDS)
calculationEngine.brief: Legal entity matching uses a hierarchical identifier cascade: LEI (preferred) → ISIN → CUSIP/SEDOL → name/country fuzzy match. Entity resolution handles corporate actions (mergers, spin-offs, name changes) by maintaining a temporal entity graph with effective dates. ESG time series are linked to the entity graph so corporate action events automatically trigger data lineage review flags.

dataPoints.name: Entity Match Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Target >90% for institutional portfolios; unmatched entities require manual review and cannot be included in regulatory financed emissions reports.

dataPoints.name: GICS Classification Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: GICS is required for sector-normalised ESG benchmarking; incomplete classification degrades sector peer analysis quality.

dataPoints.name: Corporate Action Impact Events
dataPoints.interpretation: High event counts require ESG time series splicing decisions; key operational risk in ESG data management.

userInteraction: Search legal entity by LEI, ISIN, company name, or GICS sector
Review entity hierarchy and corporate action history
Map entities to ESG data coverage and time series completeness
Export entity reference dataset for portfolio analytics integration

acronyms.def: Non-profit organisation that manages the global LEI system; maintains open, freely available LEI reference data
ESMA database of reference data for financial instruments reported under MiFIR/EMIR

/report-quality-engine.title: Sustainability Report Quality Engine
/report-quality-engine.description: Automated quality assessment engine for sustainability reports scoring TCFD recommendation alignment (11 recommendations, 0-100%), CSRD completeness, GRI Standards coverage, data consistency through year-over-year variance analysis, and third-party assurance level tracking against ISAE 3000/3410 standards.

calculationEngine.formula: quality_score = w_tcfd·TCFD_score + w_csrd·CSRD_score + w_gri·GRI_score + w_consistency·consistency_score
calculationEngine.brief: TCFD scoring maps 11 recommended disclosures across Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, and Metrics & Targets to report sections using NLP, scoring 0 (absent) to 100 (fully addressed). CSRD completeness uses the EFRAG ESRS datapoint checklist against the reported datapoints. GRI coverage counts Standards disclosed vs applicable standards. Year-over-year variance flags metrics that change by >30% without restatement note, a key audit indicator.

dataPoints.name: TCFD Alignment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >70 indicate strong TCFD alignment; regulatory momentum (UK, EU, SEC) is moving toward mandatory full alignment requirements.

dataPoints.name: CSRD Completeness Gap
dataPoints.interpretation: Gap >30% is a material compliance risk for first-year CSRD filers; gap analysis drives data collection prioritisation.

dataPoints.name: Assurance Level
dataPoints.interpretation: CSRD mandates limited assurance from 2025, moving to reasonable assurance by 2028; current Fortune 500 average: 62% limited assurance.

userInteraction: Upload sustainability report (PDF or structured XML/XBRL)
Run quality scoring for TCFD, CSRD, GRI, and data consistency
Review gap analysis and year-over-year variance flags
Generate report quality certificate and improvement action plan
references: EFRAG CSRD Reporting Completeness Assessment Guidance

acronyms.def: IAASB standard for sustainability report assurance; ISAE 3000 for general non-financial, ISAE 3410 specifically for GHG assertions
World's most widely used sustainability reporting standard; universal standards plus topic-specific standards

acronyms.full: Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures
acronyms.def: FSB-established framework for climate risk disclosure; basis for mandatory requirements in UK, EU, New Zealand, Singapore

dataLineage.source: Sustainability report PDFs/XBRL → NLP extraction + datapoint mapping
dataLineage.flow: TCFD/CSRD/GRI scoring → consistency analysis → assurance level tracking
dataLineage.output: Report quality scorecard with gap analysis and improvement recommendations

/report-quality-engine.valueSummary: Used by sustainability reporting managers, external auditors, and ESG investors to assess and compare sustainability report quality, identify material gaps, and track improvement over time.

/sustainability-report-builder.title: Sustainability Report Builder
/sustainability-report-builder.description: Automated sustainability report assembly platform drawing from live platform data to generate TCFD reports, GRI Standards index tables, CSRD ESRS disclosure templates, CDP questionnaire auto-fills, and investor-grade performance narratives. Includes ISSB S1/S2 alignment checker and one-click SFDR pre-contractual disclosure automation.

calculationEngine.methodology: Template-Driven Report Assembly from Canonical Data
calculationEngine.formula: report_completeness = populated_fields / required_fields_per_template × 100
calculationEngine.brief: Report assembly maps canonical ESG metric values to standard-specific disclosure templates using a field-mapping configuration layer. Narrative generation uses structured templates with metric-to-sentence substitution for quantitative sections; qualitative governance and strategy sections use AI-assisted drafting with human review. CDP questionnaire auto-fill uses sector-specific CDP mapping tables to route platform data to ~500 CDP question fields with evidence attachment support.

dataPoints.name: Report Auto-Fill Rate
dataPoints.interpretation: Target >80% auto-fill reduces manual reporting time by 60-70%; remaining fields require qualitative narrative input.

dataPoints.name: ISSB S1/S2 Alignment Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Scores >85 indicate ISSB-compliant disclosure; required for IOSCO-member jurisdictions adopting IFRS S1/S2 from 2025.

dataPoints.name: SFDR Pre-Contractual Completeness
dataPoints.interpretation: Must reach 100% before fund distribution in EU; incomplete templates trigger regulatory distribution restrictions.

userInteraction: Select report standard and time period
Review auto-populated fields and complete qualitative sections
Run ISSB S1/S2 alignment check and CDP mapping review
Publish and export final report in required format (PDF, XBRL, CSV)
references: ISSB S1 General Requirements for Sustainability-related Financial Disclosures (2023)

acronyms.def: IFRS Foundation body that issued S1 and S2 climate and sustainability disclosure standards adopted globally from 2025
Non-profit running the world's largest environmental disclosure platform; over 23,000 companies respond annually
EU regulation requiring sustainability disclosures by financial market participants; Article 6/8/9 product classification

dataLineage.flow: Auto-fill population → narrative generation → alignment checking

/module-navigator.title: Platform Module Navigator
/module-navigator.description: Comprehensive navigation and discovery hub for all 745 platform modules, with full-text search, domain/sprint/status filtering, guided mode previews, recently viewed history, bookmarks, module dependency mapping, and related module recommendations. Acts as the central entry point for platform users discovering analytical capabilities.

calculationEngine.formula: relevance_score = semantic_similarity(query, module_description) × status_weight × domain_weight
calculationEngine.brief: Module discovery uses BM25 retrieval over module titles, descriptions, and tags for keyword search, supplemented by semantic similarity from BERT embeddings for conceptual queries. Dependency mapping tracks which modules consume each other's outputs (e.g., DMA feeds ESRS datapoint navigator; temperature score feeds portfolio analytics) using a directed graph that enables downstream impact analysis when data inputs change.

dataPoints.name: Module Coverage
dataPoints.interpretation: Full platform scope across 93+ domains; filtered by status (live/beta/planned) for production navigation.

dataPoints.name: Search Relevance Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Score >0.7 indicates strong match; used to rank top-10 results in search and recommendations.

dataPoints.name: Module Dependency Depth
dataPoints.interpretation: Deep dependency chains (>3 hops) indicate integration complexity; shown to users before enabling a module workflow.

userInteraction: Search for modules by name, domain, topic, or standard
Browse domain tree and filter by sprint, status, or data type
View module dependency map to understand data flow prerequisites
Bookmark frequently used modules and review recently visited history

acronyms.def: Probabilistic full-text retrieval ranking algorithm; state-of-the-art for keyword-based document search
Data structure used to represent module dependencies without circular relationships
Design discipline focused on the overall experience of users interacting with a product or platform

calculationEngine.brief: Green bond pitch templates follow ICMA GBP four-component structure: use of proceeds, project evaluation/selection process, management of proceeds, and reporting commitment. SLB templates include KPI definitions, SPT (sustainability performance target) calibration rationale, step-up coupon mechanics, and third-party verification reference. Regulatory compliance checker flags claims that may constitute misleading ESG marketing under EU SFDR, UK FCA SDR, or SEC marketing rules.

dataPoints.name: Pitch Completeness Score
dataPoints.interpretation: Full ICMA GBP compliance requires 4 use of proceeds categories documented, 2nd party opinion, and ongoing impact reporting commitment.

dataPoints.name: SPT Ambition Score
dataPoints.interpretation: SPT must represent meaningful improvement vs business-as-usual to meet ICMA SLB Principles; score <50 signals weak SPT credibility.

dataPoints.name: Regulatory Compliance Risk Flags
dataPoints.interpretation: Zero flags required before distribution; each flag identifies a specific claim that requires disclosure, qualification, or removal.
